blocks:
Ayahs

الٓر ۚ تِلْكَ ءَايَـٰتُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ ٱلْحَكِيمِ (١)
Alif, Lam, Ra. These are the signs of the Wise Book. (1)
أَكَانَ لِلنَّاسِ عَجَبًا أَنْ أَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَىٰ رَجُلٍ مِّنْهُمْ أَنْ أَنذِرِ ٱلنَّاسَ وَبَشِّرِ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ أَنَّ لَهُمْ قَدَمَ صِدْقٍ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ ۗ قَالَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرُونَ إِنَّ هَـٰذَا لَسَـٰحِرٌ مُّبِينٌ (٢)
Does it seem odd to these people that We have revealed to a man from among themselves, [declaring], ‘Warn mankind, and give good news to the faithful that they are in good standing with their Lord’? The faithless say, ‘This is indeed a plain magician.’ (2)

Commentary

The Mission of the Prophet

In this Surah, we once again encounter the abbreviated letters (Muqatta'at) of the Quran, presented as Alif, Lam, Ra. We have discussed the interpretation of these types of letters sufficiently at the beginning of Surah Al-Baqarah, Surah Al-Imran, and Surah Al-A'raf. God willing, we will discuss them again in appropriate places in the future and add new insights to them.

Following this, it first points to the greatness of the Quranic verses, saying: "These are the verses of the Wise Book" (تِلْكَ آياتُ الْكِتابِ الْحَكِيمِ‌).

The use of "those" (tilka - a remote demonstrative pronoun) instead of "these" (hadhihi - a near demonstrative pronoun), similar to what appeared at the beginning of Surah Al-Baqarah, is considered one of the subtle expressions of the Quran. It is an allusion to the greatness and loftiness of the Quranic concepts.

This is because commonplace and simple matters are often referred to with a near demonstrative pronoun, but important matters that are at a high level—as if positioned above the heavens on a lofty horizon—are expressed with a remote demonstrative pronoun. Incidentally, we also use this expression in everyday speech; to honor the greatness of individuals, even if they are in our presence, we say "that gentleman" or "that Excellency" rather than "this gentleman," whereas we use the word "this humble servant" (injaneb) when expressing humility.

Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 8, p. 215

Describing the Heavenly Book, i.e., the Quran, as "Wise" (Hakim) indicates that the verses of the Quran possess such firmness, order, and precision that they repel any kind of falsehood, superstition, or vanity. It speaks nothing but the truth and invites to nothing but the path of truth.

The second verse, in connection with the reference made in the previous verse to the Glorious Quran and divine revelation, mentions one of the objections of the polytheists regarding the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. This is the same objection that appears repeatedly in the Glorious Quran, and its repetition shows it was a frequent argument of the polytheists: why was divine revelation sent from Allah to a human being? Why did an angel not undertake this great mission of messengership?! In response to such questions, the Quran says: "Is it a matter of wonderment for the people that We have sent revelation to a man from among them?" (أَ كانَ لِلنَّاسِ عَجَباً أَنْ أَوْحَيْنا إِلى‌ رَجُلٍ مِنْهُمْ‌). In fact, the answer to their objection is given with the word "from among them" (minhum).

That is to say, if the leader and guide is of the same species as his followers, knows their pains, and is aware of their needs, it is not a cause for wonder. The wonder would be if he were of a different species, such that due to lack of awareness of their condition, he could not lead them.

Then, it points to the content of this divine revelation, summarizing it in two things:

First, that We sent revelation to him "to warn the people and frighten them of the consequences of disbelief and sin" (أَنْ أَنْذِرِ النَّاسَ‌).

Second, "to give glad tidings to those who believe that they have a 'footing of sincerity' (qadama sidqin) with their Lord" (وَ بَشِّرِ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا أَنَّ لَهُمْ قَدَمَ صِدْقٍ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ‌).

There is discussion among commentators as to what is meant by "footing of sincerity" (qadama sidqin). Overall, one of the following three interpretations (or all three together) is acceptable:

  1. It refers to the fact that faith has an "innate precedence" (sabiqah fitri). In reality, by expressing faith, the believers have confirmed and emphasized the requirement of their primordial nature (fitrah) (since one of the meanings of qadam is precedence or past record), just as it is said: "So-and-so has a qadam (precedence) in Islam" or "a qadam in war" (meaning: they have a history in Islam or in battle).

  2. It refers to the issue of the Resurrection and the blessings of the Hereafter, because one of the meanings of qadam is rank and status (by analogy that a person enters their dwelling with their feet). This means that for the believers, there is a fixed and certain rank and status before Allah that nothing can change or alter.

  3. Qadam means a leader and guide; meaning that a truthful leader and guide has been sent for the believers.

Numerous narrations mentioned in both Shi'a and Sunni commentaries under this verse, which interpret qadama sidqin as the person of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله or the Wilayah of Ali عليه السلام, support this meaning [1]. As we have said, it is possible that the glad tidings of all these matters were intended by the above expression.

Again, at the end of the verse, it points to one of the accusations that the polytheists repeatedly leveled against the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, saying: "The disbelievers said, 'This man is indeed an evident sorcerer'" (قالَ الْكافِرُونَ إِنَّ هذا لَساحِرٌ مُبِينٌ‌).

The word inna (indeed), the lam of emphasis, and the adjective mubin (evident) are all signs of the emphasis they placed on this slander. The use of hadha (this - a near demonstrative pronoun) was intended to belittle the status of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله.

As for why they attributed sorcery to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, it is clear: they had no convincing answer to his miraculous words, brilliant programs and laws, and other miracles, except to interpret their extraordinary nature as sorcery, and thus cast a veil of ignorance and unawareness over it for the simple-minded.

Such expressions issued by the enemies of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله are themselves clear evidence that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله performed extraordinary acts that attracted minds and hearts. Specifically, relying on the accusation of sorcery regarding the Glorious Quran is itself a living testimony to the extraordinary attraction of this Heavenly Book, which they tried to hide under the veil of sorcery to deceive the people.

God willing, we will speak more about this under the appropriate verses.

[1]: Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 2, p. 177; and Tafsir al-Qurtubi, Vol. 5, p. 3145.

Ayahs

إِنَّ رَبَّكُمُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ فِى سِتَّةِ أَيَّامٍ ثُمَّ ٱسْتَوَىٰ عَلَى ٱلْعَرْشِ ۖ يُدَبِّرُ ٱلْأَمْرَ ۖ مَا مِن شَفِيعٍ إِلَّا مِنۢ بَعْدِ إِذْنِهِۦ ۚ ذَٰلِكُمُ ٱللَّهُ رَبُّكُمْ فَٱعْبُدُوهُ ۚ أَفَلَا تَذَكَّرُونَ (٣)
Indeed your Lord is Allah, who created the heavens and the earth in six days, and then settled on the Throne, directing the command. There is no intercessor, except by His leave. That is Allah, your Lord! So worship Him. Will you not then take admonition? (3)
إِلَيْهِ مَرْجِعُكُمْ جَمِيعًا ۖ وَعْدَ ٱللَّهِ حَقًّا ۚ إِنَّهُۥ يَبْدَؤُا۟ ٱلْخَلْقَ ثُمَّ يُعِيدُهُۥ لِيَجْزِىَ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ بِٱلْقِسْطِ ۚ وَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لَهُمْ شَرَابٌ مِّنْ حَمِيمٍ وَعَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌۢ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَكْفُرُونَ (٤)
To Him will be the return of you all; [that is] Allah’s true promise. Indeed, He originates the creation, then He will bring it back that He may reward with justice those who have faith and do righteous deeds. As for the faithless, they shall have boiling water for drink, and a painful punishment because of what they used to defy. (4)

Commentary

Monotheism and the Hereafter

After referring to the issue of revelation and prophethood in the opening verses of this Surah, the Quran turns toward the two fundamental principles of the teachings of all prophets—namely, "the Origin" (Mabda') and "the Resurrection" (Ma'ad)—and explains these two important principles in the two aforementioned verses through short and eloquent expressions.

First, It says: "Indeed, your Lord is Allah, who created the heavens and the earth in six days" (إِنَّ رَبَّكُمُ اللَّهُ الَّذِي خَلَقَ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضَ فِي سِتَّةِ أَيَّامٍ).

As we have previously pointed out, the word "Yawm" (day) in the Arabic language, "Ruz" in Persian, and its equivalents in other languages, is frequently used to mean a "period" or "aeon." For instance, we say: "There was a day when despotism ruled our country, but now, in the light of the revolution, it is the day of freedom," meaning there was a period of despotism, and after its end, the period of the people's liberation has arrived. [1] Therefore, the concept of the above sentence is that the Lord created the heavens and the earth in six periods. Since we have discussed these six periods previously, we shall refrain from repeating it. [2] Then It adds: "After the creation of the world, Allah took the reins of its affairs in His hand" (ثُمَّ اسْتَوى‌ عَلَى الْعَرْشِ).

"All the affairs of the world are by His command, and everything is within the grasp of His governance" (يُدَبِّرُ الْأَمْرَ).

The word "Arsh" (Throne) sometimes means "ceiling," sometimes something that possesses a ceiling, and sometimes high-based thrones. This is its literal meaning, but its metaphorical meaning is "power" and "authority." Just as we say "so-and-so sat upon the throne," or "the pillars of his throne collapsed," or "they brought him down from the throne"—all of these are metaphors for gaining or losing power, while it is possible that no physical throne was involved at all. For this reason, "اسْتَوى‌ عَلَى الْعَرْشِ" means that Allah took charge of the reins of the world's affairs. [1] "Tadbir" (governance/planning) comes from the root "dubur," which originally means the back or the consequence of something. Therefore, Tadbir means examining the consequences of affairs, weighing the benefits, and acting accordingly. Once it becomes clear that the Creator and Fashioner is "Allah," and the reins of managing the existence of the universe are in His hand, and the governance of all affairs is by His command, it is evident that idols—these lifeless, helpless, and weak beings—can have no role in the destiny of human beings. Thus, in the next sentence, He says: "There is no intercessor except by His permission and command" (ما مِنْ شَفِيعٍ إِلَّا مِنْ بَعْدِ إِذْنِهِ). [2] Yes, "Such is Allah, your Lord; so worship Him [alone] and not others" (ذلِكُمُ اللَّهُ رَبُّكُمْ فَاعْبُدُوهُ).

"Will you not then take heed with this clear evidence?" (أَ فَلا تَذَكَّرُونَ).

In the next verse, as we mentioned, the discourse turns to the Hereafter, expressing in short sentences the principle of this matter, its proof, and its purpose. First, It says: "To Him is the return of you all" (إِلَيْهِ مَرْجِعُكُمْ جَمِيعاً).

Then, emphasizing and stressing this important matter, It adds: "This is the certain promise of Allah" (وَعْدَ اللَّهِ حَقًّا).

Afterward, referring to its proof with this expression, It says: "Indeed, He begins the creation and then repeats it" (إِنَّهُ يَبْدَؤُا الْخَلْقَ ثُمَّ يُعِيدُهُ).

Meaning, those who doubt the Resurrection should look at the beginning of creation; He who created the world in the beginning has the power to restore it. This argument is stated in another form in Surah Al-A'raf, verse 29, within a short sentence: كَما بَدَأَكُمْ تَعُودُونَ—"As He originated you, so shall you return," the explanation of which was given in the commentary of Surah Al-A'raf.

The verses related to the Hereafter in the Quran show that the primary cause of the doubt of the polytheists and opponents was that they doubted the possibility of such a thing. They would ask with wonder: "Can these decayed and pulverized bones once again wear the garment of life and return to their original form?" Therefore, the Quran also places its finger on this issue of "possibility" and says: Do not forget that He who reorganizes the world anew and brings the dead to life is the same Creator who did so in the beginning.

Then, the purpose of the Resurrection is mentioned: this program "is so that Allah may reward those who have believed and done righteous deeds with justice," without the smallest of their actions remaining hidden or without reward from His grace and mercy (لِيَجْزِيَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ عَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ بِالْقِسْطِ).

"And those who followed the path of disbelief and denial, and naturally had no righteous deeds (because the root of good action is good belief), a painful punishment, a drink of boiling water, and a grievous torment await them because of their disbelief" (وَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لَهُمْ شَرابٌ مِنْ حَمِيمٍ وَ عَذابٌ أَلِيمٌ بِما كانُوا يَكْفُرُونَ).

Two points must be noted here

  1. Since Allah has no physical place or location, and especially considering that He is everywhere in this world and closer to us than ourselves, commentators have offered various interpretations for "إِلَيْهِ مَرْجِعُكُمْ جَمِيعاً" (To Him is the return of you all) in the above verse and other verses of the Quran.

Sometimes it is said that it means: "You return toward the reward and judgment of Allah."

Perhaps some ignorant individuals might take this expression as evidence for the physical embodiment of Allah on the Day of Resurrection, but the falsehood of this belief is too obvious to require explanation.

However, what is gathered from a careful study of the Quranic verses is that the world of life is like a caravan that has set out from the world of non-existence and is moving on its infinite path toward the Infinite, which is the Pure Essence of Allah. Although creatures are limited, and the limited will never become infinite, their journey toward perfection never stops. Even after the establishment of the Resurrection, this evolutionary journey continues (as we have explained in the discussion on the Hereafter). [1] The Quran says: يا أَيُّهَا الْإِنْسانُ إِنَّكَ كادِحٌ إِلى‌ رَبِّكَ كَدْحاً—"O man, indeed you are laboring toward your Lord with [great] exertion."

And it also says: يا أَيَّتُهَا النَّفْسُ الْمُطْمَئِنَّةُ ارْجِعِي إِلى‌ رَبِّكِ—"O soul at peace, return to your Lord."

Since the beginning of this movement started from the Creator, and the first spark of life emerged from Him, and this evolutionary movement is also toward Him, the expression "return" (ruju') has been used.

In short, such expressions, in addition to referring to the beginning of the general movement of beings from Allah, also specify the goal and destination of this movement, which is His Pure Essence.

Given that the advancement of the word "Ilayhi" (To Him) indicates exclusivity, it becomes clear that no being except His Pure Essence can be the destination of man's evolutionary journey—neither idols nor any other creature—because all of these are limited, while the path of man is an unlimited one.

  1. "Qist" (justice/equity) literally means giving another their share, and thus the concept of justice is embedded within it. Interestingly, in the above verse, this word is mentioned only regarding those who have righteous deeds and receive a good reward, but it is not mentioned regarding the punishment of the evildoers. This is because punishment and penalty do not take the form of an "income" or "allotment"; in other words, the word Qist is only appropriate for a good reward, not punishment.

[1] & [2]: For further explanation and examples of this from Arabic and Persian, refer to Volume 6, page 200, under verse 54 of Surah Al-A'raf. [1]: For further explanation and awareness of the various meanings of "Arsh," refer to Volume 6, page 204 onwards, and Volume 2, page 200. [2]: Regarding the important issue of "Intercession" (Shafa'at), we have provided sufficient explanation in Volume 1, page 160 onwards, and in Volume 2, page 196 onwards. [1]: For further explanation, refer to the book "The Resurrection and the World After Death."

Ayahs

هُوَ ٱلَّذِى جَعَلَ ٱلشَّمْسَ ضِيَآءً وَٱلْقَمَرَ نُورًا وَقَدَّرَهُۥ مَنَازِلَ لِتَعْلَمُوا۟ عَدَدَ ٱلسِّنِينَ وَٱلْحِسَابَ ۚ مَا خَلَقَ ٱللَّهُ ذَٰلِكَ إِلَّا بِٱلْحَقِّ ۚ يُفَصِّلُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ لِقَوْمٍ يَعْلَمُونَ (٥)
It is He who made the sun a radiance and the moon a light, and ordained its phases that you might know the number of years and the calculation [of time]. Allah did not create all that except with justice. He elaborates the signs for a people who have knowledge. (5)
إِنَّ فِى ٱخْتِلَـٰفِ ٱلَّيْلِ وَٱلنَّهَارِ وَمَا خَلَقَ ٱللَّهُ فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّقَوْمٍ يَتَّقُونَ (٦)
Indeed in the alternation of night and day, and whatever Allah has created in the heavens and the earth, there are surely signs for a people who are Godwary. (6)

Commentary

Glimpses of the Signs of Allah’s Greatness

In the preceding verses, a brief reference was made to the issues of the Origin (Creation) and the Resurrection. However, from these verses onward, these two fundamental principles—which form the most important basis of the prophets' call—are discussed in detail. In other words, the upcoming verses serve as an elaboration (tafsil) of what was previously mentioned in summary (ijmal).

The first verse under discussion points to aspects of the signs of Allah’s greatness in the world of creation, stating: "He is the One Who made the sun a radiance and the moon a light" (هُوَ الَّذِي جَعَلَ الشَّمْسَ ضِياءً وَ الْقَمَرَ نُوراً). The sun, with its world-encompassing light, not only warms and illuminates the habitat of living beings but also plays a major and essential role in the growth of plants and the nurturing of animals. In principle, every movement and motion on the planet Earth—even the movement of winds, the waves of the seas, and the flow of rivers and waterfalls—if we look closely, is a blessing of the sunlight. If one day these life-giving rays were cut off from our earthly sphere, darkness, silence, and death would envelop everything in a short span of time.

The moon, with its beautiful light, is the lamp of our dark nights. It not only guides night-travelers in the deserts but its gentle radiance provides tranquility and vigor for all inhabitants of the earth.

Then, it points to another beneficial effect of the moon's existence, saying:

"Allah ordained for it phases so that you might know the number of years and the reckoning of your life and affairs" (وَ قَدَّرَهُ مَنازِلَ لِتَعْلَمُوا عَدَدَ السِّنِينَ وَ الْحِسابَ).

This means that if you see the moon increasing from the first night—when it is nothing more than a thin crescent—until the middle of the month, and thereafter gradually decreasing until it sinks into the darkness of the "muhaq" (conjunction) in the last day or two of the month, only to appear again as a crescent and traverse those same previous phases, this transformation is not in vain or futile. Rather, it is a very precise and living natural calendar that both the learned and the ignorant can read to keep track of the history of their tasks and life affairs. This is in addition to the light that the moon grants us. [^1] Then it adds that this creation and this rotation of the sun and moon are not casual or for play: "Allah did not create that except in truth" (ما خَلَقَ اللَّهُ ذلِكَ إِلَّا بِالْحَقِ).

At the end of the verse, it emphasizes that "Allah explains His signs and symbols for those who understand and perceive" (يُفَصِّلُ الْآياتِ لِقَوْمٍ يَعْلَمُونَ).

However, how many heedless and sightless people pass by all these signs and symbols of the Lord without perceiving the slightest thing from them?

In the second verse, it addresses another part of the signs and proofs of His existence in the heavens and the earth, saying: "Indeed, in the alternation of the night and the day and what Allah has created in the heavens and the earth are signs for a God-fearing people" (إِنَّ فِي اخْتِلافِ اللَّيْلِ وَ النَّهارِ وَ ما خَلَقَ اللَّهُ فِي السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ لَآياتٍ لِقَوْمٍ يَتَّقُونَ). Not only are the heavens and the earth themselves among the signs of Allah, but every single particle of the beings existing within them is considered a sign and a symbol. However, only those perceive them who, in the light of piety (taqwa) and abstaining from sin, have attained purity of soul and insight,

[^1]: Regarding the fact that the moon is a natural calendar and its various states can be used to precisely determine the days of the month, we have discussed this in Volume 2, page 6.

and are able to see the face of truth and the beauty of the Beloved.

Here, several points must be noted

[The Difference Between "Diya" and "Nur"]

  1. Commentators have much discussion regarding the difference between "diya" (radiance) and "nur" (light). Some consider both to be synonymous and of the same meaning, while others have said that "diya," which is used for the sun's light in the above verse, refers to a strong light, whereas the word "nur," used for the moon, refers to a weaker light.

A third view on this matter is that "diya" means essential (intrinsic) light, while "nur" has a more general concept that includes both essential and accidental (reflected) light. Therefore, the difference in expression in the above verse points to the fact that Allah made the sun a gushing source of light, while the moon's light is acquired and originates from the sun.

This difference seems more correct in light of some other Quranic verses. In Surah Nuh, verse 16, we read: "وَ جَعَلَ الْقَمَرَ فِيهِنَّ نُوراً وَ جَعَلَ الشَّمْسَ سِراجاً" (And made the moon therein a light and made the sun a lamp), and in Surah Al-Furqan, verse 61, we read: "وَ جَعَلَ فِيها سِراجاً وَ قَمَراً مُنِيراً" (And placed therein a lamp and a luminous moon). Given that a "siraj" (lamp) radiates light from itself and is a source and origin of light, and the sun is likened to a lamp in these two verses, it becomes clear that this distinction is very appropriate in the verses under discussion as well.

  1. There is a disagreement among linguists and writers as to whether "diya" is plural or singular. Some, like the author of Al-Qamus, consider it singular. However, others like Zajjaj consider "diya" to be the plural of "daw’." The authors of Tafsir Al-Manar and Tafsir Al-Qurtubi have also accepted this meaning. Specifically, Al-Manar has derived a special benefit from the verse based on this, stating: The mention of "diya" in the plural form in the Quran regarding sunlight points to something that modern science has proven after centuries—that sunlight is composed of seven lights, or in other words, seven colors; the same colors seen in a rainbow and when light passes through glass prisms.

However, the question remains: is not the moon's light—even if weaker—also composed of different colors?

[The Phases of the Moon]

  1. There is also debate among commentators as to whether the pronoun in "qaddarahu manazila" (ordained for it phases) refers only to the "moon" or includes both the moon and the sun. Some believe that although the pronoun is singular, it refers back to both, and such instances are not uncommon in Arabic literature.

This view is chosen because not only the moon but also the sun has "phases" (stations), as it is in a specific zodiac sign at any given time, and this variation of signs is the basis for the solar calendar and months.

However, in fairness, the apparent meaning of the verse shows that this singular pronoun refers only to the "moon" (qamar) which is near it, and this itself contains a point, because:

Firstly, the months officially recognized in Islam and the Quran are lunar months.

Secondly, the moon is a moving sphere that has phases, whereas the sun is positioned in the center of the solar system and does not move within the context of this system. The variation of the zodiac signs and the sun's journey through the twelve constellations—starting from Aries and ending with Pisces—is not due to the sun's movement, but due to the Earth's movement around the sun. This revolution of the Earth causes us to see the sun against one of the twelve constellations each month. Therefore, the sun does not have different phases; rather, only the moon possesses phases. (Reflect on this).

The above verse, in fact, points to one of the scientific issues related to celestial bodies that was hidden from human knowledge at that time: that the moon possesses movement while the sun (in this context) does not.

  1. In the above verses, the alternation of night and day is counted as one of the signs of Allah. This is because if sunlight shone uniformly and continuously upon the Earth, the temperature on Earth would surely rise so high that it would be uninhabitable (like the scorching heat of the moon during its days, which are as long as 15 Earth days). Similarly, if night continued perpetually, everything would wither from the intensity of the cold (like the long nights of the moon). But Allah has placed these two one after the other to prepare and facilitate the bed of life on the planet Earth. [^1] The role of number, reckoning, history, years, and months in the system of human life, social bonds, and business is clear and obvious to everyone.

[Number and Reckoning: The Most Important Issue in Human Life]

  1. The issue of number and reckoning mentioned in the above verses is, in fact, one of the most important issues of human life in all fields.

We know that the importance of a blessing becomes clear when we study and examine life without it. On this basis, think if one day "history" (the distinction of days, months, and years) were removed from human life—for example, if neither the days of the week were clear, nor the days of the month, nor the numbering of months and years. At that point, all commercial, economic, and political matters, and every kind of contract and scheduled program, would fall apart. No task would possess order or discipline; even the state of agriculture, animal husbandry, and manufacturing industries would fall into chaos.

However, since Allah created man for a prosperous life coupled with order, He also placed the means for it at his disposal.

It is true that man can organize his affairs to some extent with a conventional (man-made) history, but if it is not based on a natural standard, it will neither become universal nor be very reliable.

The rotation of the moon and the sun (or more accurately, the Earth around the sun) and the phases they have, establish a natural calendar that is clear and reliable everywhere and for everyone.

Just as the duration of a day and night, which is a small historical unit, is created by a natural factor—namely the Earth's rotation on its axis—months and years must also rely on a natural cycle. Thus, the moon's movement around the Earth forms a larger unit (the month, which is approximately equal to 30 days), and the Earth's movement around the sun forms an even greater unit, namely the year.

We said that the Islamic calendar is based on the lunar calendar and the rotation of the moon. It is true that the sun's rotation through the twelve zodiac signs is also a good means for determining solar months, but although this calendar is natural, it is not useful for everyone. Only astronomers, through astronomical observations, can determine that the sun is in a particular sign. For this reason, others are forced to refer to calendars prepared by those same astronomers.

But the regular rotation of the moon around the Earth provides a clear calendar that even illiterate people and desert-dwellers are able to read its lines and patterns.

To explain: the appearance of the moon each night in the sky is unique, different from the night before and after, such that throughout the month, the state and appearance of the moon in the sky are never identical. If we pay a little attention to the state of the moon each night, we will gradually become accustomed to determining precisely which night of the month it is.

Some might imagine that from the second half of the month onward, the scenes of the first half are exactly repeated—for example, that the face of the moon on the twenty-first night is exactly like the seventh night. But this is a big mistake, because the "missing" part of the moon in the first half is on the upper side, while the missing part in the second half is on the lower side. In other words, the tips of the crescent at the beginning of the month point toward the east, while the tips of the moon at the end of the month point toward the west. Additionally, the moon is seen in the west at the beginning of the month, but toward the end, it is seen more in the east and rises much later.

In this way, the shape of the moon with its gradual changes can be used as a daily counter, and with precision, the days of the month can be derived from the shape of the moon.

In any case, we are indebted to this divine creation for this blessing which we call the "historical system." If it were not for the movements of the moon and the sun (Earth), such chaos and distress would arise in our lives that no reckoning could be imagined for it.

Prisoners who have been trapped in dark solitary cells and have lost track of time have fully felt this wandering and uncertainty.

One of the prisoners who was trapped in a dark solitary cell by the agents of tyranny for about a month in our era recounted: "I had no means to distinguish the time of prayer except that when they brought lunch, I would pray Dhuhr and Asr; when they brought dinner, I would pray Maghrib and Isha; and I usually prayed Fajr along with the bringing of breakfast! To count the days, I kept track of the meal times; I counted every three meals as one day! But I don't know what happened—when I came out of prison, my count differed from the count of the people outside!"

Ayahs

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يَرْجُونَ لِقَآءَنَا وَرَضُوا۟ بِٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا وَٱطْمَأَنُّوا۟ بِهَا وَٱلَّذِينَ هُمْ عَنْ ءَايَـٰتِنَا غَـٰفِلُونَ (٧)
Indeed those who do not expect to encounter Us and who are pleased with the life of this world and satisfied with it, and those who are oblivious of Our signs (7)
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ مَأْوَىٰهُمُ ٱلنَّارُ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَكْسِبُونَ (٨)
—it is they whose refuge shall be the Fire because of what they used to earn. (8)
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ يَهْدِيهِمْ رَبُّهُم بِإِيمَـٰنِهِمْ ۖ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهِمُ ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ فِى جَنَّـٰتِ ٱلنَّعِيمِ (٩)
Indeed those who have faith and do righteous deeds, their Lord guides them by the means of their faith. Streams will run for them in gardens of bliss. (9)
دَعْوَىٰهُمْ فِيهَا سُبْحَـٰنَكَ ٱللَّهُمَّ وَتَحِيَّتُهُمْ فِيهَا سَلَـٰمٌ ۚ وَءَاخِرُ دَعْوَىٰهُمْ أَنِ ٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (١٠)
Their call therein will be, ‘O Allah! Immaculate are You!’ and their greeting therein will be, ‘Peace!’ and their concluding call, ‘All praise belongs to Allah, the Lord of all the worlds.’ (10)

Commentary

The People of Paradise and the People of Hell

At the beginning of this Surah, as previously noted, the Quran first provides a brief discussion regarding the origin (Mabda') and the Resurrection (Ma'ad), and then proceeds to detail them. In the preceding verses, there was an explanation regarding the issue of the Resurrection, and in the verses under discussion, an explanation regarding the Resurrection and the fate of people in the other world is observed.

First, He says: "Indeed, those who do not expect the meeting with Us and are not believers in the Resurrection, and for this reason, are pleased only with the life of this world and feel secure in it..." (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ لا يَرْجُونَ لِقاءَنا وَ رَضُوا بِالْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا وَ اطْمَأَنُّوا بِها).

"And also those who are heedless of Our signs and do not reflect upon them, so as to attain an awakened heart and a soul filled with a sense of responsibility..." (وَ الَّذِينَ هُمْ عَنْ آياتِنا غافِلُونَ‌).

"For both of these groups, their abode is the Fire because of the deeds they used to earn" (أُولئِكَ مَأْواهُمُ النَّارُ بِما كانُوا يَكْسِبُونَ‌). In reality, the direct result of a lack of faith in the Resurrection is that very attachment to this limited life and material positions, and relying and depending upon them; the consequence of this is also pollution in terms of actions and various life activities, and its end will be nothing but the Fire.

Similarly, heedlessness of Divine signs is the source of alienation from God, and alienation from God is the source of a lack of a sense of responsibility and being tainted by injustice, corruption, and sin, and ultimately, its end cannot be anything but the Fire.

Therefore, both of the aforementioned groups—namely those who lack faith in the Origin or faith in the Resurrection—will certainly be polluted in terms of action, and the future of both groups is dark.

These two verses once again emphasize the truth that for the reform of a society and its salvation from the fire of injustice and corruption, strengthening the foundations of faith in "Allah" and the "Resurrection" are two necessary and essential conditions. This is because without faith in Allah, the sense of responsibility is removed from human existence, and without attention to the Resurrection, the fear of punishment will vanish. Thus, these two doctrinal pillars are the foundation of all social reforms.

Then, He refers to the state of another group who are the opposite of these two groups and says: "Indeed, those who have believed and done righteous deeds, their Lord will guide them by virtue of their faith" (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ عَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ يَهْدِيهِمْ رَبُّهُمْ بِإِيمانِهِمْ‌). This light of Divine guidance, which originates from the light of their faith, illuminates the entire horizon of their lives. In the radiance of this light, they attain such insight that the clamor of material schools of thought, satanic whisperings, the glitz and glamour of sin, and gold and power do not steal their thoughts, and they do not step from the right path into deviance.

This is their state in this world, "and in the other world, Allah will grant them palaces in the gardens of bliss, beneath which rivers flow" (تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِهِمُ الْأَنْهارُ فِي جَنَّاتِ النَّعِيمِ‌).

They live in an environment filled with peace, serenity, love for the Lord, and various types of blessings.

Whenever the attraction of the Essence and Attributes of Allah illuminates their existence, "they say: 'Glory be to You, O Allah!'—pure and free from any defect or deficiency" (دَعْواهُمْ فِيها سُبْحانَكَ اللَّهُمَّ), and whenever they meet one another, they speak of peace and serenity, "and their greeting therein will be: 'Peace!'" (وَ تَحِيَّتُهُمْ فِيها سَلامٌ‌).

And finally, whenever they benefit from the various blessings of Allah there, they engage in gratitude and say: "All praise is due to Allah, the Lord of the worlds" (وَ آخِرُ دَعْواهُمْ أَنِ الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ‌).

Here, several points should be noted

[The Meaning of Meeting the Lord]

  1. The "meeting with the Lord" mentioned in the first verse is certainly not a physical meeting. Rather, in addition to meeting the rewards and punishments of the Lord, it refers to a type of inner intuition (shuhud) that man attains regarding the Holy Essence on the Day of Resurrection, as he sees His signs and tokens more manifest everywhere and finds a new vision and perception for recognizing Him. [^1]

  2. In the phrase "their Lord will guide them by virtue of their faith," there is mention of human guidance in the light of faith. This guidance is not exclusive to the life of the next world; rather, in this world too, the believing person, in the light of his faith, is saved from many mistakes, deceptions, and slips born of greed, selfishness, and whims and desires. He also finds his way in the other world toward Paradise in the light of this faith, as the Quran says: "On the Day you see the believing men and believing women, their light proceeding before them and on their right..." [^2]

[^1]: For further explanation, please refer to Volume 1, page 156, under verse 46 of Surah Al-Baqarah. [^2]: Surah Al-Hadid, verse 12.

In a Hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read:

"Indeed, when the believer emerges from his grave, his deeds are personified for him in a beautiful form, saying to him: 'I am your deeds.' It then becomes a light for him and a leader to Paradise." [^1]

  1. In the verses above, it says "rivers flow beneath them" (تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِهِمُ الْأَنْهارُ), whereas in other verses of the Quran, "rivers flow beneath it [the gardens]" (تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِهَا الْأَنْهارُ) is seen. In other words, in other instances, we read that rivers flow beneath the trees of Paradise, but in the verse above, we read that rivers flow beneath the feet of the people of Paradise.

This expression might indicate that even the palaces of the people of Paradise are built over rivers, which grants them extraordinary grace and beauty.

Or it may indicate that the rivers of Paradise are at their command and within their grasp of power, just as we read in the story of Pharaoh that he said: "Does not the kingdom of Egypt belong to me, and these rivers flow beneath me [at my command]?" [^2] The possibility has also been suggested that the word "beneath" (taht) means "in front of them" (bayna aydeehim), meaning rivers of water flow before them.

  1. It is noteworthy that in the last verse under discussion, three states or three great blessings and pleasures of the people of Paradise are mentioned:

The first state is the attention to the Pure Essence of the Lord and the pleasure they derive from this attention, which is incomparable to any other pleasure.

The second state is the pleasure attained through contact with other believers in that environment full of peace and understanding, which, after the pleasure of attending to God, is superior to everything else.

The third state is the pleasure they derive from the various blessings of Paradise, which again turns their attention toward God, and they speak His praise and thanks (reflect upon this).

[^1]: Tafsir Fakhr al-Razi, Part 17, page 40. [^2]: Surah Al-Zukhruf, verse 51.

Ayahs

۞ وَلَوْ يُعَجِّلُ ٱللَّهُ لِلنَّاسِ ٱلشَّرَّ ٱسْتِعْجَالَهُم بِٱلْخَيْرِ لَقُضِىَ إِلَيْهِمْ أَجَلُهُمْ ۖ فَنَذَرُ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يَرْجُونَ لِقَآءَنَا فِى طُغْيَـٰنِهِمْ يَعْمَهُونَ (١١)
Were Allah to hasten ill for mankind with their haste for good, their term would have been over. But We leave those who do not expect to encounter Us bewildered in their rebellion. (11)
وَإِذَا مَسَّ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ ٱلضُّرُّ دَعَانَا لِجَنۢبِهِۦٓ أَوْ قَاعِدًا أَوْ قَآئِمًا فَلَمَّا كَشَفْنَا عَنْهُ ضُرَّهُۥ مَرَّ كَأَن لَّمْ يَدْعُنَآ إِلَىٰ ضُرٍّ مَّسَّهُۥ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ زُيِّنَ لِلْمُسْرِفِينَ مَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ (١٢)
When distress befalls man, he supplicates Us, [lying] on his side, sitting, or standing; but when We remove his distress, he passes on as if he had never supplicated Us concerning the distress that had befallen him. What they have been doing is thus presented as decorous to the transgressors. (12)

Commentary

Self-Grown Humans!

In these verses, the discussion continues regarding the issue of reward and punishment for the evildoers.

In the first verse, it says: "If Allah were to hasten the punishment of the wicked people in this world, just as they are hasty in seeking blessings, goodness, and virtue, their term would have been ended and no trace of them would remain" (وَلَوْ يُعَجِّلُ اللَّهُ لِلنَّاسِ الشَّرَّ اسْتِعْجَالَهُم بِالْخَيْرِ لَقُضِيَ إِلَيْهِمْ أَجَلُهُمْ).

However, since the grace of Allah encompasses all servants—even the evildoers, disbelievers, and polytheists—He does not hasten their punishment, so that perhaps they may wake up, repent, and return from the wrong path to the right way.

Furthermore, if punishment were carried out with such speed, the state of free will, which is the foundation of religious obligation, would practically vanish. The obedience of the obedient would take on a forced nature, for they would see a painful punishment immediately before them in the event of any violation.

Another possibility suggested in the interpretation of the above sentence is that a group of stubborn disbelievers—as the verses of the Quran repeatedly recount—would say to the prophets: "If you are truthful, ask God to destroy or punish us as soon as possible." If Allah had willed to grant this request of theirs, not one of them would remain.

However, the first interpretation seems closer to the context.

At the end of the verse, He says: "Their punishment is enough that We leave those who do not believe in the Resurrection and the meeting with Us to themselves, so they may wander blindly in their transgression," recognizing neither truth from falsehood, nor the path from the pit (فَنَذَرُ الَّذِينَ لَا يَرْجُونَ لِقَاءَنَا فِي طُغْيَانِهِمْ يَعْمَهُونَ).

Then, referring to the existence of the light of Monotheism (Tawhid) in the primordial nature (Fitrah) and the depths of the human soul, He says: "When harm touches man, and his hands are cut off from everywhere, he stretches his hand toward Us and calls upon Us in every state—whether lying on his side, sitting, or standing" (وَإِذَا مَسَّ الْإِنسَانَ الضُّرُّ دَعَانَا لِجَنبِهِ أَوْ قَاعِدًا أَوْ قَائِمًا). Indeed, the characteristic of difficulties and painful events is the removal of veils from the pure nature of man. In the furnace of events, all the dark layers covering this nature are burned away and vanish, and for a time, however brief, the brilliance of this Monotheistic light becomes manifest.

Then He says that these individuals are so lacking in capacity and wisdom that "as soon as We remove their affliction and discomfort, they sink into such heedlessness as if they had never made a request of Us" and as if We had not helped them (فَلَمَّا كَشَفْنَا عَنْهُ ضُرَّهُ مَرَّ كَأَن لَّمْ يَدْعُنَا إِلَىٰ ضُرٍّ مَّسَّهُ). "Thus is what the extravagant used to do made fair-seeming to them" (كَذَٰلِكَ زُيِّنَ لِلْمُسْرِفِينَ مَا كَانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ).

Regarding who makes the deeds of such individuals appear beautiful and adorned to them, we discussed this under verse 122 of Surah Al-An'am in Volume 5 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 428. The summary of the argument is:

The one who adorns is Allah, but in the sense that He has created this property in ugly and polluted deeds: that the more a person becomes stained by them, the more he becomes accustomed to them. Not only does their ugliness and vileness gradually disappear, but they slowly manifest in his eyes as worthy actions! As for why such individuals are identified as "Musrif" (extravagant/transgressor) in the above verse, it is because what extravagance is greater than for a person to waste the most important capital of his existence—namely life, health, youth, and faculties—vainly in the path of corruption, sin, and rebellion, or in the pursuit of the worthless and fleeting goods of this world, gaining nothing in return for this capital?

Is this act not extravagance, and are such people not considered extravagant?

A Point to Consider: "Man" in the Holy Quran

Various expressions are used in the Holy Quran regarding "Man."

In many verses, he is referred to as "Bashar" (human/mortal). In numerous verses, he is called "Insan" (man), and in some verses, he is referred to as "Bani Adam" (Children of Adam). It is remarkable that in many verses where he is referred to as "Insan," blameworthy and censured attributes are mentioned for him.

For example, in the verses under discussion, man is introduced as a forgetful and ungrateful being. Elsewhere, as a weak being (خُلِقَ الْإِنْسَانُ ضَعِيفاً - An-Nisa: 28). Elsewhere, as an oppressive and ungrateful being (إِنَّ الْإِنْسَانَ لَظَلُومٌ كَفَّارٌ - Ibrahim: 34). Elsewhere, man is called stingy (كَانَ الْإِنْسَانُ قَتُوراً - Al-Isra: 100). In another instance, a hasty being (وَكَانَ الْإِنْسَانُ عَجُولًا - Al-Isra: 11). Elsewhere, ungrateful (وَكَانَ الْإِنْسَانُ كَفُوراً - Al-Isra: 67). In another case, a contentious being (كَانَ الْإِنْسَانُ أَكْثَرَ شَيْ‌ءٍ جَدَلًا - Al-Kahf: 54). Elsewhere, oppressive and ignorant (إِنَّهُ كَانَ ظَلُوماً جَهُولًا - Al-Ahzab: 72). Elsewhere, a manifest ingrate (إِنَّ الْإِنْسَانَ لَكَفُورٌ مُبِينٌ - Az-Zukhruf: 19). In another instance, a being of low capacity and moody temperament, who is stingy when in blessing and full of lamentation when in affliction (إِنَّ الْإِنْسَانَ خُلِقَ هَلُوعاً إِذَا مَسَّهُ الشَّرُّ جَزُوعاً وَإِذَا مَسَّهُ الْخَيْرُ مَنُوعاً - Al-Ma'arij: 19-21). Elsewhere, arrogant even toward God (يَا أَيُّهَا الْإِنْسَانُ مَا غَرَّكَ بِرَبِّكَ الْكَرِيمِ - Al-Infitar: 6). In another case, a being who transgresses when in blessing (إِنَّ الْإِنْسَانَ لَيَطْغَىٰ أَن رَّآهُ اسْتَغْنَىٰ - Al-Alaq: 6).

Thus, we see that "Insan" in the Holy Quran is introduced as a being possessing many negative aspects and numerous points of weakness.

Is this the same man whom God created in "the best of molds" and "the best of structures"? (لَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا الْإِنْسَانَ فِي أَحْسَنِ تَقْوِيمٍ - At-Tin: 4). And is this the same man for whom God was the teacher, teaching him that which he knew not? (عَلَّمَ الْإِنْسَانَ مَا لَمْ يَعْلَمْ - Al-Alaq: 5). And is this the same man to whom God taught eloquent speech? (خَلَقَ الْإِنْسَانَ عَلَّمَهُ الْبَيَانَ - Ar-Rahman: 3). And finally, is this the same man whom God set on the path of striving toward his Lord? (يَا أَيُّهَا الْإِنْسَانُ إِنَّكَ كَادِحٌ إِلَىٰ رَبِّكَ كَدْحاً - Al-Inshiqaq: 6).

One must see what kind of humans these are who, despite all that divine dignity and love, display such points of weakness and inadequacy! It appears that these discussions all pertain to humans who have not come under the training of divine leaders, but have instead grown like "self-grown plants" (wild plants). They have had no teacher, no guide, and no awakener; their desires are free and they are immersed in whims.

Naturally, such a human not only fails to benefit from the vast possibilities and great capital of his own existence, but by employing them in deviant and wrong paths, he becomes a dangerous and ultimately powerless and wretched being.

Otherwise, the human who, by utilizing the presence of divine leaders and employing thought and reflection, and by placing himself in the path of evolutionary movement, truth, and justice, steps into the stage of "Humanity" and becomes worthy of the name "Bani Adam," reaches a point where he sees nothing but God. As the Quran says: وَلَقَدْ كَرَّمْنَا بَنِي آدَمَ وَحَمَلْنَاهُمْ فِي الْبَرِّ وَالْبَحْرِ وَرَزَقْنَاهُم مِّنَ الطَّيِّبَاتِ وَفَضَّلْنَاهُمْ عَلَىٰ كَثِيرٍ مِّمَّنْ خَلَقْنَا تَفْضِيلًا: "And We have certainly honored the children of Adam and carried them on the land and sea and provided for them of the good things and preferred them over much of what We have created, with [definite] preference" (Surah Al-Isra, Verse 70).

Ayahs

وَلَقَدْ أَهْلَكْنَا ٱلْقُرُونَ مِن قَبْلِكُمْ لَمَّا ظَلَمُوا۟ ۙ وَجَآءَتْهُمْ رُسُلُهُم بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ وَمَا كَانُوا۟ لِيُؤْمِنُوا۟ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلْمُجْرِمِينَ (١٣)
Certainly We destroyed generations before you when they perpetrated wrongs: their apostles brought them manifest proofs, but they would not have faith. Thus do We requite the guilty lot. (13)
ثُمَّ جَعَلْنَـٰكُمْ خَلَـٰٓئِفَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِمْ لِنَنظُرَ كَيْفَ تَعْمَلُونَ (١٤)
Then We made you successors on the earth after them that We may observe how you will act. (14)

Commentary

Former Oppressors and You

In these verses, reference is also made to the punishments of the oppressive and criminal individuals in this world. By drawing the attention of Muslims to past history, He warns them that if they follow their path, they will meet the same fate.

In the first verse, He says: "We destroyed the generations before you when they committed oppression, and although their messengers came to them with clear proofs and miracles for their guidance, they would never believe" (وَ لَقَدْ أَهْلَكْنَا الْقُرُونَ مِنْ قَبْلِكُمْ لَمَّا ظَلَمُوا وَ جاءَتْهُمْ رُسُلُهُمْ بِالْبَيِّناتِ وَ ما كانُوا لِيُؤْمِنُوا).

And at the end of the verse, He adds: "This program is not exclusive to a specific group; thus do We recompense the criminal people" (كَذلِكَ نَجْزِي الْقَوْمَ الْمُجْرِمِينَ).

In the next verse, He states the matter more explicitly and says:

"Then We made you successors in the land after them so that We may see how you will act" (ثُمَّ جَعَلْناكُمْ خَلائِفَ فِي الْأَرْضِ مِنْ بَعْدِهِمْ لِنَنْظُرَ كَيْفَ تَعْمَلُونَ).

Key Points

1- Qurun (قرون) is the plural of Qarn (قرن), which is usually used to mean a long period of time. However, as lexicographers have stated, it also means a people or a population living in the same era, because its root meaning is "conjunction" and "proximity." In the verse under discussion, it is used in this very sense, meaning groups and "contemporary" nations.

2- In the above verses, the cause of the destruction of past nations is mentioned as Zulm (oppression/injustice). This is because the word Zulm has such a comprehensive meaning that it encompasses every kind of sin and corruption.

3- From the phrase وَ ما كانُوا لِيُؤْمِنُوا ("it was not such that they would believe"), it is understood that Allah only punishes with destruction those groups for whom there is no hope of believing in the future either. In this way, nations that might believe in the future are not subject to such punishments.

For there is a great difference between saying "they did not believe" and saying "it was not such that they would believe." (Reflect on this).

4- The phrase لِنَنْظُرَ كَيْفَ تَعْمَلُونَ ("so that We may see how you act") certainly does not mean looking with the eye, nor does it mean contemplation or looking with the heart, as Allah is free from both. Rather, its concept is a state similar to expectation; meaning, We have left you to your own devices and are waiting to see what you will do.

Ayahs

وَإِذَا تُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْهِمْ ءَايَاتُنَا بَيِّنَـٰتٍ ۙ قَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يَرْجُونَ لِقَآءَنَا ٱئْتِ بِقُرْءَانٍ غَيْرِ هَـٰذَآ أَوْ بَدِّلْهُ ۚ قُلْ مَا يَكُونُ لِىٓ أَنْ أُبَدِّلَهُۥ مِن تِلْقَآئِ نَفْسِىٓ ۖ إِنْ أَتَّبِعُ إِلَّا مَا يُوحَىٰٓ إِلَىَّ ۖ إِنِّىٓ أَخَافُ إِنْ عَصَيْتُ رَبِّى عَذَابَ يَوْمٍ عَظِيمٍ (١٥)
When Our manifest signs are recited to them, those who do not expect to encounter Us say, ‘Bring a Quran other than this, or alter it.’ Say, ‘I may not alter it of my own accord. I follow only what is revealed to me. Indeed should I disobey my Lord, I fear the punishment of a tremendous day.’ (15)
قُل لَّوْ شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ مَا تَلَوْتُهُۥ عَلَيْكُمْ وَلَآ أَدْرَىٰكُم بِهِۦ ۖ فَقَدْ لَبِثْتُ فِيكُمْ عُمُرًا مِّن قَبْلِهِۦٓ ۚ أَفَلَا تَعْقِلُونَ (١٦)
Say, ‘Had Allah [so] wished, I would not have recited it to you, nor would He have made it known to you, for I have dwelled among you for a lifetime before it. Do you not exercise your reason?’ (16)
فَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّنِ ٱفْتَرَىٰ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ كَذِبًا أَوْ كَذَّبَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِهِۦٓ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ لَا يُفْلِحُ ٱلْمُجْرِمُونَ (١٧)
So who is a greater wrongdoer than him who fabricates a lie against Allah, or denies His signs? Indeed the guilty will not be felicitous. (17)

Occasion of Revelation

Some commentators have stated that these verses were revealed concerning several idolaters who came to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and said: "What has been included in this Quran regarding the abandonment of the worship of our great idols—Lat, Uzza, Manat, and Hubal—as well as the criticism directed at them, is intolerable for us. If you want us to follow you, bring another Quran that does not contain these objections! Or at least change such matters in the current Quran!" The above verses were revealed and provided them with an answer.

Commentary

These verses follow the previous ones that spoke about the Origin (Creation) and the Resurrection, continuing the discussion on the same theme and related issues.

First, it points to one of the great mistakes of the idolaters, saying:

"And when Our clear and manifest verses are recited to them, those who do not hope for the meeting with Us say, 'Bring a Quran other than this, or at least change it'" (وَ إِذا تُتْلى‌ عَلَيْهِمْ آياتُنا بَيِّناتٍ قالَ الَّذِينَ لا يَرْجُونَ لِقاءَنَا ائْتِ بِقُرْآنٍ غَيْرِ هذا أَوْ بَدِّلْهُ‌).

These wretched, ignorant people did not want the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) for their guidance; rather, they were inviting him to follow their own superstitions and falsehoods. They wanted a Quran from him that would follow their deviations, not one that would reform their society. Not only did they lack faith in the Resurrection and feel no responsibility for their actions, but their words also showed that they had fundamentally failed to understand the concept of "Prophethood," or were perhaps treating it as a joke.

The Quran explicitly corrects them of this great error and commands the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) to tell them: "It is not for me to change it of my own accord" (قُلْ ما يَكُونُ لِي أَنْ أُبَدِّلَهُ مِنْ تِلْقاءِ نَفْسِي‌) [1]. Then, for emphasis, it adds: "I only follow what is revealed to me" (إِنْ أَتَّبِعُ إِلَّا ما يُوحى‌ إِلَيَ‌).

Not only can I not make any change or substitution in this heavenly revelation, but "Indeed, I fear, if I should disobey my Lord, the punishment of a tremendous Day (the Resurrection)" (إِنِّی أَخافُ إِنْ عَصَیْتُ رَبِّی عَذابَ یَوْمٍ عَظِیمٍ‌).

In the next verse, it addresses the reason for this matter and says: Tell them, I have not the slightest will of my own regarding this Heavenly Book, "and if Allah had willed, I would not have recited it to you, nor would He have made it known to you" (قُلْ لَوْ شاءَ اللَّهُ ما تَلَوْتُهُ عَلَيْكُمْ‌ و لا ادریكم به).

The reason being: "for I have dwelt among you a lifetime before it." You never heard such words from me. If the verses were from my own side, they would surely have flowed from my thoughts onto my tongue during these forty years, and at least some of you would have heard fragments of it (فَقَدْ لَبِثْتُ فِيكُمْ عُمُراً مِنْ قَبْلِهِ‌).

Do you not understand a matter so clear? (أَ فَلا تَعْقِلُونَ‌).

Again, for emphasis, it adds that I know full well that the worst type of injustice and oppression is for someone to forge a lie against Allah: "Who then is more unjust than one who invents a lie against Allah?" (فَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّنِ افْتَرى‌ عَلَى اللَّهِ كَذِباً).

[1]: The word tilqa’ is an infinitive or a verbal noun, meaning "confrontation" or "alignment," and in this verse and similar ones, it means "direction" or "from," i.e., I cannot change it from my own direction or from myself.

Therefore, how could I possibly commit such a great sin?

"Likewise, the act of one who denies His signs is also the greatest injustice" (أَوْ كَذَّبَ بِآياتِهِ‌).

If you are unaware of the magnitude of the sin of denying and rejecting the verses of truth, I am not unaware. In any case, this act of yours is a great crime, and "Indeed, the criminals will never succeed" (إِنَّهُ لا يُفْلِحُ الْمُجْرِمُونَ‌).

Key Points

[The Polytheists Requested to Change the Quran!]

  1. The polytheists asked the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) to either replace the Quran with another book or to change it. The difference between these two is clear: in the first request, their goal was for this book to be completely set aside and another book provided by the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) in its place. In the second request, they wanted at least the verses that opposed their idols to be modified, so they would feel no discomfort from that side!

We see with what a decisive tone the Quran answers them: that neither "replacement" is within the Prophet's authority, nor "change," nor even the timing of the revelation.

Truly, what lowly and immature thoughts they had! They sought a prophet who would follow their superstitions and whims, not a leader, teacher, reformer, and guide!

  1. It is noteworthy that in responding to their two requests, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) contents himself with stating his inability to fulfill the second demand, saying: "I cannot change it of my own accord." By this statement, the first request is also denied a fortiori (by way of priority). For when changing some of the verses is beyond the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) authority, could the replacement of the entirety of this Heavenly Book possibly be at his disposal? This is a form of eloquence in expression where the Quran, in ultimate conciseness and brevity, addresses all issues without a single redundant sentence or word.

  2. It might be said that the evidence mentioned in the above verses for denying the attribution of the Quran to the person of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and affirming that it is certainly from Allah is not convincing. Why must it be that if this book were attributed to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), something similar must have been heard from him previously?

However, the answer to this question becomes clear with a little reflection. According to what psychologists have stated, intellectual genius and the discovery or invention of new and emerging matters in humans usually begins from the age of twenty and reaches its peak by thirty-five to forty. That is, if a person does not initiate a new innovation by this time, it is often not possible afterward. This subject, which is regarded today as a psychological discovery, was certainly not as clear in the past, yet most people, guided by their innate nature (fitrah), are aware that it is habitually impossible for a person to possess a method or school of thought and live among a people for forty years without ever manifesting it. The Quran relies on this very principle: how could the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) have possessed such thoughts until this age and kept them absolutely hidden?

  1. As we pointed out under verse 21 of Surah Al-An'am, in many instances in the Quran, a group is introduced as the "most unjust" of people (using the term azlam). At first, it might seem that these are contradictory, for when one group is introduced as the most unjust, how can another group accept this title?

In response to this question, we have said that all these titles return to a single heading: the issue of polytheism, disbelief, and stubbornness. Forging lies and denying the divine verses in the verses under discussion also stem from this same root (for more explanation, refer to Volume 5, page 183).

Ayah

وَيَعْبُدُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ مَا لَا يَضُرُّهُمْ وَلَا يَنفَعُهُمْ وَيَقُولُونَ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ شُفَعَـٰٓؤُنَا عِندَ ٱللَّهِ ۚ قُلْ أَتُنَبِّـُٔونَ ٱللَّهَ بِمَا لَا يَعْلَمُ فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَلَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ سُبْحَـٰنَهُۥ وَتَعَـٰلَىٰ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ (١٨)
They worship besides Allah that which neither causes them any harm, nor brings them any benefit, and they say, ‘These are our intercessors with Allah.’ Say, ‘Will you inform Allah about something He does not know in the heavens or on the earth?’ Immaculate is He and exalted above [having] any partners that they ascribe [to Him]! (18)

Commentary

Useless Deities

In this verse, the discussion of "Monotheism" (Tawhid) is pursued through the negation of the divinity of idols, and the worthlessness of idols is proven with clear reasoning: "They worship besides Allah those who neither harm them—such that they would worship them out of fear of their harm—nor benefit them—such that they would worship them for the sake of their benefit" (وَيَعْبُدُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ مَا لَا يَضُرُّهُمْ وَلَا يَنْفَعُهُمْ).

It is evident that even if idols were hypothetically the source of benefit or harm, they would still not be worthy of servitude; however, the Quran intends to convey through this expression that idolaters do not have the slightest excuse for this act. They worship beings that are absolutely useless, and this is the worst and most repulsive form of worship.

Then, addressing the vain claim of the idolaters, it says: "They say: These are our intercessors before Allah," meaning they can become a source of benefit or harm through intercession, even if they cannot achieve anything independently (وَيَقُولُونَ هَؤُلَاءِ شُفَعَاؤُنَا عِنْدَ اللَّهِ).

The belief in the intercession of idols was one of the motives for idolatry. As recorded in history, when "Amr ibn Luhay," a leader of the Arabs, traveled to the region of Sham (Greater Syria) to use its mineral waters and seek treatment for himself, the practices of the idolaters there caught his attention. When he asked them the reason for this worship, they told him that these idols are the cause of rainfall, the resolution of problems, and intercession before God. Being a superstitious man, he was influenced and requested that they provide him with some idols to bring back to the Hijaz; in this way, idolatry spread among the people of the Hijaz.

In response to this delusion, the Quran says: "Say, 'Do you inform Allah of something He knows not in the heavens or on the earth?'" (قُلْ أَتُنَبِّئُونَ اللَّهَ بِمَا لَا يَعْلَمُ فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ وَلَا فِي الْأَرْضِ).

This is an allusion to the fact that if Allah had such intercessors, He would be aware of their existence in any part of the heavens or the earth, for the vastness of Allah's knowledge is such that there is not the smallest atom in the heavens or the earth except that He is aware of it.

In other words, this is exactly like telling someone, "You have such-and-such a representative," and he replies, "I have no knowledge of the existence of such a representative." This is the best evidence to negate his existence, for it is impossible for someone to be unaware of the existence of his own representative.

At the end of the verse, for emphasis, it states: "Glorified and Exalted is He above the partners they associate with Him" (سُبْحَانَهُ وَتَعَالَى عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ).

(Intercession has been discussed in detail in Volume 1, page 163, and Volume 2, page 196).

Ayah

وَمَا كَانَ ٱلنَّاسُ إِلَّآ أُمَّةً وَٰحِدَةً فَٱخْتَلَفُوا۟ ۚ وَلَوْلَا كَلِمَةٌ سَبَقَتْ مِن رَّبِّكَ لَقُضِىَ بَيْنَهُمْ فِيمَا فِيهِ يَخْتَلِفُونَ (١٩)
Mankind were but a single [religious] community; then they differed. And were it not for a prior decree of your Lord, decision would have been made between them concerning that about which they differ. (19)

Commentary

In connection with the discussion in the previous verse regarding the negation of polytheism and idol-worship, this verse refers to the monotheistic nature (fitrah) of all human beings and states: "In the beginning, all of mankind were one single community" and there was no creed among them other than monotheism (وَ ما كانَ النَّاسُ إِلَّا أُمَّةً واحِدَةً).

This monotheistic nature, which was initially intact, underwent changes over time due to narrow-mindedness and satanic inclinations; a group deviated from the path of monotheism and turned toward polytheism, and naturally, human society was divided into two different groups: a group of monotheists and a group of polytheists (فَاخْتَلَفُوا).

Therefore, polytheism is, in reality, a type of innovation (bid'ah) and a deviation from nature—a deviation that originates from a handful of baseless illusions and fantasies.

At this point, a question might arise: Why does Allah not eliminate this disagreement through the swift punishment of the polytheists, so that the entire human society may once again become monotheistic?

The Quran immediately adds in response to this question: "Had it not been for a prior Word from your Lord regarding the freedom of man in the path of guidance—which is the secret to his perfection and progress—Allah would have quickly judged between them concerning that wherein they differed," and He would have punished the polytheists and the deviants (وَ لَوْ لا كَلِمَةٌ سَبَقَتْ مِنْ رَبِّكَ لَقُضِيَ بَيْنَهُمْ فِيما فِيهِ يَخْتَلِفُونَ‌).

Consequently, the "Word" (kalimah) in the above verse refers to the Sunnah (tradition) and the decree of creation regarding the freedom of human beings, which has been the case from the beginning. For if the deviants and polytheists were punished immediately, the faith of the monotheists would take on an almost forced and compulsory nature, and would certainly be out of fear and terror. Such faith is neither a source of honor nor an evidence of perfection. Allah has reserved this judgment and punishment mostly for the Hereafter, so that the righteous and the pure may choose their path freely.

Ayah

وَيَقُولُونَ لَوْلَآ أُنزِلَ عَلَيْهِ ءَايَةٌ مِّن رَّبِّهِۦ ۖ فَقُلْ إِنَّمَا ٱلْغَيْبُ لِلَّهِ فَٱنتَظِرُوٓا۟ إِنِّى مَعَكُم مِّنَ ٱلْمُنتَظِرِينَ (٢٠)
They say, ‘Why has not some sign been sent down to him from his Lord?’ Say, ‘[The knowledge of] the Unseen belongs only to Allah. So wait. I too am waiting along with you.’ (20)

Commentary

Arbitrary Miracles

Once again, the Quran addresses the excuses of the polytheists when they refuse to accept faith and Islam, stating: "The polytheists say, 'Why has a miracle not been sent down to him from his Lord?'" (وَ يَقُولُونَ لَوْ لا أُنْزِلَ عَلَيْهِ آيَةٌ مِنْ رَبِّهِ‌).

Of course, based on evidence we will mention later, they did not mean just any miracle, for the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله certainly possessed other miracles besides the Quran, and Islamic history as well as several Quranic verses bear witness to this fact.

Rather, their intention was that whenever they proposed a miracle according to their own whims, he should perform it immediately! They imagined that performing miracles was a matter within the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله own control, to be performed whenever and however he willed. Furthermore, they believed he was obligated to use this power before every stubborn claimant and excuse-seeker, acting according to their desires.

Therefore, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله is immediately commanded: "Tell them: Miracles belong only to Allah and pertain to the world of the Unseen and the supernatural" (فَقُلْ إِنَّمَا الْغَيْبُ لِلَّهِ‌).

Consequently, it is not something at my disposal for me to perform a new miracle every day according to your whims, only for you to later refrain from believing by offering some excuse.

At the end of the verse, He addresses them with a threatening tone: "Now that you will not cease your stubbornness, then wait; I too am waiting with you" (فَانْتَظِرُوا إِنِّي مَعَكُمْ مِنَ الْمُنْتَظِرِينَ‌).

You wait for divine punishment, and I wait for my victory! Or, you wait for the arrival of such miracles, and I wait for the punishment of you stubborn individuals!

Two points must be noted here

[This verse is not evidence for the denial of miracles]

1- As we mentioned above, although the word "Ayah" (miracle) is absolute and includes any kind of miracle, there are clues indicating that they were not seeking a miracle to recognize the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله; rather, they were seeking "arbitrary miracles" (Mu'jizat al-Iqtirahi). That is, whatever miracle occurred to them each day, they would propose it to the Prophet and expect him to submit to their proposal—as if the Prophet were an idle man holding the keys to all miracles, waiting for someone to come along and make a suggestion! They were oblivious to the fact that, firstly, a miracle is the work of Allah and is performed only by His command. Secondly, a miracle is for the purpose of recognizing the Prophet and for guidance, and a single instance is sufficient for this purpose. The Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله had already shown them sufficient miracles; anything beyond that was nothing but a collection of whimsical demands.

The evidence that "Ayah" in the above sentence refers to arbitrary miracles is as follows:

Firstly—The end of the verse threatens them; if they were seeking a miracle to discover the truth, there would be no place for a threat.

Secondly—A few verses earlier, we read that they were so stubborn that they suggested the Prophet change his Heavenly Book to another book, or at least change the verses that refer to the negation of idolatry!

Thirdly—According to the established principle in the interpretation of verses that "The Quran explains itself" (القرآن يفسر بعضه بعضا), we can clearly understand from verses such as 90-94 of Surah Al-Isra that these stubborn idolaters were not seeking miracles for guidance. Thus, one would say: "We will not believe in you until you cause springs to gush forth from this parched earth." Another would say: "This is not enough; you must have a palace of gold." Another would say: "This is not convincing for us; you must fly into the sky before our eyes." Then he would add: "Flying into the sky is also not enough unless you bring down a letter from Allah for us!"—and other such nonsense and absurdities.

From what has been said above, it becomes clear that those who have tried to use this verse as evidence to deny all miracles, or to deny miracles other than the Quran, are in error. (God willing, you will read more details on this subject under verse 59 of Surah Al-Isra).

2- The word "Ghayb" (Unseen) in the phrase "إِنَّمَا الْغَيْبُ لِلَّهِ‌" may refer to the fact that a miracle is a matter related to the world of the Unseen, is not in my control, and belongs exclusively to Allah.

Or, it may indicate that the underlying wisdom of affairs—and in which case wisdom necessitates the sending of a miracle—is among the secrets of the Unseen and belongs exclusively to Allah. He sends a miracle whenever He deems it appropriate and sees the seeker of the miracle as a true seeker of truth, for the Unseen and hidden secrets belong to His Pure Essence alone.

However, the first interpretation seems closer to the intended meaning.

Ayahs

وَإِذَآ أَذَقْنَا ٱلنَّاسَ رَحْمَةً مِّنۢ بَعْدِ ضَرَّآءَ مَسَّتْهُمْ إِذَا لَهُم مَّكْرٌ فِىٓ ءَايَاتِنَا ۚ قُلِ ٱللَّهُ أَسْرَعُ مَكْرًا ۚ إِنَّ رُسُلَنَا يَكْتُبُونَ مَا تَمْكُرُونَ (٢١)
When We let people taste [Our] mercy after a distress that has befallen them, behold, they scheme against Our signs! Say, ‘Allah is more swift at devising.’ Indeed Our messengers write down what you scheme. (21)
هُوَ ٱلَّذِى يُسَيِّرُكُمْ فِى ٱلْبَرِّ وَٱلْبَحْرِ ۖ حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا كُنتُمْ فِى ٱلْفُلْكِ وَجَرَيْنَ بِهِم بِرِيحٍ طَيِّبَةٍ وَفَرِحُوا۟ بِهَا جَآءَتْهَا رِيحٌ عَاصِفٌ وَجَآءَهُمُ ٱلْمَوْجُ مِن كُلِّ مَكَانٍ وَظَنُّوٓا۟ أَنَّهُمْ أُحِيطَ بِهِمْ ۙ دَعَوُا۟ ٱللَّهَ مُخْلِصِينَ لَهُ ٱلدِّينَ لَئِنْ أَنجَيْتَنَا مِنْ هَـٰذِهِۦ لَنَكُونَنَّ مِنَ ٱلشَّـٰكِرِينَ (٢٢)
It is He who carries you across land and sea. When you are in the ships, and they sail along with them with a favourable wind, exulting in it, there comes upon them a tempestuous wind and waves assail them from every side, and they think that they are besieged, they invoke Allah putting exclusive faith in Him, ‘If You deliver us from this, we will surely be among the grateful.’ (22)
فَلَمَّآ أَنجَىٰهُمْ إِذَا هُمْ يَبْغُونَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ بِغَيْرِ ٱلْحَقِّ ۗ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ إِنَّمَا بَغْيُكُمْ عَلَىٰٓ أَنفُسِكُم ۖ مَّتَـٰعَ ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا ۖ ثُمَّ إِلَيْنَا مَرْجِعُكُمْ فَنُنَبِّئُكُم بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ (٢٣)
But when He delivers them, behold, they commit violations on the earth unduly! O mankind! Your violations are only to your own detriment. [These are] the wares of the life of this world; then to Us will be your return, whereat We will inform you concerning what you used to do. (23)

Commentary

[The Beliefs and Actions of the Polytheists]

In these verses, the discourse once again turns to the beliefs and actions of the polytheists, followed by an invitation to monotheism (Tawhid) and the negation of any form of shirk (associating partners with Allah).

In the first verse, referring to one of the ignorant schemes of the polytheists, it says: "When We cause people to taste mercy after some adversity has touched them—intended to awaken and make them aware—and then We remove it, granting them the taste of Our peace and mercy, instead of turning toward Us, they begin to mock and ridicule these signs and verses, or they resort to false justifications to deny them." For instance, they might attribute calamities and hardships to the wrath of idols, and blessings and peace to the idols' compassion and affection, or they might simply dismiss everything as a series of coincidences (وَ إِذا أَذَقْنَا النَّاسَ رَحْمَةً مِنْ بَعْدِ ضَرَّاءَ مَسَّتْهُمْ إِذا لَهُمْ مَكْرٌ فِي آياتِنا).

The word "makr" (مكر) in the above verse, which means any kind of devising or planning, refers to the improper justifications and evasive maneuvers the polytheists devised in the face of the Lord's signs and the manifestation of calamities and blessings.

However, Allah warns them through His Prophet: "Say to them: Allah is swifter in planning and swifter in executing crushing designs" (قُلِ اللَّهُ أَسْرَعُ مَكْراً).

As we have repeatedly noted, "makr" originally means any kind of planning or devising accompanied by secrecy; it does not carry the same connotation it has in modern Persian, which implies a type of malicious mischief. Therefore, it can be applied to both Allah and His servants. [^1]

[^1]: For further explanation, please refer to Volume 2, page 429; Volume 6, page 271; and Volume 7, page 148.

As for what constitutes this "Divine Makr" in the verse under discussion, it apparently refers to the punishments of the Lord, which are sometimes carried out in total secrecy, without any prior notice, and with the utmost speed. Sometimes, He even punishes the criminals by their own hands. It is obvious that He who is the most Powerful and most capable of removing obstacles and preparing means will also have the swiftest plans.

In other words, whenever He wills to punish or discipline someone, it is realized immediately, whereas others are not so.

Then He threatens them, warning that they should not imagine these conspiracies and schemes will be forgotten: "Our messengers—meaning the angels who record deeds—write down all the schemes you devise to extinguish the light of truth" (إِنَّ رُسُلَنا يَكْتُبُونَ ما تَمْكُرُونَ‌).

Thus, you must prepare yourselves to be held accountable and punished in the next abode. Regarding the recording of deeds and the angels commissioned for it, we will discuss this under the appropriate verses.

In the following verse, the discourse reaches into the depths of human nature (fitrah) and explains innate monotheism—how man, in the face of great difficulties and at the moment of danger, forgets everything except Allah. Yet, as soon as the calamity is removed, the fire is extinguished, and the problem is solved, he once again resorts to transgression and becomes a stranger to God.

First, it says: "He is the One who enables you to travel on land and sea" (هُوَ الَّذِي يُسَيِّرُكُمْ فِي الْبَرِّ وَ الْبَحْرِ).

"Until, when you are in ships and they sail with them with a favorable wind, and they rejoice therein..." (حَتَّى إِذا كُنْتُمْ فِي الْفُلْكِ وَ جَرَيْنَ بِهِمْ بِرِيحٍ طَيِّبَةٍ وَ فَرِحُوا بِها).

"But suddenly, a fierce and crushing storm wind blows, and waves come upon them from every side, such that they see death with their own eyes and lose all hope of life" (جاءَتْها رِيحٌ عاصِفٌ وَ جاءَهُمُ الْمَوْجُ مِنْ كُلِّ مَكانٍ وَ ظَنُّوا أَنَّهُمْ أُحِيطَ بِهِمْ‌).

Exactly at such a moment, they remember Allah "and they call upon Him, sincere to Him in religion, purifying their faith from any kind of shirk or idolatry!" (دَعَوُا اللَّهَ مُخْلِصِينَ لَهُ الدِّينَ‌).

At this time, they raise their hands in prayer and say: "O Allah! If You deliver us from this peril, we will surely be among the grateful" (لَئِنْ أَنْجَيْتَنا مِنْ هذِهِ لَنَكُونَنَّ مِنَ الشَّاكِرِينَ‌). We shall neither commit injustice nor turn to anyone but You.

"But when He delivers them and they reach the shore of safety, they begin to commit injustice and rebellion on earth without right" (فَلَمَّا أَنْجاهُمْ إِذا هُمْ يَبْغُونَ فِي الْأَرْضِ بِغَيْرِ الْحَقِ‌).

But "O people, know that whatever injustice or rebellion you commit, and whatever deviation from the truth you find, its harm is only against yourselves" (يا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ إِنَّما بَغْيُكُمْ عَلى‌ أَنْفُسِكُمْ‌).

The most you can do is "enjoy for a few days the fleeting goods of the life of this world" (مَتاعَ الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا) [^1] "then to Us is your return" (ثُمَّ إِلَيْنا مَرْجِعُكُمْ‌).

"Then We will inform you of what you used to do" (فَنُنَبِّئُكُمْ بِما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ‌).

[^1]: The word "Mata'" (متاع) is the object of an implied verb, originally: "You enjoy the goods of the life of this world."

Key Points

  1. What we read in the above verses is not exclusive to idolaters; rather, it is a general principle regarding all those who are tainted, worldly, narrow-minded, and forgetful. When waves of calamity surround them, when their hands are cut off from all help, when the knife reaches the bone, and they see no helper for themselves, they reach out to the threshold of Allah. They make a thousand vows and promises to Him, offering sacrifices and prayers that "if we are delivered from these calamities, we will do such and such."

However, this awakening and awareness—which is a reflection of the spirit of innate monotheism—does not last long for such individuals. As soon as the storm of calamity subsides and the problem is solved, veils of negligence fall over their hearts—heavy veils that nothing but a storm of calamity could move.

Although this temporary awakening has no educational effect on extremely tainted individuals, it completes the proof (hujjah) against them and will be a reason for their condemnation.

However, individuals who have only minor impurities are usually awakened by such events and correct their path. But as for the true servants of Allah, their account is clear; in times of peace, they are as attentive to Allah as they are in times of hardship, for they know that every good and blessing that apparently reaches them through natural causes is, in reality, from Allah. In any case, this reminder and admonition appears in many verses of the Holy Quran.

  1. In the verses above, the opposite of "dharaa" (ضراء - distress and harm) is mentioned as "rahmah" (رحمة - mercy), rather than "sarraa" (سراء - happiness and joy). This indicates that any good that reaches man is from Allah and His infinite mercy, whereas evils—if they are not intended as a lesson—are the effects of man's own actions.

  2. At the beginning of the second verse under discussion, the pronouns are mentioned in the second person (addressing "you"), but in the middle of the verse, they switch to the third person (referring to "them"). This certainly has a point.

Some commentators have said that this change in the tone of the verse is to describe the state of the polytheists when they are caught in a storm and calamity as a lesson for others; for this reason, they are treated as "absent" (third person) while the rest are "present" (second person).

Others have said the point is to show indifference and contempt toward them; as if Allah first accepts them into His presence and addresses them, then distances them and leaves them.

It is also possible that the verse is a natural depiction of the people's situation: as long as they are on the ship and have not left the shore, they are among the community and can thus be addressed. But when the ship carries them away from the shore and they gradually disappear from sight, they are counted as an absent group. This is a vivid depiction of their two different states.
