  1. The phrase "uheetu bihim" (أُحِيطَ بِهِمْ‌) means they are surrounded by waves of calamity from every side, but here it is a metaphor for destruction and annihilation, which is the inevitable consequence of being so surrounded.

Ayahs

إِنَّمَا مَثَلُ ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا كَمَآءٍ أَنزَلْنَـٰهُ مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ فَٱخْتَلَطَ بِهِۦ نَبَاتُ ٱلْأَرْضِ مِمَّا يَأْكُلُ ٱلنَّاسُ وَٱلْأَنْعَـٰمُ حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَآ أَخَذَتِ ٱلْأَرْضُ زُخْرُفَهَا وَٱزَّيَّنَتْ وَظَنَّ أَهْلُهَآ أَنَّهُمْ قَـٰدِرُونَ عَلَيْهَآ أَتَىٰهَآ أَمْرُنَا لَيْلًا أَوْ نَهَارًا فَجَعَلْنَـٰهَا حَصِيدًا كَأَن لَّمْ تَغْنَ بِٱلْأَمْسِ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ نُفَصِّلُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ لِقَوْمٍ يَتَفَكَّرُونَ (٢٤)
The parable of the life of this world is that of water which We send down from the sky. It mingles with the earth’s vegetation from which humans and cattle eat. When the earth puts on its luster and is adorned, and its inhabitants think they have power over it, Our edict comes to it, by night or day, whereat We turn it into a mown field, as if it did not flourish the day before. Thus do We elaborate the signs for a people who reflect. (24)
وَٱللَّهُ يَدْعُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰ دَارِ ٱلسَّلَـٰمِ وَيَهْدِى مَن يَشَآءُ إِلَىٰ صِرَٰطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ (٢٥)
Allah invites to the abode of peace, and He guides whomever He wishes to a straight path. (25)

Commentary

A Vision of Worldly Life

In the preceding verses, there was an allusion to the instability of worldly life. In the first verse under discussion, this reality is explained through a striking parable to pull back the veils of pride and negligence from before the eyes of the heedless and the rebellious.

"The example of the worldly life is but like water which We have sent down from the sky" (إِنَّما مَثَلُ الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا كَماءٍ أَنْزَلْناهُ مِنَ السَّماءِ).

These life-giving drops of rain fall upon prepared lands, "and by it, the various plants of the earth mingle, of which men and cattle eat" (فَاخْتَلَطَ بِهِ نَباتُ الْأَرْضِ مِمَّا يَأْكُلُ النَّاسُ وَ الْأَنْعامُ‌).

In addition to the nutritional properties they provide for living beings, these plants cover the surface of the earth and adorn it, to the point that "the earth takes on its finest golden ornament and is embellished" (حَتَّى إِذا أَخَذَتِ الْأَرْضُ زُخْرُفَها وَ ازَّيَّنَتْ‌).

At this moment, blossoms decorate the branches, flowers smile, plants glisten under the sunlight, and stalks and boughs sway in delight with the breeze. Grains and fruits gradually reveal themselves, embodying a scene teeming with life in every sense of the word, filling hearts with hope and eyes with joy and delight, such that "the people of the earth think they have mastery over it"—both over the fruits and their life-giving grains (وَ ظَنَّ أَهْلُها أَنَّهُمْ قادِرُونَ عَلَيْها).

"But suddenly, Our command reaches it (a severe cold, heavy hail, or a crushing storm overcomes them) and We make it like a reaped harvest, as if it had not flourished only yesterday!" (أَتاها أَمْرُنا لَيْلًا أَوْ نَهاراً فَجَعَلْناها حَصِيداً كَأَنْ لَمْ تَغْنَ بِالْأَمْسِ‌).

"لَمْ تَغْنَ‌" is derived from the root "غنا" (ghina), meaning to reside in a place. Therefore, the phrase "لم تغن بالامس" means "it was not in this place yesterday," which is a metaphor for something being completely destroyed as if it had never existed!

At the end of the verse, for further emphasis, He says: "Thus do We explain the signs in detail for a people who give thought" (كَذلِكَ نُفَصِّلُ الْآياتِ لِقَوْمٍ يَتَفَكَّرُونَ‌).

What has been described is a clear and eloquent depiction of the fleeting, deceptive, and glamorous life of the material world, where neither its status nor its wealth is lasting, nor is it a place of security and well-being. Therefore, in the next verse, with a short sentence, He points to the opposite of such a life and says: "Allah invites to the Abode of Peace" (وَ اللَّهُ يَدْعُوا إِلى‌ دارِ السَّلامِ‌).

There, there is no news of the conflicts of the plunderers of the material world, nor the foolish disturbances of the God-unaware hoarders of wealth, nor war, bloodshed, colonialism, or exploitation. All these concepts are gathered in the term "دارِ السَّلامِ‌" (The Abode of Peace).

And whenever life in this world also takes on a monotheistic and resurrection-oriented form, it too transforms into "Dar al-Salam" and emerges from the state of that "calamity-stricken, storm-ravaged field."

Then He adds: "And He guides whom He wills (and deems worthy and capable) to a straight path"—the very path that leads to the Abode of Peace and the center of safety and security (وَ يَهْدِي مَنْ يَشاءُ إِلى‌ صِراطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ‌).

Two points must be noted here

1- Since the Quran is a book of human-building and education, in many instances, it resorts to mentioning parables to clarify intellectual truths. Sometimes, it visualizes subjects whose beginning and end take years in a fleeting scene that can be studied before the eyes of the people.

Examining the eventful history of a human life or a generation, which sometimes lasts a hundred years, is not easy for ordinary people. However, when a scene like the life of many plants, which is summarized in a few months (from birth, growth, and beauty to destruction), is placed before them, they can very easily see the nature of their own lives in this transparent mirror.

Visualize this scene exactly before your eyes: a garden full of trees, greenery, and plants, all of which have borne fruit, and the clamor of life is evident throughout. But on a dark night or a bright day, suddenly black clouds cover the sky; thunder, lightning, and then storms, heavy downpours, and large hailstones crush it from every side.

Tomorrow, when we come to see that garden, we observe broken trees, scattered and withered plants, and everything tumbled together upon the soil, such that we cannot believe this is the same lush, green garden that was smiling at us yesterday.

Yes, such is the story of human lives, especially in our age and time, where sometimes an earthquake or a few hours of war so crushes a prosperous and flourishing city that nothing remains but a ruin with a handful of dismembered corpses.

Oh, how heedless are the people who have set their hearts on such an unstable life?! Regarding the phrase "فَاخْتَلَطَ بِهِ نَباتُ الْأَرْضِ‌", it should be noted that "اختلاط" (ikhtilat), as Raghib has said in Al-Mufradat, originally means to bring together two or more things, whether they be liquid or solid. "Ikhtilat" is more general than "imtizaj" (as imtizaj is usually used for liquids). Therefore, the meaning of the sentence is that by means of rainwater, plants of every kind mingle together—plants that are useful for humans or used by animals. [1] The above sentence also gives an implicit hint to the fact that Allah, from rainwater which is of only one type and one reality, grows various kinds of plants that provide for the different needs of humans and animals with their diverse nutritional substances.

Ayahs

۞ لِّلَّذِينَ أَحْسَنُوا۟ ٱلْحُسْنَىٰ وَزِيَادَةٌ ۖ وَلَا يَرْهَقُ وُجُوهَهُمْ قَتَرٌ وَلَا ذِلَّةٌ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْجَنَّةِ ۖ هُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ (٢٦)
Those who are virtuous shall receive the best reward and an enhancement. Neither dust nor abasement shall overcast their faces. They shall be the inhabitants of paradise, and they shall remain in it [forever]. (26)
وَٱلَّذِينَ كَسَبُوا۟ ٱلسَّيِّـَٔاتِ جَزَآءُ سَيِّئَةٍۭ بِمِثْلِهَا وَتَرْهَقُهُمْ ذِلَّةٌ ۖ مَّا لَهُم مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ مِنْ عَاصِمٍ ۖ كَأَنَّمَآ أُغْشِيَتْ وُجُوهُهُمْ قِطَعًا مِّنَ ٱلَّيْلِ مُظْلِمًا ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلنَّارِ ۖ هُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ (٢٧)
For those who have committed misdeeds, the requital of a misdeed shall be its like, and they shall be overcast by abasement. They shall have no one to protect [them] from Allah. [They will be] as if their faces were covered with dark patches of the night. They shall be the inmates of the Fire, and they shall remain in it [forever]. (27)

Commentary

The Bright-Faced and the Dark-Faced

In the preceding verses, reference was made to the abode of the Hereafter and the Day of Resurrection; accordingly, the verses under discussion describe the fate of the righteous and those tainted by sin in that realm.

First, it states: "For those who do good is the best reward and more" (لِلَّذِينَ أَحْسَنُوا الْحُسْنى‌ وَ زِيادَةٌ) [^1]. There is discussion among commentators regarding what is meant by "more" (ziyadah) in this sentence. However, considering that the verses of the Quran interpret one another, it refers to the multiplied and abundant rewards that are sometimes added tenfold and sometimes thousands of times over (proportionate to the sincerity, purity, piety, and value of the deed). In Surah Al-An'am, verse 160, we read: مَنْ جاءَ بِالْحَسَنَةِ فَلَهُ عَشْرُ أَمْثالِها: "Whoever brings a good deed shall have ten times the like thereof."

And elsewhere we read: فَأَمَّا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ عَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ فَيُوَفِّيهِمْ أُجُورَهُمْ وَ يَزِيدُهُمْ مِنْ فَضْلِهِ‌: "As for those who believed and did righteous deeds, He will give them their rewards in full and will add to them out of His grace and bounty" (Surah An-Nisa, verse 173).

In the verses related to charity in Surah Al-Baqarah (verse 261), there is also mention of the reward for the righteous being up to seven hundred times or several multiples thereof.

Another point to be noted here is that it is entirely possible for this "addition" to increase continuously in the other world; meaning that every day, a new blessing and grace from Allah is bestowed upon them. This actually shows that life in the other world is not monotonous and progresses toward perfection in an infinite form.

The narrations quoted from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله in the interpretation of this verse, stating that "more" refers to beholding the manifestation of the Pure Essence of the Lord and benefiting from this great spiritual blessing, may point to this very fact.

In some narrations narrated from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام, "more" has been interpreted as the blessings of this world, which Allah grants to the righteous in addition to the reward of the other world.

However, there is no obstacle to the word "more" in the above verse referring to all of these blessings.

Then it adds: "The righteous on that day have radiant faces, and neither darkness nor humiliation shall cover their faces" (وَ لا يَرْهَقُ وُجُوهَهُمْ قَتَرٌ وَ لا ذِلَّةٌ).

"Yarhaqu" is derived from the root "rahaq," meaning to cover forcibly and compulsorily, and "qatar" means dust or smoke.

At the end of the verse, it says: "These are the companions of Paradise; they will abide therein eternally" (أُولئِكَ أَصْحابُ الْجَنَّةِ هُمْ فِيها خالِدُونَ‌).

The expression "companions" (ashab) refers to the compatibility that exists between the spirit of this group and the environment of Paradise.

In the next verse, mention is made of the people of Hell, who are the exact opposite of the first group. It says: "And those who have earned evil deeds, the recompense of an evil deed is the like thereof" (وَ الَّذِينَ كَسَبُوا السَّيِّئاتِ جَزاءُ سَيِّئَةٍ بِمِثْلِها).

Here, there is no mention of "more," because in reward, "more" is grace and mercy, but in punishment, justice requires that it be not a fraction more than the sin.

But they, unlike the first group, have dark faces "and humiliation covers them" (وَ تَرْهَقُهُمْ ذِلَّةٌ) [^1]. The question might be asked: justice requires they not be punished more than their sin, so is this darkness of face and the dust of humiliation settling upon it something extra?

However, it must be noted that this is the property and effect of the deed itself, which is reflected outward from within the human soul. It is exactly like saying that drunkards must be lashed, while at the same time, alcohol causes various diseases of the stomach, heart, liver, and nerves.

In any case, the evildoers might imagine they will have a way of escape or salvation, or that idols and the like can intercede for them; but the following sentence explicitly states that "none can protect them from Allah" (ما لَهُمْ مِنَ اللَّهِ مِنْ عاصِمٍ‌).

The darkness of their faces is so intense that "it is as if their faces had been covered with patches of the night, dark and gloomy, one after another" (كَأَنَّما أُغْشِيَتْ وُجُوهُهُمْ قِطَعاً مِنَ اللَّيْلِ مُظْلِماً).

"They are the companions of the Fire; they will abide therein eternally" (أُولئِكَ أَصْحابُ النَّارِ هُمْ فِيها خالِدُونَ‌).

[^1]: It should be noted that "al-husna" in this sentence is a delayed subject (mubtada' mu'akhkhar), and the meaning of the verse is: "The best reward is for those who do good." Therefore, "ziyadah," which is conjoined to it, is in the nominative case (marfu'). "Al-husna" is an adjective for "al-mathubah" (the reward), which is implied and stands in place of the noun it describes.

[^1]: It is possible, by the context of the previous verse, that the phrase "tarhaquhum dhillah" implies "tarhaquhum qatarun wa dhillah," where "qatar" (darkness/dust) has been omitted for brevity due to the parallel structure.

Ayahs

وَيَوْمَ نَحْشُرُهُمْ جَمِيعًا ثُمَّ نَقُولُ لِلَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا۟ مَكَانَكُمْ أَنتُمْ وَشُرَكَآؤُكُمْ ۚ فَزَيَّلْنَا بَيْنَهُمْ ۖ وَقَالَ شُرَكَآؤُهُم مَّا كُنتُمْ إِيَّانَا تَعْبُدُونَ (٢٨)
On the day when We gather them all together, We shall say to those who ascribe partners [to Allah], ‘Stay where you are—you and your partners!’ Then We shall set them apart from one another, and their partners will say, ‘It was not us that you worshipped. (28)
فَكَفَىٰ بِٱللَّهِ شَهِيدًۢا بَيْنَنَا وَبَيْنَكُمْ إِن كُنَّا عَنْ عِبَادَتِكُمْ لَغَـٰفِلِينَ (٢٩)
Allah suffices as a witness between you and us. We were indeed unaware of your worship.’ (29)
هُنَالِكَ تَبْلُوا۟ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ مَّآ أَسْلَفَتْ ۚ وَرُدُّوٓا۟ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ مَوْلَىٰهُمُ ٱلْحَقِّ ۖ وَضَلَّ عَنْهُم مَّا كَانُوا۟ يَفْتَرُونَ (٣٠)
There every soul will examine what it has sent in advance, and they will be returned to Allah, their real master, and what they used to fabricate will forsake them. (30)

Commentary

A Scene from the Resurrection of the Idolaters

These verses continue the previous discussions regarding "the Origin" (Creation) and "the Resurrection," as well as the state of the polytheists, portraying their helplessness when they are brought before the Divine Court of Justice and His reckoning.

First, it says: "And remember the day when We shall gather all servants together" (وَ يَوْمَ نَحْشُرُهُمْ جَمِيعاً).

"Then We shall say to those who associated partners [with Allah]: 'Stay in your place, you and your partners,' so that your account may be settled" (ثُمَّ نَقُولُ لِلَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا مَكانَكُمْ أَنْتُمْ‌ وَ شُرَكاؤُكُمْ). [^1] It is interesting that in the above verse, the "idols" are referred to as "your partners" (شركائكم), meaning "your own partners," whereas the polytheists had considered the idols to be partners to Allah, not to themselves.

This expression is, in fact, a subtle allusion to the point that the idols were never truly partners to Allah; rather, it was the delusions and fantasies of the idolaters that granted them this status. That is, they are partners of your own choosing. This is exactly like someone who chooses an unfit teacher or principal for a school, and that person commits wrongdoings; we say to the one who chose him: "Come and see what this teacher of yours and this principal of yours has done" (while he is neither his personal teacher nor his principal, but the teacher and principal of the school, yet he was the one who selected them).

Then it adds that "We shall separate these two groups (the worshipped and the worshippers) from one another," and We shall question each separately (just as is customary in all courts of law where each person is interrogated individually).

We ask the idolaters: "For what reason did you associate these idols with Allah and worship them?" And We ask the worshipped ones: "For what reason did you become objects of worship or submit to this act?" (فَزَيَّلْنا بَيْنَهُمْ). [^1] At this moment, the partners they had fabricated will speak: "And their partners will say: 'It was not us that you used to worship'" (وَ قالَ شُرَكاؤُهُمْ ما كُنْتُمْ إِيَّانا تَعْبُدُونَ).

In reality, you were worshipping your own whims, desires, delusions, and fantasies, not us. Furthermore, your worship of us was neither by our command nor with our consent, and worship that is such is not truly worship at all.

[^1]: "مكانكم" is actually the object of an omitted verb, and originally it was: "الزموا مَكانَكُمْ أَنْتُمْ وَ شُرَكاؤُكُمْ حتّى تُسئَلوا" (Remain in your place, you and your partners, until you are questioned). This sentence is essentially similar to verse 24 of Surah As-Saffat which says: "وَ قِفُوهُمْ إِنَّهُمْ مَسْؤُلُونَ" (And stop them, for they are to be questioned).

Then, for further emphasis, they say: "Allah is sufficient as a witness between us and you, that we were indeed unaware of your worship" (فَكَفى‌ بِاللَّهِ شَهِيداً بَيْنَنا وَ بَيْنَكُمْ إِنْ كُنَّا عَنْ عِبادَتِكُمْ لَغافِلِينَ). ^2 There is discussion among commentators regarding who is meant by the "idols" and "partners" in the above verse, and how they are able to speak such words.

Some have suggested that the reference is to human, satanic, or angelic deities who possess intellect, consciousness, and perception, yet were unaware that a group was worshipping them, either because such worship took place in their absence or after their death (like humans who were worshipped after they passed away).

[^1]: "زيلنا" is from the root "تزييل" meaning to separate. As some linguists have stated, its triliteral root is "زال يزيل" meaning to become separate, not from the root "زال يزول" meaning to cease to exist or vanish.

Therefore, their speaking would be entirely natural, and this verse is similar to verse 41 of Surah Saba which says: وَ يَوْمَ يَحْشُرُهُمْ جَمِيعاً ثُمَّ يَقُولُ لِلْمَلائِكَةِ أَ هؤُلاءِ إِيَّاكُمْ كانُوا يَعْبُدُونَ: "And the day He will gather them all together, then He will say to the angels: 'Was it you these people used to worship?'"

Another possibility mentioned by many commentators is that on that day, Allah will create life and consciousness in the idols such that they can express the truths. The above sentence quoted from the idols, where they call Allah to witness that they were unaware of the worship of their worshippers, is more consistent with this meaning, as stone and wooden idols normally understand nothing at all.

We can also suggest an interpretation that includes all deities: those that possess intellect and consciousness will speak the truth with their own tongues, while those that lack intellect and consciousness will speak through their "state of being" (zaban-e hal) and by reflecting the effects of the deeds. This is just as we say, "The color of your face reveals the secret of your heart." The Quran also states in verse 21 of Surah Fussilat that human skins will speak in the world of Resurrection, and similarly in Surah Al-Zalzalah, it says the lands upon which humans lived will recount the truths.

This matter is not a complex issue in our age and time; when a voiceless tape records all our words and replays them when necessary, it is not surprising that idols could also reflect the realities of the deeds of their worshippers!

In any case, "on that day, in that place, and in that state—as the Quran says in the final verse under discussion—"every soul will test [the results of] what it had previously sent forth" (هُنالِكَ تَبْلُوا كُلُّ نَفْسٍ ما أَسْلَفَتْ). They will see the results, or rather the deeds themselves—whether they were worshippers or the misguided deities who invited people to worship them, whether they were polytheists or believers of every group and kind.

"And on that day, they will all be returned to Allah, their true Master and Guardian" (وَ رُدُّوا إِلَى اللَّهِ مَوْلاهُمُ الْحَقِ). The court of the Gathering will show that sovereignty belongs solely to His command.

"And finally, all the idols and fabricated deities that they had falsely associated as partners with Allah will be lost, vanished, and destroyed from them" (وَ ضَلَّ عَنْهُمْ ما كانُوا يَفْتَرُونَ).

For that is the arena where all the hidden secrets of the servants are manifested and brought to light, and no reality remains except that it reveals itself. Fundamentally, there are stations and positions there that require neither questioning nor dispute and dialogue; rather, the state of affairs alone recounts everything, and there is no need for speech!

Ayahs

قُلْ مَن يَرْزُقُكُم مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ أَمَّن يَمْلِكُ ٱلسَّمْعَ وَٱلْأَبْصَـٰرَ وَمَن يُخْرِجُ ٱلْحَىَّ مِنَ ٱلْمَيِّتِ وَيُخْرِجُ ٱلْمَيِّتَ مِنَ ٱلْحَىِّ وَمَن يُدَبِّرُ ٱلْأَمْرَ ۚ فَسَيَقُولُونَ ٱللَّهُ ۚ فَقُلْ أَفَلَا تَتَّقُونَ (٣١)
Say, ‘Who provides for you out of the sky and the earth? Who controls [your] hearing and sight, and who brings forth the living from the dead and brings forth the dead from the living, and who directs the command?’ They will say, ‘Allah.’ Say, ‘Will you not then be wary [of Him]?’ (31)
فَذَٰلِكُمُ ٱللَّهُ رَبُّكُمُ ٱلْحَقُّ ۖ فَمَاذَا بَعْدَ ٱلْحَقِّ إِلَّا ٱلضَّلَـٰلُ ۖ فَأَنَّىٰ تُصْرَفُونَ (٣٢)
That, then, is Allah, your true Lord. So what is there after the truth except error? Then where are you being led away? (32)
كَذَٰلِكَ حَقَّتْ كَلِمَتُ رَبِّكَ عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ فَسَقُوٓا۟ أَنَّهُمْ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ (٣٣)
Thus the word of your Lord became due against those who transgress, that they shall not have faith. (33)

Commentary

[Signs of the Lord]

These verses speak of the signs of the Lord's existence and His worthiness of worship, following the discussions of the previous verses in this regard.

First, He says: Tell the polytheists and idol-worshippers who are wandering in the wrong path:

"Say: Who provides for you from the heaven and the earth?" (قُلْ مَنْ يَرْزُقُكُمْ مِنَ السَّماءِ وَ الْأَرْضِ).

"Rizq" (provision) means a continuous grant and gift, and since the giver of all blessings is in reality Allah, the terms "Raziq" and "Razzaq" (The Provider) apply in their true sense only to Him. If this word is used for anyone other than Him, it undoubtedly carries a metaphorical aspect, as in verse 233 of Surah Al-Baqarah regarding nursing women: "And upon the father is their provision and their clothing according to what is acceptable" (وَ عَلَى الْمَوْلُودِ لَهُ رِزْقُهُنَّ وَ كِسْوَتُهُنَّ بِالْمَعْرُوفِ).

It is also necessary to remind that most of man's provisions come from the heaven; life-giving rain falls from the sky, and the air required by all living beings is situated above the earth. Most importantly, sunlight—without which no living being and no movement or activity would exist across the entire earth—comes from the heaven. Even animals in the depths of the seas live by the reflection of sunlight, for we know that the food for many of them is tiny plants that grow amidst the waves on the ocean surface under the radiance of sunlight, and another portion of those animals feed on the flesh of other sea creatures that were nourished by those plants.

However, the earth only nourishes the roots of plants through its own nutrients, and perhaps this is why the above verse first mentions the provisions of the heaven and then the provisions of the earth (due to the difference in the degree of importance). Then, it refers to two of the most important human senses, without which the acquisition of knowledge and science would be impossible for mankind, saying: "Or who is it that owns [and creates] the hearing and the sight [and gives power to these two human senses]?" (أَمَّنْ يَمْلِكُ السَّمْعَ وَ الْأَبْصارَ).

In fact, this verse first refers to material blessings, and then to spiritual gifts and provisions, without which material blessings lack purpose and substance.

The word "Sam'" (hearing) is singular, while "Absar" is the plural of "Basar" (sight/eyes). Here the question arises: why is "Sam'" mentioned as singular everywhere in the Quran, while "Basar" appears sometimes as plural and sometimes as singular? We have provided the answer to this question in Volume 1, page 56.

Next, it mentions the two phenomena of death and life, which are the most wondrous phenomena in the world of creation, saying: "And who brings out the living from the dead and brings out the dead from the living?" (وَ مَنْ يُخْرِجُ الْحَيَّ مِنَ الْمَيِّتِ وَ يُخْرِجُ الْمَيِّتَ مِنَ الْحَيِّ).

This is the very subject that has left the intellects of scientists, naturalists, and biologists bewildered to this day: how does a living being emerge from lifeless matter? Could such a thing—which the continuous efforts of scientists have so far failed to replicate—be a simple matter dependent on chance and unguided, purposeless accidents of nature? Undoubtedly, the complex, delicate, and mysterious phenomenon of life requires extraordinary knowledge, power, and a Universal Intellect.

He not only created the living being from the lifeless matter of the earth in the beginning, but furthermore, His Sunnah (way) is established such that life is not eternal; thus, He created death within the heart of life to open the field for transformation and evolution.

In the commentary of the above verse, the possibility has also been suggested that besides material death and life, it also includes spiritual death and life. For we see intelligent, virtuous, and faithful human beings who are sometimes born to polluted, faithless, and misguided parents. The opposite has also been observed, where—contrary to the law of inheritance—worthless and "dead" individuals are born to valuable parents [1]. Of course, there is no obstacle to the verse referring to both aspects, as both are among the wonders of creation and amazing phenomena of the world, illuminating the truth that beyond natural factors, the hand of power of a Wise and All-Knowing Creator is at work.

(In Volume 5, page 356, under verse 95 of Surah Al-An'am, we have provided further explanations on this matter).

Then it adds: "And who is it that regulates the affairs [of this world]?" (وَ مَنْ يُدَبِّرُ الْأَمْرَ).

In truth, first there is talk of the creation of blessings, then talk of their Preserver, Guardian, and Regulator.

After the Quran poses these three questions, it immediately says:

"They will soon say: 'Allah'."

From this sentence, it is clearly understood that even the polytheists and idol-worshippers of the Age of Ignorance considered Allah to be the Creator, Provider, Giver of Life, and Regulator of the affairs of the universe. They had realized this truth through both reason and primordial nature (Fitrah)—that this calculated system of the universe cannot be the product of disorder or the creation of idols.

At the end of the verse, He commands the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "Say: 'Will you not then be mindful [of Him]?'" (فَقُلْ أَ فَلا تَتَّقُونَ).

The only one worthy of worship and adoration is He in whose hand is the creation and regulation of the world. If worship is for the sake of the worthiness and greatness of the essence of the Deity, this worthiness and greatness belong only to Allah; and if it is because He can be the source of benefit and harm, this too is exclusive to Allah.

After mentioning examples of the signs of Allah's greatness and regulation in the heavens and the earth, and calling upon the conscience and intellect of the opponents to judge—to which they confessed—the next verse states in a decisive tone: "That then is Allah, your true Lord!" (فَذلِكُمُ اللَّهُ رَبُّكُمُ الْحَقُّ).

Not the idols, nor the other beings you have made partners with Allah in worship and before whom you prostrate and bow.

How can they be worthy of worship when they not only cannot participate in the creation and regulation of the world, but are themselves entirely in need and want?

Then it concludes: "Now that you have recognized the truth clearly, what is there after the truth except error [and misguidance]?" (فَما ذا بَعْدَ الْحَقِّ إِلَّا الضَّلالُ).

In this state, how are you turned away from the worship of Allah, while you know there is no true deity but Him? (فَأَنَّى تُصْرَفُونَ).

This verse, in fact, proposes a clear logical path for identifying falsehood and abandoning it: first, one must take steps through conscience and reason to recognize the truth; once the truth is recognized, whatever is other than it and contrary to it is falsehood and misguidance, and must be set aside.

In the final verse, to explain why they do not follow the truth despite its clarity and brightness, it says: "Thus has the word of your Lord become true against those who are rebellious [and have turned away from obedience out of knowledge and intent, contrary to reason and conscience]—that they will not believe." (كَذلِكَ حَقَّتْ كَلِمَةُ رَبِّكَ عَلَى الَّذِينَ فَسَقُوا أَنَّهُمْ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ) [1]. In reality, this is the property of their incorrect and continuous actions, which darken their hearts and pollute their souls such that they do not see the truth despite its clarity and instead go astray.

Therefore, the above verse does not indicate the issue of predestination (Jabr) at all; rather, it refers to the effects of man's own actions. However, there is no doubt that these actions have such a property by the command of Allah.

It is exactly like saying to someone: "We told you a hundred times not to go near narcotics and alcohol; now that you did not listen and have become severely addicted, you are condemned to remain in misery for a long time."

^1: This theme appears in several narrations in Volume 1 of Tafsir al-Burhan, page 543, under verse 95 of Surah Al-An'am.

Ayahs

قُلْ هَلْ مِن شُرَكَآئِكُم مَّن يَبْدَؤُا۟ ٱلْخَلْقَ ثُمَّ يُعِيدُهُۥ ۚ قُلِ ٱللَّهُ يَبْدَؤُا۟ ٱلْخَلْقَ ثُمَّ يُعِيدُهُۥ ۖ فَأَنَّىٰ تُؤْفَكُونَ (٣٤)
Say, ‘Is there anyone among your partners who originates the creation and then brings it back?’ Say, ‘Allah originates the creation, then He will bring it back.’ Then where do you stray? (34)
قُلْ هَلْ مِن شُرَكَآئِكُم مَّن يَهْدِىٓ إِلَى ٱلْحَقِّ ۚ قُلِ ٱللَّهُ يَهْدِى لِلْحَقِّ ۗ أَفَمَن يَهْدِىٓ إِلَى ٱلْحَقِّ أَحَقُّ أَن يُتَّبَعَ أَمَّن لَّا يَهِدِّىٓ إِلَّآ أَن يُهْدَىٰ ۖ فَمَا لَكُمْ كَيْفَ تَحْكُمُونَ (٣٥)
Say, ‘Is there anyone among your partners who may guide to the truth?’ Say, ‘Allah guides to the truth. Is He who guides to the truth worthier to be followed, or he who is not guided unless shown the way? What is the matter with you? How do you judge?’ (35)
وَمَا يَتَّبِعُ أَكْثَرُهُمْ إِلَّا ظَنًّا ۚ إِنَّ ٱلظَّنَّ لَا يُغْنِى مِنَ ٱلْحَقِّ شَيْـًٔا ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلِيمٌۢ بِمَا يَفْعَلُونَ (٣٦)
Most of them just follow conjecture; indeed conjecture is no substitute for the truth. Indeed Allah knows best what they do. (36)

Commentary

One of the Signs of Truth and Falsehood

These verses continue the arguments related to the Origin (Creation) and the Resurrection.

In the first verse, He commands the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله): "Say to them: Is there any of your 'partners' (those you associate with Allah) who can originate creation and then bring it back again?" (قُلْ هَلْ مِنْ شُرَكائِكُمْ مَنْ يَبْدَؤُا الْخَلْقَ ثُمَّ يُعِيدُهُ‌).

Then He adds: "Say: Allah originates creation and then brings it back" (قُلِ اللَّهُ يَبْدَؤُا الْخَلْقَ ثُمَّ يُعِيدُهُ‌).

"How then are you turned away [from the truth] and wander aimlessly?" (فَأَنَّى تُؤْفَكُونَ‌).

Two questions arise here: First, the Arab polytheists generally did not believe in the Resurrection, especially in the form described by the Quran; so how does the Quran demand an admission from them?

Second, in the previous verse, the speech was about the admission of the polytheists, but here He commands the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) to testify to this reality himself. What is the reason for this change in expression?

By considering one point, the answer to both questions becomes clear:

Although the polytheists did not believe in the Resurrection (the physical Resurrection), the fact that they believed the beginning of creation was from Allah is sufficient for accepting the Resurrection. This is because whoever performed the beginning is capable of repeating it. Therefore, belief in the Origin, with a little reflection, proves the Resurrection.

From this, it becomes clear why the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) testifies to this reality instead of the polytheists. Since faith in the Resurrection is a necessary consequence of faith in the Origin, but they were heedless of this logical necessity, the mode of expression changed, and the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) testifies on their behalf.

Once again, He commands the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله): "Say to them: Is there any of your false deities who guides to the truth?" (قُلْ هَلْ مِنْ شُرَكائِكُمْ مَنْ يَهْدِي إِلَى الْحَقِ‌).

This is because a deity must be a leader for its worshippers, and specifically a leader toward the truth. Meanwhile, the deities of the polytheists—whether inanimate idols or living beings—are none of them capable of guiding anyone to the truth without Divine guidance. This is because guiding toward the truth requires the station of infallibility and protection from error and mistake, which is impossible without the leadership and support of Allah.

Therefore, He immediately adds: "Say: Allah alone guides to the truth" (قُلِ اللَّهُ يَهْدِي لِلْحَقِ‌).

This being the case, "Is He who guides to the truth more worthy to be followed, or he who finds not guidance unless he is guided?" (أَ فَمَنْ يَهْدِي إِلَى الْحَقِّ أَحَقُّ أَنْ يُتَّبَعَ أَمَّنْ لا يَهِدِّي إِلَّا أَنْ يُهْدى‌) [1]. At the end of the verse, He says in a rebuking and reproachful tone: "What is [the matter] with you? How do you judge?" (فَما لَكُمْ كَيْفَ تُحْكُمُونَ‌).

In the final verse, He points to the source and the main factor of their deviations, saying: "Most of them follow nothing but conjecture; indeed, conjecture can never take the place of truth or lead to it" (وَ ما يَتَّبِعُ أَكْثَرُهُمْ إِلَّا ظَنًّا إِنَّ الظَّنَّ لا يُغْنِي مِنَ الْحَقِّ شَيْئاً).

Finally, with a threatening tone toward such individuals who follow no logic or reason, He says: "Indeed, Allah is All-Knowing of what they do" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ عَلِيمٌ بِما يَفْعَلُونَ‌).

[1]: "Yahiddi" (يَهِدِّي) was originally "Yahtadi" (يَهْتَدِي), where the "ta" (ت) was changed to "dal" (د) and merged into it.

Key Points

  1. In the above verses, we read that only Allah guides to the truth. This exclusivity is either because "guidance" here does not mean merely "showing the way" but also "conveying to the destination," which is solely in the hands of the Lord. Or, it is because even showing the way is primarily the work of Allah; others, namely the Prophets and Divine guides, only become aware of the paths of guidance through His guidance and become knowledgeable through His teaching.

  2. We read in the verses above that the deities of the polytheists not only cannot guide anyone but are themselves in need of Divine guidance. Although this does not literally apply to stone and wooden idols—as they possess no intellect or consciousness whatsoever—it applies perfectly to conscious deities such as angels or humans who were taken as objects of worship. It is also possible that the aforementioned sentence is in the form of a conditional proposition; meaning, even assuming the idols had intellect and consciousness, they would not find the way themselves without Divine guidance, let alone be able to guide others. In any case, the above verses clearly show that one of the primary programs of the Lord for His servants is guiding them toward the truth. This is accomplished through granting intellect and reason, providing various lessons through primordial nature (fitrah), presenting His signs in the world of creation, and also through sending Prophets and Heavenly Books.

  3. In the last verse under discussion, we read that "most" of the idolaters and polytheists follow their own conjecture. Here the question arises: why did it not say "all" of them? We know that all idolaters share in the false notion that idols are rightful deities, masters of benefit and harm, and intercessors before Allah. For this reason, some have felt compelled to interpret the word "most" (akthar) as "all," believing that this word sometimes carries that meaning. However, this response is not very significant. It is better to say that the idolaters are of two groups: one group, which forms the majority, are superstitious, ignorant, and unaware individuals who have fallen under the influence of false conjectures and chosen idols for worship. But the other group, which forms the minority, are the black-hearted and aware leaders who, despite knowing the baselessness of idol worship, invite people toward idols to preserve their own interests. For this reason, Allah addresses only the first group, as they are capable of being guided, while He pays no heed whatsoever to the second group who knowingly traverse this wrong path.

  4. A group of scholars of Usul al-Fiqh (Principles of Jurisprudence) consider the above verse and its likes as proof that conjecture (zann) can in no way be a legal proof (hujjah) or authority, and that only definitive evidence (qati') can be relied upon. However, another group—noting that among jurisprudential proofs we have many "conjectural" proofs (such as the authority of the literal meanings of words, the testimony of two just witnesses, or the report of a single reliable narrator, etc.)—say that the above verse proves that the primary rule regarding conjecture is its lack of authority, unless its authority is proven by definitive evidence, as in the examples mentioned above. But in fairness, the above verse speaks only of baseless notions and unfounded, superstitious conjectures, such as the conjectures of the idolaters. It has nothing to do with the "reliable conjecture" that exists among rational people. Therefore, the above verse and similar verses cannot be cited as evidence regarding the general lack of authority of conjecture (Reflect carefully).

Ayahs

وَمَا كَانَ هَـٰذَا ٱلْقُرْءَانُ أَن يُفْتَرَىٰ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ وَلَـٰكِن تَصْدِيقَ ٱلَّذِى بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ وَتَفْصِيلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ لَا رَيْبَ فِيهِ مِن رَّبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٣٧)
This Quran could not have been fabricated by anyone besides Allah; rather, it is a confirmation of what was [revealed] before it, and an elaboration of the Book, there is no doubt in it, from the Lord of all the worlds. (37)
أَمْ يَقُولُونَ ٱفْتَرَىٰهُ ۖ قُلْ فَأْتُوا۟ بِسُورَةٍ مِّثْلِهِۦ وَٱدْعُوا۟ مَنِ ٱسْتَطَعْتُم مِّن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ (٣٨)
Do they say, ‘He has fabricated it?’ Say, ‘Then bring a surah like it, and invoke whomever you can, besides Allah, should you be truthful.’ (38)
بَلْ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِمَا لَمْ يُحِيطُوا۟ بِعِلْمِهِۦ وَلَمَّا يَأْتِهِمْ تَأْوِيلُهُۥ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ كَذَّبَ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ ۖ فَٱنظُرْ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٣٩)
Indeed, they deny something whose knowledge they do not comprehend, and whose explanation has not yet come to them. Those who were before them denied likewise. So observe how was the fate of the wrongdoers! (39)
وَمِنْهُم مَّن يُؤْمِنُ بِهِۦ وَمِنْهُم مَّن لَّا يُؤْمِنُ بِهِۦ ۚ وَرَبُّكَ أَعْلَمُ بِٱلْمُفْسِدِينَ (٤٠)
Some of them believe in it, and some of them do not believe in it, and your Lord best knows the agents of corruption. (40)

Commentary

The Grandeur and Truthfulness of the Quranic Call

These verses address another part of the polytheists' improper claims. They were not only deviated in their recognition of the Origin (God) but also fabricated slanders against the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family), claiming he had authored the Quran from his own mind and attributed it to Allah. Thus, we read in the preceding verses that they demanded the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) bring a Quran other than this one or at least change it; this itself is proof that they considered the Quran a product of his own thought! The first verse under discussion says: "It is not fitting that this Quran should be attributed to Allah without Divine revelation" (وَمَا كَانَ هَٰذَا الْقُرْآنُ أَنْ يُفْتَرَىٰ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ). Interestingly, instead of a simple negation, a "negation of suitability" (shaniyyat) is used. We know that this form of expression is more eloquent than a simple negation; it is exactly like someone saying in self-defense, "It is not in my character to lie," which is far more meaningful and profound than saying, "I do not lie."

Then, it proceeds to provide evidence for the authenticity of the Quran and its status as Heavenly revelation, saying:

"But it is a confirmation of the [Heavenly] books before it" (وَلَٰكِنْ تَصْدِيقَ الَّذِي بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ).

This means that all the glad tidings and signs of truthfulness mentioned in previous Heavenly books perfectly align with the Quran and its bringer. This itself proves that it is not a fabrication against Allah but a reality. Fundamentally, the Quran itself—by the principle of "the sun is the proof of the sun"—is a witness to the truth of its own content.

From here, it becomes clear that those who take such verses as proof of the non-distortion of the Torah and the Gospel are mistaken. The Quran does not confirm the [current] contents of those books as they existed during the era of the Quran's revelation; rather, it confirms the signs of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) and the Quran that were present in those books (take note). Further explanations on this matter were provided in Volume 1 of Tafsir Nemouneh under verse 41 of Surah Al-Baqarah.

Then, it mentions another reason for the authenticity of this Heavenly revelation, saying: "In this Quran is an explanation of the original books of the previous Prophets and a statement of their fundamental laws and core beliefs; for this reason, there is no doubt in it that it is from the Lord of the worlds" (وَتَفْصِيلَ الْكِتَابِ لَا رَيْبَ فِيهِ مِنْ رَبِّ الْعَالَمِينَ).

In other words, it has no contradiction with the programs of past Prophets; rather, the perfection of those teachings and programs is seen within it. If this Quran were forged, it would certainly have been contrary and inconsistent with them.

From this, we know that regarding fundamental issues—whether in religious beliefs, social programs, the protection of rights, the struggle against ignorance, the call to truth and justice, as well as the revival of moral values and the like—there is no disagreement among the Heavenly books. The only difference is that each subsequent book revealed was at a higher and more complete level, much like the different levels of instruction in primary school, high school, and university, until it reached the final book intended for the era of the completion of the nations' religious education: the Quran.

There is no doubt that differences exist in the details of laws and branches among Heavenly religions and sects, but the discussion here concerns their fundamental principles, which are harmonious everywhere.

In the next verse, it mentions a third proof for the authenticity of the Quran, saying: "They say the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) has falsely attributed this Quran to Allah; tell them: if you are truthful, then bring a surah like it, and call upon whomever you can besides Allah for cooperation" (but you will never be able to do so, and thus it is proven that this is Heavenly revelation) (أَمْ يَقُولُونَ افْتَرَاهُ ۖ قُلْ فَأْتُوا بِسُورَةٍ مِثْلِهِ وَادْعُوا مَنِ اسْتَطَعْتُمْ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صَادِقِينَ).

This verse is among those that explicitly state the miraculous nature (i'jaz) of the Quran—not just the whole Quran, but even the miracle of a single surah. It invites all the worlds without exception: if you believe these verses are not from Allah, bring something like them, or at least like one surah of them.

As we explained in Volume 1 under verse 23 of Surah Al-Baqarah, the Quranic verses sometimes issue a "challenge" (tahaddi)—an invitation to challenge—regarding the entire Quran, sometimes ten surahs, and sometimes a single surah. This shows that both the parts and the whole of the Quran are miraculous.

Since no specific surah is mentioned, it includes every surah of the Quran.

Of course, there is no doubt that the miracle of the Quran is not limited to the aspect of eloquence, rhetoric, sweetness of expression, and clarity of terms—as a group of earlier commentators thought. Rather, in addition to these, it is also miraculous in terms of its statement of religious knowledge, sciences unknown until that day, the statement of laws and regulations, the mention of past histories purified from any error or superstition, and the absence of contradiction and discrepancy within it [1].

[1]: For further explanation, refer to Volume 1, page 83 onwards, and the book The Quran and the Final Prophet.

A New Manifestation of the Quran's Miracle

Interestingly, with the passage of time, new manifestations of the Quran's miracle are revealed that were not noticed in the past. Among these are the numerous calculations performed on the words of the Quranic verses using electronic brains (computers), which have proven new characteristics for the words and sentence structures of the Quran and their relationship with the time of each surah's revelation. What you read below is an example of this:

The research of some contemporary investigative scholars has led to the discovery of complex relationships and highly precise computational formulas that are astonishing. With the certainty that such a scientific order exists in the structure of the Quran, they have succeeded—through statistical analysis and the language of mathematics—in discovering precise rules, mathematical formulas, and perfect, healthy curves that evoke the significance of Newton's discovery of gravity.

A great Quranic scholar's starting point is this simple issue: the verses revealed in Mecca are short, and the verses revealed in Medina are long. This is a natural matter; any skilled writer or speaker changes the length of sentences and the rhythm of words according to the subject of the speech:

Descriptive matters use short sentences, while analytical and argumentative matters use long ones. Where the speech is provocative, critical, or stating general principles of belief, the tone becomes slogan-like with short phrases. Where a story begins, or speech is used for moral conclusions, the tone is calm, the phrases are long, and the rhythm is soft.

The issues raised in Mecca are of the first type, and the issues raised in Medina are of the second type, because in Mecca it was the beginning of a movement and the statement of general principles of belief and critique, while in Medina it was a society with legal and moral issues, historical stories, and intellectual and scientific conclusions.

However, the Quran is a natural form of speech and is inevitably subject to the beautiful and eloquent style of human speech, and consequently, the observance of the brevity or length of verses is in proportion to the concepts.

But if we accept that this speech is simultaneously a book and a "nature," then this brevity and length must not be haphazard. It should follow a precise scientific rule, starting from short verses and moving uniformly and progressively toward long verses. Based on this, the length of each verse should be shorter than a verse revealed the following year and longer than a verse revealed the previous year, and the scale of this lengthening should be precise and calculated. Therefore, over the 23 years that revelation was descending, we should have 23 average lengths in the verses! Based on this rule, we can have 23 columns where all verses are categorized according to length. Now, how can we know if these categorizations are correct? We know that the "occasion of revelation" (shan al-nuzul) for some Quranic verses is known; some are determined by historical reports stating explicitly which year they were revealed, and some can be determined by their concepts: for example, verses stating laws such as the change of the Qibla, the prohibition of wine, the establishment of the hijab, Zakat, and Khums, and verses speaking of the "Migration" (Hijrah)—the year these laws were established is known.

With extraordinary amazement, we see that these verses—whose year of revelation is known—fall exactly into the same columns that were assumed in this table based on the average length of verses specific to each year! (Take note).

What is even more interesting is the discovery of two or three exceptional cases. This means that Surah Al-Ma'idah is the last large surah revealed, while a few verses from it, according to the formula, should have been revealed in the early years. After researching the texts of commentaries and Islamic narrations, we find the opinions of reliable commentators who stated that these few verses were revealed early on but were placed in Surah Al-Ma'idah by the order of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) during the compilation. In this way, the year of revelation for every verse can be determined by this mathematical criterion, and the Quran can be compiled according to the year of revelation! What orator is there in the world whose year of uttering every sentence can be determined by the length of the phrase? Especially since this text is not a book where its writer sat down and wrote it continuously over a specific period like a scientific or literary work, and especially not a book that its writer composed on a single subject—or even a defined field—but rather various issues that were expressed by the leader gradually according to the needs of society and in response to questions raised, or events and issues that arose in the path of a long struggle, and were then collected and organized [1].

[The Miraculous Rhythm of the Quran and the Interesting Story of Sayyid Qutb]

Rather, according to some commentators, the specific rhythm of the Quranic vocabulary and words is also miraculous in its own kind.

Various interesting evidences have been mentioned for this subject, including the following incident that happened to the famous commentator Sayyid Qutb:

Under the verse in question, he says:

I will not speak of events that happened to others; I will only describe an event that happened to me personally, witnessed by six people (myself and five others).

We were six Muslims traveling across the Atlantic Ocean toward New York on an Egyptian ship. The passengers on the ship were 120 men and women, and no one among the passengers was Muslim except us. On Friday, we thought of performing the Friday prayer in the heart of the ocean on the ship. In addition to performing the religious obligation, we wished to create an Islamic epic in front of a Christian missionary who did not cease his missionary program even inside the ship, especially since he even wanted to proselytize us to Christianity!

The captain of the ship, who was an Englishman, agreed that we could form the congregational prayer on the ship's deck, and the ship's staff, who were all Muslims from Africa, were also allowed to pray with us. They were very happy about this because it was the first time Friday prayer was being performed on the ship! I (Sayyid Qutb) began delivering the Friday sermon and leading the prayer. Interestingly, the non-Muslim passengers had gathered around us and were carefully observing the performance of this Islamic obligation.

After the prayer ended, a large group of them came to us and congratulated us on this success. But among this group was a lady whom we later learned was a Yugoslavian Christian woman who had fled from the "hell" of Tito and his communism!

She was so incredibly moved by our prayer that tears were streaming from her eyes and she was unable to control herself.

She spoke in simple English mixed with intense emotion and a specific humility and reverence. Among her words was: "Tell me, in what language was your priest speaking?" (She thought that a priest or a clergyman must perform the prayer as is the case with Christians, but we soon made her understand that any faithful Muslim can perform this Islamic program). Finally, we told her that we were speaking in the Arabic language.

But she said: "Even though I did not understand a single word of your content, I clearly saw that these words had a strange rhythm. But more importantly, what caught my attention extraordinarily was that within the sermon of your Imam, there were sentences that were distinct from the rest. They had an incredibly effective and deep rhythm, such that they made my body tremble. Surely these sentences were something else; I think your Imam was filled with the Holy Spirit when he uttered these sentences!" We thought for a moment and realized that these sentences were the very verses of the Quran that I was reciting during the sermon and in the prayer. This matter shook us and made us realize that the specific rhythm of the Quran is so effective that it deeply moves even a lady who does not understand a single word of its meaning [1].

[1]: Quoted from Falaq magazine (a university student publication). [1]: Tafsir Fi Zilal, Volume 4, page 422.

In the next verse, it points to one of the fundamental causes of the polytheists' opposition, saying: They did not deny the Quran because of problems or objections, "rather, their denial and rejection was because they had no knowledge of its content" (بَلْ كَذَّبُوا بِمَا لَمْ يُحِيطُوا بِعِلْمِهِ).

In reality, the factor behind the denial was their lack of awareness and their ignorance.

As for what is meant by this sentence regarding which matters they were ignorant of, commentators have given several possibilities, all of which could be intended in the verse, including:

Ignorance of religious knowledge and the Origin and the Resurrection, just as the Quran quotes the polytheists regarding the true Deity (Allah) saying: "Has he turned the gods into one God? This is indeed a strange thing" (Surah Sad, verse 5). Or regarding the Resurrection they said: "When we have become bones and dust, shall we really be raised in a new creation? Has he fabricated a lie against Allah, or is he possessed by madness?" (Sajdah, 10).

In truth, they had no proof for denying the Origin and the Resurrection; only ignorance and unawareness resulting from superstitions and habituation to the religion of their ancestors stood in their way.

Or ignorance of the secrets of the laws.

Ignorance of the meaning of some "ambiguous" (mutashabih) verses.

Ignorance of the meaning of the "disjointed letters" (huruf al-muqatta'ah).

And ignorance of the cautionary lessons that were the ultimate goal of mentioning the history of the predecessors.

The sum of these ignorances and lack of awareness compelled them to deny and reject.

"While the interpretation (ta'wil) and reality of the unknown matters had not yet become clear to them" (وَلَمَّا يَأْتِهِمْ تَأْوِيلُهُ).

"Ta'wil" in its original linguistic sense means "returning something," and therefore, whenever an action or speech reaches its ultimate goal, we say its ta'wil has come. For this reason, stating the main goal of an action, or the true interpretation of a speech, or the interpretation and result and end of a dream, or the realization of the reality of a matter—all of these are called ta'wil (we have spoken about this in detail in Volume 2, page 324).

The Quran then adds that this incorrect method is not limited to the polytheists of the Age of Ignorance, but "the misguided nations of the past were also afflicted with this same trouble; they too, without investigating to recognize realities or waiting for their realization, would deny and reject the truths" (كَذَٰلِكَ كَذَّبَ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ).

In verses 113 and 118 of Surah Al-Baqarah, reference is also made to the state of past nations in this regard.

In reality, the excuse for all of them was their ignorance, and their misfortune lay in the lack of investigation and the failure to seek and recognize realities, whereas reason and logic dictate that a person should never deny what he does not know, but rather should seek and investigate.

At the end of the verse, addressing the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family), it says: "Behold, what was the end of these wrongdoers" (فَانْظُرْ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَاقِبَةُ الظَّالِمِينَ).

Meaning, these people will also meet the same fate.

In the last verse under discussion, it refers to two great groups of polytheists and says: They do not all remain in this state, but "a group of them, in whom the spirit of seeking the truth has not died, will eventually believe in this Quran, while another group will continue to persist in ignorance and stubbornness and will not believe" (وَمِنْهُمْ مَنْ يُؤْمِنُ بِهِ وَمِنْهُمْ مَنْ لَا يُؤْمِنُ بِهِ).

It is clear that this second group consists of corrupt and mischief-making individuals, and for this reason, at the end of the verse, He says: "And your Lord knows best the mischief-makers" (وَرَبُّكَ أَعْلَمُ بِالْمُفْسِدِينَ).

This is an indication that those who do not submit to the truth are individuals who tear apart the fabric of society and play an effective role in corrupting the social order.

Ignorance and Denial

As can be inferred from the above verses, fundamentally a significant part of the opposition, enmity, and struggle against the truth stems from ignorance and lack of knowledge. For this reason, it has been said: "The end of ignorance is disbelief!" The first duty of every truth-seeking human is to adopt silence and anticipation regarding what he does not know, to rise and search, and to examine all aspects of the matter he is ignorant of. Until he finds a definitive reason for negation, he should not resort to negation, just as he should not accept something as proven without definitive evidence.

The late Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan narrated an interesting hadith from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) in this regard, who said: "Allah has given two important lessons to this nation through two verses of the Quran: first, that they should not say except what they know, and second, that they should not deny what they do not know." Then he recited the following two verses: "Was not the covenant of the Book taken from them that they should not say about Allah except the truth?" (أَلَمْ يُؤْخَذْ عَلَيْهِمْ مِيثاقُ الْكِتابِ أَنْ لا يَقُولُوا عَلَى اللَّهِ إِلَّا الْحَقَ) and "Rather, they denied that which they did not encompass in knowledge" (بَلْ كَذَّبُوا بِما لَمْ يُحِيطُوا بِعِلْمِهِ)—meaning the polytheists denied things they were unaware of, whereas ignorance is not a reason for denial.

Ayahs

وَإِن كَذَّبُوكَ فَقُل لِّى عَمَلِى وَلَكُمْ عَمَلُكُمْ ۖ أَنتُم بَرِيٓـُٔونَ مِمَّآ أَعْمَلُ وَأَنَا۠ بَرِىٓءٌ مِّمَّا تَعْمَلُونَ (٤١)
If they deny you, say, ‘My deeds belong to me and your deeds belong to you: you are absolved of what I do and I am absolved of what you do.’ (41)
وَمِنْهُم مَّن يَسْتَمِعُونَ إِلَيْكَ ۚ أَفَأَنتَ تُسْمِعُ ٱلصُّمَّ وَلَوْ كَانُوا۟ لَا يَعْقِلُونَ (٤٢)
There are some of them who prick up their ears at you. But can you make the deaf hear even if they do not exercise their reason? (42)
وَمِنْهُم مَّن يَنظُرُ إِلَيْكَ ۚ أَفَأَنتَ تَهْدِى ٱلْعُمْىَ وَلَوْ كَانُوا۟ لَا يُبْصِرُونَ (٤٣)
There are some of them who observe you. But can you guide the blind even if they do not perceive? (43)
إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَظْلِمُ ٱلنَّاسَ شَيْـًٔا وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱلنَّاسَ أَنفُسَهُمْ يَظْلِمُونَ (٤٤)
Indeed Allah does not wrong people in the least; rather, it is people who wrong themselves. (44)

Commentary

The Blind and the Deaf

In these verses, the discussion regarding the stubborn denial and rejection by the polytheists, which was mentioned in the previous verses, continues:

In the first verse, a new method of confrontation is taught to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, saying: "If they deny you, tell them: my deeds are for me and your deeds are for yourselves" (وَ إِنْ كَذَّبُوكَ فَقُلْ لِي عَمَلِي وَ لَكُمْ عَمَلُكُمْ).

"You are disassociated from what I do, and I am disassociated from what you do" (أَنْتُمْ بَرِيئُونَ مِمَّا أَعْمَلُ وَ أَنَا بَرِي‌ءٌ مِمَّا تَعْمَلُونَ).

This declaration of disavowal and indifference, which is coupled with confidence and firm faith in one's own school of thought, has a specific psychological effect, especially on stubborn deniers. It makes them understand that there is no insistence or compulsion regarding their acceptance; by not submitting to the truth, they only lead themselves toward deprivation and harm only themselves.

Similar expressions have appeared in other verses of the Quran, as we read in Surah Al-Kafirun: لَكُمْ دِينُكُمْ وَ لِيَ دِينِ—"For you is your religion, and for me is my religion."

From this statement, it becomes clear that the content of such verses does not contradict the command for preaching or Jihad against the polytheists in a way that would require us to consider these verses abrogated. Rather, as mentioned, this is a form of logical confrontation through indifference toward stubborn and spiteful individuals.

In the next two verses, it points to the reason for their deviation and lack of submission to the truth, stating: "For the guidance of a human being, correct teachings, soul-stirring and miraculous verses, and clear proofs are not enough; rather, the readiness, potential for acceptance, and worthiness to receive the truth are also necessary. Just as for the growth of greenery and flowers, ready seeds alone are not sufficient—fertile soil is also required." Therefore, it first says: "A group of them listen to you, but it is as if they are deaf" (وَ مِنْهُمْ مَنْ يَستَمِعُونَ إِلَيْكَ) [1]

[1]: In this verse, the phrase "as if they are deaf and do not hear" is implied.

"Despite the fact that they do not have a hearing ear, can you make the deaf hear, even though they do not understand?" (أَ فَأَنْتَ تُسْمِعُ الصُّمَّ وَ لَوْ كانُوا لا يَعْقِلُونَ).

"And another group of them fix their gaze upon you and observe your actions, each of which contains a sign of your truthfulness and the sincerity of your words, yet it is as if they are blind" (وَ مِنْهُمْ مَنْ يَنْظُرُ إِلَيْكَ) [1] "Can you then guide the blind, even though they lack insight?" (أَ فَأَنْتَ تَهْدِي الْعُمْيَ وَ لَوْ كانُوا لا يُبْصِرُونَ).

[1]: Here too, the phrase "as if they are blind and do not see" is implied.

However, know—and they too must know—that this deficiency in thought, lack of insight, blindness to the face of truth, and deafness to the word of God is not something they brought with them from their mothers into this world, nor has Allah done them any injustice. Rather, it was they themselves who, through their wrong actions and enmity and rebellion against the truth, darkened their own souls and disabled their eyes of insight and hearing ears. For "Indeed, Allah does not wrong the people at all, but it is the people who wrong themselves" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ لا يَظْلِمُ النَّاسَ شَيْئاً وَ لكِنَّ النَّاسَ أَنْفُسَهُمْ يَظْلِمُونَ).

Here, two points must be noted

1- The fact that we read in the second verse "they listen to you" and in the third verse "they look toward you" indicates that one group hears your miraculous words and another group observes your miracle-like actions, all of which are clear proofs of the truthfulness of your speech and the legitimacy of your mission. Yet, neither of these two groups benefits from their listening or their looking, because their looking is not for the sake of understanding and comprehension, but rather for criticism, fault-finding, and opposition.

They also do not benefit from their listening because it is not with the intention of perceiving the content of the speech; rather, they want to find a pretext for denial and rejection. We know that a person's intention and motive give shape to his actions and transform their effects.

2- At the end of the second verse, the phrase "وَ لَوْ كانُوا لا يَعْقِلُونَ" (even though they do not understand) is mentioned, and at the end of the third verse, the phrase "وَ لَوْ كانُوا لا يُبْصِرُونَ" (even though they do not see) is mentioned. This points to the fact that listening—meaning the mere acceptance of words and letting them into the ear—is not enough on its own; rather, reflection and contemplation are necessary for a person to benefit from the content. Similarly, looking alone has no effect; rather, insight (understanding the concepts of what one sees) is necessary to reach its depth and be guided.

Ayahs

وَيَوْمَ يَحْشُرُهُمْ كَأَن لَّمْ يَلْبَثُوٓا۟ إِلَّا سَاعَةً مِّنَ ٱلنَّهَارِ يَتَعَارَفُونَ بَيْنَهُمْ ۚ قَدْ خَسِرَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِلِقَآءِ ٱللَّهِ وَمَا كَانُوا۟ مُهْتَدِينَ (٤٥)
On the day He will gather them [it will be] as if they had not remained [in the world] except for an hour of the day getting acquainted with one another. They are certainly losers who deny the encounter with Allah, and they are not guided. (45)
وَإِمَّا نُرِيَنَّكَ بَعْضَ ٱلَّذِى نَعِدُهُمْ أَوْ نَتَوَفَّيَنَّكَ فَإِلَيْنَا مَرْجِعُهُمْ ثُمَّ ٱللَّهُ شَهِيدٌ عَلَىٰ مَا يَفْعَلُونَ (٤٦)
Whether We show you a part of what We promise them, or take you away [before that], [in any case] their return will be to Us and Allah will be witness to what they do. (46)
وَلِكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ رَّسُولٌ ۖ فَإِذَا جَآءَ رَسُولُهُمْ قُضِىَ بَيْنَهُم بِٱلْقِسْطِ وَهُمْ لَا يُظْلَمُونَ (٤٧)
There is an apostle for every nation; so when their apostle comes, judgement is made between them with justice, and they are not wronged. (47)

Commentary

[The Shortness of Worldly Life Compared to the Hereafter]

Following the description of some characteristics of the polytheists in the previous verses, here the Quran points to their painful situation on the Day of Resurrection, stating: "Remember the day when Allah will gather and assemble them all, while they feel as if their entire life in this world was no more than an hour of a single day, just enough for them to see and recognize one another" (وَ يَوْمَ يَحْشُرُهُمْ كَأَنْ لَمْ يَلْبَثُوا إِلَّا ساعَةً مِنَ النَّهارِ يَتَعارَفُونَ بَيْنَهُمْ‌).

This feeling of the brevity of their stay in the world is either because, fundamentally, compared to the eternal life of the Hereafter, it is indeed no more than an hour;

Or it is because this unstable world passed by them with such speed that it was as if it lasted only an hour;

Or perhaps, due to their failure to use their lives correctly, they perceive that their entire lifespan was worth no more than an hour! According to what we have said in the above commentary, the phrase يَتَعارَفُونَ بَيْنَهُمْ‌ (they recognize one another) refers to the duration of their stay in the world; meaning they feel life was so short that it was as if it only lasted long enough for two people to see each other, have a brief introduction, and then part ways.

Another possibility suggested in the interpretation of this verse is that it refers to the feeling of the shortness of the period in Barzakh (the purgatory stage between death and resurrection). That is, they fall into a state similar to sleep during the Barzakh period, such that they do not feel the passage of years, centuries, and ages. Consequently, at the time of Resurrection, they think their stay in Barzakh—which may have been thousands or tens of thousands of years—was no more than an hour.

Evidence for this interpretation is found in verses 55 and 56 of Surah Ar-Rum, which state: وَ يَوْمَ تَقُومُ السَّاعَةُ يُقْسِمُ الْمُجْرِمُونَ ما لَبِثُوا غَيْرَ ساعَةٍ كَذلِكَ كانُوا يُؤْفَكُونَ وَ قالَ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْعِلْمَ وَ الْإِيمانَ لَقَدْ لَبِثْتُمْ فِي كِتابِ اللَّهِ إِلى‌ يَوْمِ الْبَعْثِ فَهذا يَوْمُ الْبَعْثِ وَ لكِنَّكُمْ كُنْتُمْ لا تَعْلَمُونَ‌.

From these two verses, it is understood that a group of criminals, when the Resurrection is established, will swear that their stay in Barzakh was no more than an hour. However, the believers will say to them that it was a long period and now the Resurrection has been established, but you did not know (and we know that Barzakh is not the same for everyone, and we will provide its explanation under the appropriate verses).

According to this interpretation, the meaning of the phrase "يَتَعارَفُونَ بَيْنَهُمْ‌" would be that they feel the time in Barzakh was so short that they have not forgotten anything from the world and recognize each other well.

Or it could mean that they see each other's ugly deeds there and recognize one another's inner reality, which in itself is a great disgrace for them.

Then it adds that on that day, it will be proven to all of them that "those who denied the Day of Resurrection and the meeting with their Lord have indeed lost," and they have lost all the capital of their existence without achieving any result (قَدْ خَسِرَ الَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا بِلِقاءِ اللَّهِ‌).

"And because of this denial, rejection, insistence on sin, and stubbornness, they were not prepared for guidance" (وَ ما كانُوا مُهْتَدِينَ‌).

This is because their hearts were dark and their souls were shrouded in gloom.

In the next verse, as a threat to the disbelievers and a consolation to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, it says: "Whether We show you some of the punishments We have promised them and you witness their torment and punishment during your lifetime, or whether We take you from this world before they meet such a fate, in any case, their return is to Us, and Allah is a witness to the deeds they used to do" (وَ إِمَّا نُرِيَنَّكَ بَعْضَ الَّذِي نَعِدُهُمْ أَوْ نَتَوَفَّيَنَّكَ فَإِلَيْنا مَرْجِعُهُمْ ثُمَّ اللَّهُ شَهِيدٌ عَلى‌ ما يَفْعَلُونَ‌).

In the last verse under discussion, a general law regarding all Prophets, including the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, and all nations, including the nation living in the era of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, is stated: "For every nation, there is a Messenger from Allah" (وَ لِكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ رَسُولٌ‌).

"When their Messenger came and conveyed the message, and a group submitted to the truth and accepted it while another group rose in opposition and denial, Allah judges between them with His justice, and no one is wronged." The believers and the righteous remain, while the wicked and the opponents are either destroyed or condemned to defeat (فَإِذا جاءَ رَسُولُهُمْ قُضِيَ بَيْنَهُمْ بِالْقِسْطِ وَ هُمْ لا يُظْلَمُونَ‌).

Just as it happened regarding the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله and his contemporary nation; the opponents of his call either perished in wars or were eventually defeated and cast out from the social scene, and the believers took the reins of affairs.

Therefore, the judgment and decree referred to in this verse is that very existential (takwini) and executive judgment in this world. As for the possibility suggested by some commentators that it refers to Allah's judgment on the Day of Resurrection, it is contrary to the apparent meaning of the verse.

Ayahs

وَيَقُولُونَ مَتَىٰ هَـٰذَا ٱلْوَعْدُ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ (٤٨)
They say, ‘When will this promise be fulfilled, should you be truthful?’ (48)
قُل لَّآ أَمْلِكُ لِنَفْسِى ضَرًّا وَلَا نَفْعًا إِلَّا مَا شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ ۗ لِكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ أَجَلٌ ۚ إِذَا جَآءَ أَجَلُهُمْ فَلَا يَسْتَـْٔخِرُونَ سَاعَةً ۖ وَلَا يَسْتَقْدِمُونَ (٤٩)
Say, ‘I have no control over any benefit for myself nor [over] any harm except what Allah may wish. There is a time for every nation: when their time comes, they shall not defer it by a single hour nor shall they advance it.’ (49)
قُلْ أَرَءَيْتُمْ إِنْ أَتَىٰكُمْ عَذَابُهُۥ بَيَـٰتًا أَوْ نَهَارًا مَّاذَا يَسْتَعْجِلُ مِنْهُ ٱلْمُجْرِمُونَ (٥٠)
Say, ‘Tell me, should His punishment overtake you by night or day, [you will not be able to avert it]; so what part of it do the guilty seek to hasten?’ (50)
أَثُمَّ إِذَا مَا وَقَعَ ءَامَنتُم بِهِۦٓ ۚ ءَآلْـَٔـٰنَ وَقَدْ كُنتُم بِهِۦ تَسْتَعْجِلُونَ (٥١)
‘What! Do you believe it when it has befallen? Now? While you would seek to hasten it [earlier]?!’ (51)
ثُمَّ قِيلَ لِلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ ذُوقُوا۟ عَذَابَ ٱلْخُلْدِ هَلْ تُجْزَوْنَ إِلَّا بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَكْسِبُونَ (٥٢)
Then it will be said to those who were wrongdoers, ‘Taste the everlasting punishment. Shall you not be requited for what you used to earn?’ (52)

Commentary

Divine Punishment is Not in My Hands

Following the threats mentioned in the previous verses regarding the torment and punishment of the deniers of truth, these verses first quote them as saying out of mockery, ridicule, and denial: "When will this promise [of punishment] be fulfilled, if you are truthful?" (وَيَقُولُونَ مَتَىٰ هَٰذَا الْوَعْدُ إِن كُنتُمْ صَادِقِينَ).

This statement was certainly made by the polytheists of the era of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, as the subsequent verses containing the Prophet's response are a clear witness to this fact.

In any case, by using this expression, they intended to show their complete indifference toward the Prophet's threats and to provide heartening reassurance and peace of mind to those individuals who had become unsettled by these warnings.

In response to this question, Allah commands His Prophet to answer them in several ways:

First, He says: "Tell them: the timing and appointment of this matter is not in my power; I possess no power of benefit or harm for myself—let alone for you—except what Allah wills and intends" (قُل لَّا أَمْلِكُ لِنَفْسِي ضَرًّا وَلَا نَفْعًا إِلَّا مَا شَاءَ اللَّهُ).

I am only His Prophet and messenger; determining the time for the descent of punishment lies solely with Him. When I do not possess the power of benefit or harm for myself, I certainly will not possess it for you.

This sentence, in reality, refers to "Monotheism in Actions" (Tawhid al-Af'ali), signifying that in this world, everything returns to Allah, and every act originates from Him. It is He who, through His wisdom, grants victory to the believers, and it is He who, through His justice, punishes the deviants.

It is obvious that this does not contradict the fact that Allah has granted us strengths and powers through which we possess a portion of our own benefit and harm and can decide our own destiny. In other words, this verse negates "essential" (bi-dhat) ownership, not "conferred" (bi-l-ghayr) ownership, and the phrase "إِلَّا مَا شَاءَ اللَّهُ" (except what Allah wills) is a clear context for this subject.

From this, it becomes clear that the attempt by some biased individuals, such as the author of Tafsir al-Manar, to use this verse to negate the permissibility of seeking intercession (Tawassul) from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله is entirely baseless.

For if the intention of Tawassul were to consider the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله as the possessor of power and the essential owner of benefit and harm, that would certainly be polytheism (shirk), and no Muslim could hold such a belief. However, if this ownership is from Allah and falls under the heading of "إِلَّا مَا شَاءَ اللَّهُ", what is the obstacle? This is the very essence of faith and monotheism.

Yet, due to neglecting this point, he wasted his time and that of his readers with lengthy discussions. Unfortunately, despite all the merits present in his commentary, he has many such errors, the source of all of which can be considered prejudice!

Then the Quran proceeds to another response, saying: "For every nation and community, there is a fixed term and time; when their term comes, they shall not put it back by a single hour, nor shall they put it forward" (لِكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ أَجَلٌ ۚ إِذَا جَاءَ أَجَلُهُمْ فَلَا يَسْتَأْخِرُونَ سَاعَةً ۖ وَلَا يَسْتَقْدِمُونَ).

In other words, no nation or people is immune to Divine punishments—which are the result of their own actions—when they deviate from the path of truth. When they step into such paths and deviate from the definitive laws of creation, they lose their capabilities and eventually fall, just as world history remembers many such examples.

In fact, the Quran warns the polytheists who were hurrying the arrival of Divine punishment not to rush pointlessly; when their time arrives, this punishment will not be delayed or advanced by a single moment.

Additionally, it should be noted that "hour" (sa'ah) sometimes means a moment and sometimes a short period of time, even though today its well-known meaning is one-twenty-fourth of a day and night.

In the next verse, the third response is presented, saying: "Tell them: if the punishment of your Lord comes upon you by night or by day, it is not an impossible thing; and can you repel this sudden punishment from yourselves?" (قُلْ أَرَأَيْتُمُ إِنْ أَتَاكُمْ عَذَابُهُ بَيَاتًا أَوْ نَهَارًا). "In spite of this, what part of it do the criminals and sinners seek to hasten?" (مَّاذَا يَسْتَعْجِلُ مِنْهُ الْمُجْرِمُونَ).

In other words: if these audacious criminals are not certain of the descent of punishment, they should at least consider the possibility that it might come upon them suddenly. What security, guarantee, or proof do they have that the Prophet's threats will never occur? A rational person, faced with such a possibility of harm, should at least observe caution and be wary of it.

Similar meanings have appeared in other verses of the Quran with different expressions, such as: "Do you then feel secure that He will not cause a side of the land to swallow you up, or send against you a storm of stones? Then you would not find for yourselves a protector" (Surah Al-Isra, Verse 68).

And this is exactly what is referred to in the science of Theology (Kalam) and Principles of Jurisprudence (Usul) as the rule of "The Necessity of Repelling Potential Harm." [1]

In the next verse, the fourth response is given to them, which is: "If you imagine that you will believe when the punishment descends and your faith will be accepted, that is a vain thought" (أَثُمَّ إِذَا مَا وَقَعَ آمَنتُم بِهِ). For after the descent of punishment, the doors of repentance are closed to you and faith has the least effect; rather, "it will be said to you: Now you believe? While before you were seeking to hasten the punishment out of mockery and denial?" (آلْآنَ وَقَدْ كُنتُم بِهِ تَسْتَعْجِلُونَ).

This is their worldly punishment, "Then on the Day of Resurrection, it will be said to those who did wrong: Taste the eternal punishment!" (ثُمَّ قِيلَ لِلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا ذُوقُوا عَذَابَ الْخُلْدِ).

"Are you recompensed except for what you used to earn?" (هَلْ تُجْزَوْنَ إِلَّا بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَكْسِبُونَ).

In reality, it is your own deeds that have seized you; they are the very things manifested before you, tormenting you forever.

Key Points

1- As we mentioned under Verse 34 of Surah Al-A'raf, some modern-day cult-founders have argued from verses such as "لِكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ أَجَلٌ..." (which appears twice in the Quran) to negate the finality of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, concluding that every religion and creed eventually ends and gives way to another religion.

However, "Ummah" means a group or community, not a religion, and specifically not the followers of a religion.

The goal of these verses is that the law of life and death is not exclusive to individuals; rather, nations and groups are also subject to this law, and when they place themselves in the path of oppression and corruption, they will become extinct.

Specifically regarding the current discussion, considering the verses before and after it, this truth is clearly proven: the talk is not about the abrogation of a religion, but about the descent of punishment and the destruction of a group and nation, as both the preceding and succeeding verses discuss worldly punishment and retribution.
