2- Given the above verses, the question arises: do Islamic societies also face punishment and torment in this world?

The answer to this question is positive, for we have no evidence that this Ummah is an exception; rather, this law applies to all nations and peoples. As for what we read in some verses of the Quran (Al-Anfal, 33) that Allah will not punish this Ummah, it is conditional upon one of two conditions: either the presence of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله among them or seeking forgiveness and repenting for sins—it is not unconditional.

3- The above verses once again emphasize the truth that upon the descent of punishment, the doors of repentance are closed and regret will be of no use. The reason for this is clear, for repentance in such a state is akin to forced and desperate repentance, and such repentance is worthless.

[1]: From what we have said above, it becomes clear that in the verse above, which contains a conditional clause, the condition is mentioned and its result (jaza) is implied. The phrase "مَّاذَا يَسْتَعْجِلُ مِنْهُ الْمُجْرِمُونَ" is an independent sentence. The implied meaning of the verse is: "Tell me, if His punishment comes to you by night or by day, would you be able to repel it or would you consider it an impossible matter? Since the matter is thus, what part of it do the criminals seek to hasten?" The possibility suggested by some that the phrase "what part... hasten" is the result of the condition is very unlikely (reflect on this).

Ayahs

۞ وَيَسْتَنۢبِـُٔونَكَ أَحَقٌّ هُوَ ۖ قُلْ إِى وَرَبِّىٓ إِنَّهُۥ لَحَقٌّ ۖ وَمَآ أَنتُم بِمُعْجِزِينَ (٥٣)
They inquire of you, ‘Is it true?’ Say, ‘Yes! By my Lord, it is true, and you cannot frustrate [Him].’ (53)
وَلَوْ أَنَّ لِكُلِّ نَفْسٍ ظَلَمَتْ مَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ لَٱفْتَدَتْ بِهِۦ ۗ وَأَسَرُّوا۟ ٱلنَّدَامَةَ لَمَّا رَأَوُا۟ ٱلْعَذَابَ ۖ وَقُضِىَ بَيْنَهُم بِٱلْقِسْطِ ۚ وَهُمْ لَا يُظْلَمُونَ (٥٤)
Were any soul that has done wrong to possess whatever there is on the earth, it would surely offer it for ransom. They will hide their remorse when they sight the punishment; and judgement will be made between them with justice and they will not be wronged. (54)
أَلَآ إِنَّ لِلَّهِ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۗ أَلَآ إِنَّ وَعْدَ ٱللَّهِ حَقٌّ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (٥٥)
Look! To Allah indeed belongs whatever is in the heavens and the earth. Look! Allah’s promise is indeed true; but most of them do not know. (55)
هُوَ يُحْىِۦ وَيُمِيتُ وَإِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ (٥٦)
It is He who gives life and brings death, and to Him you shall be brought back. (56)

Commentary

Do Not Doubt Divine Punishment

In the preceding verses, the discussion pertained to the retribution, punishment, and torment of criminals in this world and the hereafter; the verses under discussion continue this subject.

First, it states: "The criminals and polytheists ask you out of wonder and inquiry whether this promise of Divine punishment in this world and the hereafter is true?"

(وَ يَسْتَنْبِئُونَكَ أَ حَقٌّ هُوَ).

Of course, "truth" (حق) here is not in contrast to "falsehood," but rather the intent is whether this punishment and retribution are a reality and will be realized. This is because "truth" (حق) and "realization" (تحقق) both stem from the same root. Certainly, if "truth" in contrast to "falsehood" is interpreted in its broad sense, it encompasses every existing reality, and its opposite is the non-existent and the void.

Allah commands His Prophet to say in response to this question with the utmost emphasis: "Say: Yes, by my Lord, it is a reality, and there is no doubt or hesitation in it" (قُلْ إِي وَ رَبِّي إِنَّهُ لَحَقٌ‌).

And if you think you can escape from the clutches of Divine punishment, you have made a great mistake, for "you can never prevent it, nor can you render Him powerless with your strength" (وَ ما أَنْتُمْ بِمُعْجِزِينَ‌).

In fact, this sentence, along with the one above it, is of the nature of stating the "requisite" and the "impediment." In the first sentence, He says the punishment of criminals is a reality, and in the second sentence, He adds:

No power can prevent it, exactly like verses 8 and 9 of Surah At-Tur: "Indeed, the punishment of your Lord will occur; of it there is no preventer" (إِنَّ عَذابَ رَبِّكَ لَواقِعٌ ما لَهُ مِنْ دافِعٍ‌).

The emphases observed in the verse are noteworthy: on one hand, the "oath," and on the other, the word "inna" (indeed) and the "lam of emphasis," and on the third, the phrase "وَ ما أَنْتُمْ بِمُعْجِزِينَ‌" (and you cannot escape). All of these emphasize that upon the commission of grave crimes, Divine punishment is certain.

In the next verse, it relies on the magnitude of this punishment, especially on the Day of Resurrection, saying:

"The Divine torment is so terrifying and horrifying that if every soul that has wronged owned all the wealth on earth, they would be prepared to give it all to be ransomed from this severe retribution" (وَ لَوْ أَنَّ لِكُلِّ نَفْسٍ ظَلَمَتْ ما فِي الْأَرْضِ لَافْتَدَتْ بِهِ‌) [1]. In reality, they are ready to give the largest bribe imaginable to escape the clutches of Divine torment, but no one will accept it from them, nor will their punishment be reduced by even a tiny bit.

Specifically, some of these punishments have a spiritual aspect, which is that "they will be filled with regret upon witnessing the Divine torment, yet they will conceal their remorse—which would cause further disgrace before their criminal rivals or followers" (وَ أَسَرُّوا النَّدامَةَ لَمَّا رَأَوُا الْعَذابَ‌).

Then it emphasizes that despite all these circumstances, judgment will be passed between them with justice, and no wrong or oppression shall be done to them (وَ قُضِيَ بَيْنَهُمْ بِالْقِسْطِ وَ هُمْ لا يُظْلَمُونَ‌).

This sentence is an emphasis on the Quran's consistent method regarding punishment and justice; because the emphases of the previous verse regarding punishment might create the illusion for heedless individuals that a matter of "vengeance" is at play. Therefore, the Quran first says:

Judgment between them is done with justice, and again emphasizes that no wrong or oppression will befall them.

Then, so that people do not take these Divine promises and threats as a joke and do not think Allah is incapable of fulfilling them, it adds: "Be aware...

[1]: In fact, the above sentence has an implied phrase, which is "from the terror of the Resurrection and the torment."

...that whatever is in the heavens and the earth belongs to Allah, and His ownership and sovereignty encompass the entire universe, and no one can exit His domain" (أَلا إِنَّ لِلَّهِ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ‌).

And also: "Be aware that the promise of Allah regarding the punishment of criminals is true, even though many people, over whose souls ignorance has cast a dark shadow, do not know this reality" (أَلا إِنَّ وَعْدَ اللَّهِ حَقٌّ وَ لكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لا يَعْلَمُونَ‌).

The last verse under discussion is also a renewed emphasis on this vital issue, saying: "It is Allah who gives life, and it is He who causes death" (هُوَ يُحيِي وَ يُمِيتُ‌). Therefore, He has the power both to cause the death of His servants and to bring them to life for the court of the Resurrection.

"And ultimately, to Him you shall all be returned" (وَ إِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ‌).

And you shall find the reward for all your deeds there.

Two points must be noted here

1- Among the questions raised regarding the above verses is whether the polytheists' question about the reality of Divine retribution was mocking in nature or a truly genuine question? Some have said a genuine question indicates doubt, which is inconsistent with the state of the polytheists.

However, considering that many polytheists were in doubt, and a group of them—despite knowing the truthfulness of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله—had risen in opposition due to stubbornness and prejudice and the like, it becomes clear that a genuine question from them is not at all unlikely.

2- The reality of "remorse" (ندامت) is regret over performing an action whose undesirable effects have become manifest, whether the person can compensate for it or not. The regret of the criminals on the Day of Resurrection is of the second type, and their concealing it is because revealing it would lead to further disgrace.

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ قَدْ جَآءَتْكُم مَّوْعِظَةٌ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ وَشِفَآءٌ لِّمَا فِى ٱلصُّدُورِ وَهُدًى وَرَحْمَةٌ لِّلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (٥٧)
O mankind! There has certainly come to you an advice from your Lord, and a cure for what is in the breasts, and a guidance and mercy for the faithful. (57)
قُلْ بِفَضْلِ ٱللَّهِ وَبِرَحْمَتِهِۦ فَبِذَٰلِكَ فَلْيَفْرَحُوا۟ هُوَ خَيْرٌ مِّمَّا يَجْمَعُونَ (٥٨)
Say, ‘In Allah’s grace and His mercy—let them rejoice in that! It is better than what they amass.’ (58)

Commentary

The Quran, a Great Divine Mercy

In a portion of the preceding verses, discussions regarding the Quran were presented, and some of the oppositions of the polytheists were reflected therein. In these verses, on the same occasion, the Quran is mentioned; first, as a universal and global message, it addresses all human beings, saying:

"O mankind! There has come to you an instruction from your Lord" (يَا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ قَدْ جَاءَتْكُمْ مَوْعِظَةٌ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ).

"And a healing for what is in the breasts" (وَشِفاءٌ لِما فِي الصُّدُورِ).

"And a guidance" (وَهُدًى).

"And a mercy for the believers" (وَرَحْمَةٌ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ).

In this verse, four attributes are mentioned for the Quran. For a better understanding, we must first focus on its vocabulary.

Wa'z (and maw'izah), as stated in "Al-Mufradat," is a prohibition mixed with a threat. However, it seems the meaning of maw'izah is broader than this, as quoted from "Khalil," the famous Arabic scholar, in the same book Al-Mufradat: Maw'izah consists of reminding one of good things in a way that is accompanied by tenderness of heart. In fact, any kind of advice that influences the listener, frightens them from evils, or turns their heart toward virtues is called wa'z or maw'izah.

Of course, the concept of this statement is not that every sermon must be effective, but rather that it is capable of leaving such an effect on prepared hearts.

The meaning of "healing the diseases of the hearts," or in the Quranic expression, "healing for what is in the breasts," refers to spiritual and moral pollutions such as greed, malice, envy, cowardice, polytheism, hypocrisy, and the like, all of which are spiritual and moral diseases.

The meaning of "guidance" is finding the way toward the goal, namely the perfection and progress of man in all positive aspects.

And the meaning of "mercy" refers to the material and spiritual divine blessings that encompass worthy human beings. As we read in the book Al-Mufradat, when "mercy" is attributed to Allah, it means His bestowing of blessings, and when attributed to humans, it means tenderness of heart and affection.

In fact, the above verse describes four stages of the stages of human education and perfection under the shadow of the Quran.

The first stage is the stage of "admonition and advice."

The second stage is the stage of purifying the human soul from various moral vices.

The third stage is the stage of guidance, which takes place after purification.

And the fourth stage is the stage where man has gained the merit to be included in the mercy and blessing of the Lord. Each of these stages follows the other, and it is interesting that all of them take place in the light of the Quran.

It is the Quran that admonishes humans, and it is the Quran that washes the rust of sin and ugly traits from their hearts, and it is the Quran that shines the light of guidance into the hearts, and it is also the Quran that brings down divine blessings upon the individual and society.

Imam Ali عليه السلام, in his comprehensive discourse in Nahj al-Balagha, explained this reality with the most eloquent expression where he says:

...

فَاسْتَشْفُوهُ مِنْ أَدْوَائِكُمْ وَ اسْتَعِينُوا بِهِ عَلَى لَأْوَائِكُمْ فَإِنَّ فِيهِ شِفَاءً مِنْ أَكْبَرِ الدَّاءِ وَ هُوَ الْكُفْرُ وَ النِّفَاقُ وَ الْغَيُّ وَ الضَّلَالُ

"Seek healing from the Quran for your ailments, and seek its assistance for your hardships, for indeed in the Quran is the healing for the greatest diseases, which are disbelief, hypocrisy, rebellion, and misguidance." [^1] This itself shows that the Quran is a prescription for the recovery of the individual and society from various moral and social diseases. This is the very reality that Muslims have consigned to oblivion; instead of benefiting from this healing medicine, they seek their cure in other schools of thought, and have made this great Heavenly Book only a book to be read, not to be contemplated and acted upon.

[^1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 176.

In the next verse, to complete this discussion and emphasize this great divine blessing—namely the Holy Quran, which is superior and higher than any blessing—He says: "Say [O Prophet], in the bounty of Allah and in His endless mercy, and in this great Heavenly Book which encompasses all blessings, in that let them rejoice; it is better than the volume of wealth, the greatness of positions, and the abundance of their kinsmen and tribes" (قُلْ بِفَضْلِ اللَّهِ وَبِرَحْمَتِهِ فَبِذلِكَ فَلْيَفْرَحُوا).

For this capital is better and higher than all that they have gathered for themselves, and none of those are comparable to this (هُوَ خَيْرٌ مِمَّا يَجْمَعُونَ).

Here, two points should be noted

1- Is the heart the center of feelings?

The literal appearance of the first verse under discussion, like some other verses of the Quran, is that the center of moral diseases is the "heart." This statement might initially produce the objection that we know all moral attributes and intellectual and emotional issues return to the human soul; the heart is nothing more than an automatic pump for the transfer of blood and the irrigation and nourishment of the body's cells.

Of course, this is correct—that the heart only has the mission of managing the human body, and psychological issues relate to the human soul. However, there is a subtle point here which, when considered, clarifies the secret of this Quranic expression: in the human body, there are two centers, each of which is a manifestation of a part of human psychological actions. That is, each of these two centers immediately shows a reaction to psychological interactions. One is the "brain" and the other is the "heart."

When we examine intellectual issues in the environment of the soul, their reaction immediately appears in the brain. In other words, the brain is a tool to assist the soul in the matter of thinking; therefore, during thinking, blood circulates faster in the brain, brain cells have more interaction and absorb more nutrients, and send out more waves. But when it comes to emotional issues such as love, affection, decision, will, anger, malice, envy, and forgiveness, a strange activity begins in the human heart. Sometimes it develops a heavy beat, sometimes its beat becomes so weak it seems it wants to stop, and sometimes we feel our heart wants to explode. All these are because of the close connection the heart has with this series of psychological issues.

For this reason, the Holy Quran attributes faith to the heart (وَلَمَّا يَدْخُلِ الْإِيمَانُ فِي قُلُوبِكُمْ) — (Al-Hujurat: 14) or describes ignorance, stubbornness, and lack of submission to the truth as the blindness of the heart (وَلَكِنْ تَعْمَى الْقُلُوبُ الَّتِي فِي الصُّدُورِ — Al-Hajj: 46).

It should not remain unsaid that such expressions are not exclusive to the Quran; in the literature of various languages in the past and today, different manifestations of this issue are seen. We often say to someone we love, "You have a place in our heart," or "Our heart is turned toward you," or "Our heart is a hostage to your love," and the symbol of love is always drawn as a human heart.

All this is because man has always had this feeling: that during love and affection, or malice and envy and the like, he feels a specific effect and impression in his heart. That is, the first spark of these psychological issues, when transferred to the body, appears in the heart.

Of course, in addition to all this, we have previously mentioned that one of the meanings of "heart" (qalb) in linguistics is the intellect and soul of man, and its meaning is not limited to this specific organ inside the chest. This itself can be another interpretation for the verses of the heart, but not for all of them, because in some it is explicitly stated: "the hearts which are in the breasts" (pay close attention).

2- Regarding the difference between "Bounty" (Fadl) and "Mercy" (Rahmah) mentioned in the second verse, there is much discussion among commentators:

A- Some have considered Divine Bounty to refer to outward blessings and Mercy to refer to inward blessings; or in other words, one refers to material blessings and the other to spiritual blessings (repeatedly in the verses of the Quran, the phrase "and seek of His bounty" or "that you may seek of His bounty" has come to mean seeking livelihood and material income).

B- Others have said: Divine Bounty is the beginning of the blessing, and His Mercy is the continuation of the blessing. (Of course, considering that Fadl means bestowing a blessing, and considering that the mention of Mercy after it must be something added to it, the above interpretation is understandable). And the fact that we read in numerous narrations that Divine Bounty refers to the existence of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the blessing of Prophethood, and the Mercy of the Lord refers to the existence of Ali عليه السلام and the blessing of Wilayah (Guardianship), perhaps also points to this interpretation. For the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was the beginning of Islam and Ali عليه السلام became the cause of its survival and continuation (one being the initiating cause and the other the sustaining cause). [^1] Others have considered the possibility that Bounty refers to the blessings of Paradise, and Mercy refers to the forgiveness of sins.

C- The possibility has also been given that Bounty refers to the general blessing of the Lord to friend and foe alike, and Mercy—considering the word "for the believers" mentioned as a qualification for mercy in the previous verse—refers to His special mercy toward people of faith.

[^1]: For awareness of these narrations, refer to Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 2, pp. 307 and 308.

Another interpretation mentioned for these two is that the Bounty of the Lord refers to the matter of faith, and Mercy refers to the Holy Quran, which was spoken of in the verse before it.

Of course, most of these meanings are not in conflict with each other, and it is possible for all of them to be gathered within the comprehensive concept of Bounty and Mercy.

Ayahs

قُلْ أَرَءَيْتُم مَّآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ لَكُم مِّن رِّزْقٍ فَجَعَلْتُم مِّنْهُ حَرَامًا وَحَلَـٰلًا قُلْ ءَآللَّهُ أَذِنَ لَكُمْ ۖ أَمْ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ تَفْتَرُونَ (٥٩)
Say, ‘Have you regarded what Allah has sent down for you of [His] provision, whereupon you have made some of it unlawful and [some] lawful?’ Say, ‘Did Allah give you the sanction [to do so], or do you fabricate a lie against Allah?’ (59)
وَمَا ظَنُّ ٱلَّذِينَ يَفْتَرُونَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ ٱلْكَذِبَ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَذُو فَضْلٍ عَلَى ٱلنَّاسِ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لَا يَشْكُرُونَ (٦٠)
What is the idea of those who fabricate lies against Allah [concerning their situation] on the Day of Resurrection? Indeed Allah is gracious to mankind, but most of them do not give thanks. (60)
وَمَا تَكُونُ فِى شَأْنٍ وَمَا تَتْلُوا۟ مِنْهُ مِن قُرْءَانٍ وَلَا تَعْمَلُونَ مِنْ عَمَلٍ إِلَّا كُنَّا عَلَيْكُمْ شُهُودًا إِذْ تُفِيضُونَ فِيهِ ۚ وَمَا يَعْزُبُ عَن رَّبِّكَ مِن مِّثْقَالِ ذَرَّةٍ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَلَا فِى ٱلسَّمَآءِ وَلَآ أَصْغَرَ مِن ذَٰلِكَ وَلَآ أَكْبَرَ إِلَّا فِى كِتَـٰبٍ مُّبِينٍ (٦١)
You do not engage in any work, neither do you recite any part of the Quran, nor do you perform any deed without Our being witness over you when you are engaged therein. Not an atom’s weight escapes your Lord in the earth or in the sky, nor [is there] anything smaller than that nor bigger, but it is in a manifest Book. (61)

Commentary

He is the Observer Everywhere!

In the preceding verses, the discussion centered on the Quran, divine exhortation, and the guidance and mercy contained within this Heavenly Book. In the verses under discussion, appropriate to that context, the text speaks of the fabricated and superstitious laws and the false decrees of the polytheists. This is because anyone who believes in Allah and knows that all blessings and provisions come from Him must also accept the reality that the declaration of the rulings regarding these blessings—what is permissible and what is impermissible—is in the hands of the Lord. Interfering in this matter without His permission and command is unlawful.

In the first verse under discussion, addressing the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, He says:

"Say: 'Have you seen what provision Allah has sent down for you, and you have made some of it unlawful and some lawful?'" (قُلْ أَرَأَيْتُمْ مَا أَنْزَلَ اللَّهُ لَكُمْ مِنْ رِزْقٍ فَجَعَلْتُمْ مِنْهُ حَرَامًا وَحَلَالًا).

According to your superstitious customs, you have prohibited certain livestock under titles such as Sa’ibah, Bahirah, and Wasilah [^1], as well as a portion of your crops and agricultural produce, thereby depriving yourselves of these pure blessings. Furthermore, it is not up to you to decide what should be lawful or unlawful; this is solely at the disposal of the command of their Lord and Creator.

"Say: 'Has Allah permitted you [to do so], or do you invent lies against Allah?'" (قُلْ آللَّهُ أَذِنَ لَكُمْ أَمْ عَلَى اللَّهِ تَفْتَرُونَ).

[^1]: Bahirah referred to an animal that had given birth several times; Sa’ibah was a camel that had produced ten or twelve offspring; and Wasilah referred to a sheep that had given birth seven times (for further explanation, refer to Volume 5, page 103).

This means the matter is confined to two possibilities with no third option: either it must have occurred with the permission of the Lord, or it is a slander and fabrication. Since the first possibility is ruled out, it can be nothing other than slander and fabrication.

Now that it is certain that through these superstitious and fabricated rulings, they have not only deprived themselves of divine blessings but have also attributed lies and fabrications to the Holy Presence of the Lord, it adds: "What do those who invent lies against Allah think about the Day of Resurrection? Have they secured any guarantee for escape from this painful punishment?" (وَمَا ظَنُّ الَّذِينَ يَفْتَرُونَ عَلَى اللَّهِ الْكَذِبَ يَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ).

However, "Indeed, Allah is full of bounty and extensive mercy toward people." For this reason, He does not immediately punish them for such ugly deeds (إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَذُو فَضْلٍ عَلَى النَّاسِ).

But instead of utilizing this divine respite, taking heed, showing gratitude, and returning to Allah, most of them are heedless "and do not give thanks for this great blessing" (وَلَكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لَا يَشْكُرُونَ).

There is also the possibility in the interpretation of this verse that the lawfulness of all these blessings and provisions (except for harmful and impure things which are excluded) is itself a great divine blessing. Many people, instead of being grateful for this blessing, show ingratitude and deprive themselves of it through superstitious rulings and groundless prohibitions.

To prevent the thought that this divine respite is due to a lack of the Lord's encompassing knowledge over their actions, the final verse under discussion expresses this reality in the most eloquent terms: that He is aware and informed of every atom of existence across the expanse of the heavens and the earth, and the details of the servants' deeds.

He says: "You are not [engaged] in any matter, nor do you recite any portion of the Quran, nor do you [people] do any deed except that We are witnesses over you when you are involved in it" (وَمَا تَكُونُ فِي شَأْنٍ وَمَا تَتْلُو مِنْهُ مِنْ قُرْآنٍ وَلَا تَعْمَلُونَ مِنْ عَمَلٍ إِلَّا كُنَّا عَلَيْكُمْ شُهُودًا إِذْ تُفِيضُونَ فِيهِ). [^1] "Shuhud" is the plural of "Shahid", which originally means presence accompanied by observation with the eye, or the heart and mind. The use of the plural form indicates that not only Allah, but also the angels who are obedient to Him and monitor human actions, are aware of all these deeds and are witnesses and observers.

As we have previously noted, the use of the plural form regarding Allah—despite His Pure Essence being One and Unique in every respect—is to indicate the greatness of His station and that He always has agents who follow His command and are ready and present in obedience to Him. In reality, the speech is not only about Him, but about Him and all His obedient agents.

Then, with further emphasis, the issue of Allah's knowledge of everything is pursued, saying: "Not even the weight of an insignificant atom in the earth or in the heaven is hidden from the knowledge of your Lord, nor anything smaller than that or larger, but it is recorded and registered in a Clear Book [the Preserved Tablet] of Allah's knowledge" (وَمَا يَعْزُبُ عَنْ رَبِّكَ مِنْ مِثْقَالِ ذَرَّةٍ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَلَا فِي السَّمَاءِ وَلَا أَصْغَرَ مِنْ ذَلِكَ وَلَا أَكْبَرَ إِلَّا فِي كِتَابٍ مُبِينٍ).

[^1]: Some have referred the pronoun "minhu" back to Allah, meaning the verses you recite from Allah. However, it appears more likely that this pronoun refers to the "matter" (sha'n) or the "Quran," as many commentators have stated; meaning the verses you recite regarding any important matter, or the verses of the Quran that you recite.

"Ya'zub" is from the root "uzub", which originally means distance and separation from home and family to find pasture for sheep and livestock, and was subsequently used to mean absence and hiddenness in an absolute sense.

"Zarrah" means a very tiny body, and for this reason, tiny ants are also called zarrah (for further explanation, refer to Volume 3, page 389).

"Kitabun Mubin" (Clear Book) refers to the vast knowledge of the Lord, which is sometimes expressed as the "Preserved Tablet" (Lawh-e-Mahfuz) (we have also discussed this in Volume 5, page 271).

Here, several points should be noted

1- Legislation Belongs Exclusively to Allah

The above verses, through concise expressions, prove the reality that the right of legislation belongs exclusively to Allah. Anyone who proceeds with such a task without His permission, leave, and command has committed slander and fabrication against Allah. This is because all provisions and blessings of the world have been sent down from His side, and in truth, the original owner of all of them is Allah. Therefore, it is He who has the right to declare some as "permissible" and some as "impermissible," even though His commands in this regard are in the direction of the interests and perfection of the servants, and He has not the slightest need for this. Regardless, the authority and Legislator is Him.

Unless He delegates the permission for this task, within the limits He deems appropriate, to someone like the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله.

As is understood from numerous narrations, the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله made certain matters obligatory or prohibited, which in the language of narrations is called "Fard al-Nabi" (The Prophet's Decree). Of course, all of this is according to Allah's command and the authorities He delegated to His Prophet.

The phrase "آللَّهُ أَذِنَ لَكُمْ" (Has Allah permitted you) is also evidence that it is possible for Allah to grant such permission to someone.

This discussion pertains to the issue of "Legislative Authority" (Wilayah Tashri'iyyah), which, God willing, we will explain more extensively in another place.

2- The Term "Sending Down" for Provisions

The fact that the term "sending down" (nuzul) is used in the above verses regarding provisions—even though we know it is only rainwater that descends from the sky—is either because these life-giving drops constitute the primary root of all provisions, or because it refers to "rank-based descent" (nuzul-e-maqami) as we have previously mentioned. This expression is also seen in everyday speech: if a command or a gift is given by a high-ranking person to a subordinate, it is said that this program was ordered from "above," or has reached us from above.

3- The Principle of Non-Authority of Conjecture

Scholars of the Principles of Jurisprudence (Usul al-Fiqh) have derived the "principle of the non-authority of conjecture" from the phrase "آللَّهُ أَذِنَ لَكُمْ أَمْ عَلَى اللَّهِ تَفْتَرُونَ". They have stated that this expression shows that no divine ruling can be proven without certainty and conviction; otherwise, it is a prohibited fabrication against Allah (of course, we have discussions regarding this deduction which are mentioned in the studies of Usul al-Fiqh).

4- Legislation Against Allah's Law is the Way of Ignorance

The above verses provide us with another lesson: that legislating in opposition to Allah's law is the custom of the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah), where people gave themselves the right to enact rulings with their inadequate thoughts. However, a true monotheist can never be like this.

The fact that we see in our own era a group who claim faith in Allah and Islam, yet at the same time stretch out a begging hand toward the laws of others—which are non-Islamic laws—or allow themselves to set aside Islamic laws on the pretext that they are not practicable and personally engage in fabricating laws, they too are followers of the customs of Ignorance.

True Islam is not divisible. When we say we are Muslims, we must recognize all its laws. The claim by some that these laws are not all implementable is a baseless notion arising from a type of "Westoxification" (Gharb-zadegi) and loss of self-identity.

Of course, Islam, due to its comprehensiveness, has left our hands open in a series of issues by mentioning general principles, so that we may organize and implement the needs of every age and time through consultation according to those principles.

5- Thoughts, Words, and Deeds

In the final verse under discussion, while referring to the vastness of the Lord's knowledge, He emphasizes three points and says: You are not in any work or state, you do not recite any verse, and you do not perform any deed except that We are witnesses and observers over you.

These three expressions actually refer to the "thoughts," "words," and "deeds" of humans. That is, just as Allah sees our deeds, He hears our words and is aware and informed of our thoughts and intentions; nothing of these is outside the sphere of the Lord's knowledge.

Undoubtedly, intention and spiritual states occupy the first stage, followed by speech, and then action and deed; hence, they are mentioned in this order in the verse.

Additionally, the fact that we see the first and second parts mentioned in the singular form addressing the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, while the third part is in the plural addressing the general body of Muslims, may be because the stage of decision-making in Islamic programs pertains to the leader of the Ummah and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله—just as receiving the verses of the Quran from Allah and reciting them to the people is from his side—but acting upon these programs concerns the general public, and no one is exempt from it.

6- A Great Lesson for All Muslims

In the final verses above, a great lesson is stated for all Muslims—a lesson that can set them on the path of truth and prevent them from deviations and straying; a lesson that, if attended to, builds a righteous and pure society. It is this:

We must pay attention to the reality that every step we take, every word we speak, every thought we nurture in our heads, every direction we look, and every state we are in—not only the Pure Essence of Allah but also His angels are monitoring us and watching us with full attention.

The smallest movement in the expanse of the heavens and the earth is not hidden or concealed from the view of His knowledge. Not only is it not hidden, but all of it is recorded in that Tablet which is Preserved, where error, mistake, and change find no way—in the page of Allah's infinite knowledge, in the minds of the near-stationed angels and the scribes of human deeds, in our records, and in the book of deeds of us all.

It is not without reason that Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام says: Whenever the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله recited this verse, he would weep intensely (كَانَ رَسُولُ اللَّهِ إِذَا قَرَأَ هَذِهِ الْآيَةَ بَكَى بُكَاءً شَدِيدًا) [^1]. When the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, with all that sincerity, servitude, service to creation, and worship of the Creator, is fearful of his work before the knowledge of Allah, the state of ourselves and others is clear.

Ayahs

أَلَآ إِنَّ أَوْلِيَآءَ ٱللَّهِ لَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ (٦٢)
Look! The friends of Allah will indeed have no fear nor will they grieve (62)
ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَكَانُوا۟ يَتَّقُونَ (٦٣)
—those who have faith and are Godwary. (63)
لَهُمُ ٱلْبُشْرَىٰ فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا وَفِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ ۚ لَا تَبْدِيلَ لِكَلِمَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ هُوَ ٱلْفَوْزُ ٱلْعَظِيمُ (٦٤)
For them is good news in the life of this world and in the Hereafter. (There is no altering the words of Allah.) That is the great success. (64)
وَلَا يَحْزُنكَ قَوْلُهُمْ ۘ إِنَّ ٱلْعِزَّةَ لِلَّهِ جَمِيعًا ۚ هُوَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ (٦٥)
Do not grieve at their remarks; indeed all might belongs to Allah; He is the All-hearing, the All-knowing. (65)

Commentary

Peace of the Soul in the Shadow of Faith

Since the previous verses discussed some of the states of the polytheists and faithless individuals, these verses describe the state of the sincere, striving, and God-fearing believers who are their exact opposites. This is done so that through comparison—as is the method of the Quran—light may be distinguished from darkness and felicity from wretchedness.

In the first verse, It says: "Behold! Verily for the friends of Allah there is no fear, nor shall they grieve" (أَلا إِنَّ أَوْلِياءَ اللَّهِ لا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَ لا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ).

To accurately understand the content of this statement, the meaning of "Awliya" (friends/allies) must be well understood.

"Awliya" is the plural of "Wali," which originally stems from the root "Wali-Yali," meaning the absence of an intermediary between two things and their closeness or succession. For this reason, anything that has proximity or nearness to another—whether in terms of place, time, lineage, or status—is called "Wali." The use of this word to mean "guardian," "friend," and the like also originates from this.

Therefore, the "Awliya" of Allah are those between whom and Allah there is no barrier or distance. The veils have been removed from their hearts, and in the light of gnosis, faith, and pure deeds, they see Allah with the eye of the heart such that no doubt or hesitation finds its way into their souls. Because of this intimacy with Allah—who is the Infinite Existence, the Enduring Power, and Absolute Perfection—everything other than Allah appears small, low-value, unstable, and insignificant in their eyes.

One who is familiar with the ocean finds no value in a drop, and one who sees the sun is indifferent to a dim candle.

From here, it becomes clear why they have no fear or grief. Fear usually arises from the possibility of losing blessings one possesses or from dangers that might threaten one in the future. Similarly, grief and sorrow are usually regarding the past and the loss of opportunities or resources one once had. The true "Awliya" and friends of Allah are free from any attachment or captivity to the material world.

"Asceticism" (Zuhd) in its true sense governs their existence; they neither wail and lament over the loss of material possibilities, nor does the fear of the future regarding such matters occupy their thoughts.

Therefore, the "griefs" and "fears" that keep others in a constant state of agitation and anxiety regarding the past and future find no path into their being.

A small vessel of water is agitated by a human's breath, but even storms have little effect on the vastness of the Pacific Ocean—and for this reason, it is called the "Peaceful Ocean" (Pacific). (لِكَيْلا تَأْسَوْا عَلى‌ ما فاتَكُمْ وَ لا تَفْرَحُوا بِما آتاكُمْ) "In order that you may not despair over matters that pass you by, nor exult over favors bestowed upon you" (Al-Hadid: 23).

Neither on the day they possessed things did they become attached to them, nor today when they are separated from them do they grieve. Their souls are greater and their thoughts higher than for such events of the past or future to affect them.

In this way, true security and tranquility reign over their existence, and as the Quran says: أُولئِكَ لَهُمُ الْأَمْنُ "For them there is security" (Al-An'am: 82), or in another expression: أَلا بِذِكْرِ اللَّهِ تَطْمَئِنُّ الْقُلُوبُ "Behold, in the remembrance of Allah do hearts find rest" (Ar-Ra'd: 28).

In short, grief and fear in humans usually stem from a spirit of worldliness. It is very natural for those who are void of this spirit to have no grief or fear.

This was the logical explanation of the matter. Sometimes this same subject is presented in another way that has a mystical (gnostic) form, as follows:

The Awliya of Allah are so drowned in His attributes of Beauty and Majesty, and so lost in the contemplation of His Pure Essence, that they consign everything other than Him to oblivion. It is clear that grief, sorrow, fear, and terror necessarily require the conception of losing something or facing an enemy or a dangerous entity. How can one whose heart has no room for other than Allah, who thinks of none but Him, and accepts none but Him in his soul, possibly have grief, sorrow, fear, or terror?

From what we have said, it also becomes clear that what is meant is material grief and worldly fear. Otherwise, the existence of the friends of Allah is overflowing with fear of Him—fear of failing to perform duties and responsibilities, and grief over missed opportunities for success. This fear and grief have a spiritual dimension and are the cause of the perfection of human existence and his progress, unlike material fear and grief which are causes of degradation and decline.

The Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, says in the famous "Hammam" sermon, where the states of the Awliya of Allah are depicted in the most sublime manner: "Their hearts are grieving, and people are safe from their harm." He also says: "If it were not for the term (death) which Allah has ordained for them, their souls would not remain in their bodies for the blink of an eye, out of longing for the reward and fear of the punishment." [^1] The Holy Quran also says regarding the believers: الَّذِينَ يَخْشَوْنَ رَبَّهُمْ بِالْغَيْبِ وَ هُمْ مِنَ السَّاعَةِ مُشْفِقُونَ "Those who fear their Lord in the unseen and are apprehensive of the Hour" (Al-Anbiya: 49). Therefore, they have a different kind of fear. ^2

[^1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 193 (Subhi al-Salih). ^2: As we are writing these verses, the news of the martyrdom of one of the true Awliya of Allah, the great philosopher, scholar, and mujahid, Mr. Murtaza Motahhari, reached us. It proved once again the reality that to preserve this great Islamic revolution... we must give more blood and sacrifices. But this time they took a man from us whose entire life was in service of knowledge... He was a sincere mujahid and a free believer; he was among those of whom the Quran says they have no fear nor grief, for he fulfilled his mission well and was martyred in its path (May 2, 1979).

There is discussion among commentators as to who is meant by "Awliya of Allah," but the second verse clarifies the matter and ends the discussions, saying: "Those who have believed and used to fear Allah (practiced Taqwa)" (الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ كانُوا يَتَّقُونَ).

It is interesting that "faith" (Iman) is mentioned in the absolute past tense, while "Taqwa" is mentioned in the past continuous tense. This indicates that their faith has reached the level of perfection, but the matter of Taqwa—which is reflected in daily actions, requires new work every day and hour, and has a gradual aspect—has become a permanent program and duty for them.

Yes, these are the ones who, possessing these two fundamental pillars of religion and personality, feel such a tranquility within their souls that none of the storms of life shake them. Rather, according to the theme: "The believer is like a firm mountain that storms cannot move," they show steadfastness like a mountain against the gale of events.

In the third verse, It emphasizes the absence of fear, grief, and terror in the existence of the Awliya of Truth with the phrase: "For them are glad tidings in the life of this world and in the Hereafter" (لَهُمُ الْبُشْرى‌ فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا وَ فِي الْآخِرَةِ).

In this way, not only do they have no fear or grief, but glad tidings, happiness, and joy due to the abundant blessings and endless divine favors are granted to them both in this life and in that life (note that "al-Bushra" is mentioned with the definite article 'al' denoting genus and in an absolute form, encompassing all types of glad tidings).

Again, for emphasis, It adds: "There is no change in the words of Allah," and Allah fulfills His promise to His friends (لا تَبْدِيلَ لِكَلِماتِ اللَّهِ).

"That is the supreme success" for whoever receives it (ذلِكَ هُوَ الْفَوْزُ الْعَظِيمُ).

In the final verse, the address is turned to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, who is the head of the lineage of the Awliya and friends of Allah. By way of comfort and consolation, It says to him: "Let not their speech grieve you"—the discordant words of the opponents and the heedless polytheists (وَ لا يَحْزُنْكَ قَوْلُهُمْ).

"For all honor and power belong to Allah," and against the Divine Will, the enemies can achieve nothing (إِنَّ الْعِزَّةَ لِلَّهِ جَمِيعاً).

He is aware of all their plans; "He is the All-Hearing, the All-Knowing" of their words and the secrets within them (هُوَ السَّمِيعُ الْعَلِيمُ).

Two points must be noted here

1- What is meant by "Glad Tidings" in this verse?

There is discussion among commentators as to what the "glad tidings" are that Allah has bestowed upon His friends in this world and the Hereafter in the above verses.

Some have considered it specific to the glad tidings that the angels give to the believers at the threshold of death and the transition from this world, saying: "And receive the glad tidings of Paradise which you were promised" (Fussilat: 30).

Others consider it an allusion to the Lord's promises of victory, triumph over enemies, and governance on earth as long as they are believers and righteous.

In some narrations, this glad tidings is interpreted as the good dreams that believers see. However, as we said, given the absolute nature of this word and the definite article of genus in "al-Bushra," a broad concept is hidden within it that includes every kind of glad tidings, joy, victory, and success. All that was mentioned above is included within it, and in fact, each refers to a corner of this vast Divine glad tidings.

Perhaps the fact that in some narrations it is interpreted as good dreams and righteous visions is an indication that every kind of glad tidings—even small ones—falls under the concept of "al-Bushra," not that it is limited to them.

In reality, as previously stated, this is the ontological and natural effect of faith and Taqwa, which removes from the human soul and body the types of anxieties and terrors born of doubt and hesitation, as well as those born of sin and various pollutions. How is it possible for someone who has no faith or spiritual support within their soul to still feel tranquility?

He is like an unanchored ship in a stormy sea, where mountain-like waves toss him in every direction at every moment, and whirlpools have opened their mouths to swallow him.

How can someone whose hands are stained with oppression, the shedding of people's blood, and the usurpation of the property and rights of others have peace of mind? Unlike the believers, he does not even have peaceful sleep and often sees terrifying dreams in which he finds himself struggling with enemies. This itself is a proof of the restlessness and agitation of their souls.

It is natural for a criminal—especially one being pursued—to see in the world of sleep terrifying ghosts determined to catch and pursue him, or for the soul of that oppressed victim to cry out from within his subconscious and torture him. Thus, when he wakes up, he says like Yazid: "What business did I have with Hussain?" or like Hajjaj: "What business did I have with Sa'id ibn Jubayr?!"

2- Who are the Awliya of Allah?

Under the above verses, interesting narrations have been transmitted from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليه السلام, some of which we mention:

The Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, recited the verse "Behold! Verily for the friends of Allah..." and then asked his companions: "Do you know who the 'Awliya of Allah' are?" They said: "O Commander of the Faithful, you tell us who they are."

The Imam said: "They are we and our followers. So whoever follows us after us, blessedness (Tuba) is for us, and blessedness is for them even more than for us." They asked: "O Commander of the Faithful, why is their blessedness greater than ours? Are we and they not upon the same matter?" He said: "No, they carry what you were not made to carry, and they endure what you do not endure!" [[^1]]

In the book Kamal al-Din, it is narrated from Abu Basir from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام that he said: "Blessed are the followers (Shi'a) of our Qa'im, who wait for his appearance during his occultation, and are obedient to him upon his appearance. They are the Awliya of Allah, those for whom there is no fear nor shall they grieve." ^2

[^1]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 2, p. 309.

One of the companions of Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام narrates that the Imam عليه السلام said: "The followers of this school see things in the final moments of their lives that delight their eyes."

The narrator says: "I insisted, 'What do they see?' and I repeated this more than ten times, but each time the Imam contented himself with only this phrase: 'They see'!"

At the end of the gathering, he turned to me, called me, and said: "It seems you insist on knowing what they see?" I said: "Yes, certainly!"... then I wept.

The Imam took pity on my state and said: "They see those two." I insisted: "Which two?" He said: "The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and Ali عليه السلام. No person of faith closes his eyes to the world except that he will see these two great personages giving him glad tidings." Then he said: "Allah has mentioned this in the Quran." They asked: "Where and in which Surah?" He said: "In Surah Yunus, where It says: 'Those who have believed and used to fear Allah, for them are glad tidings in the life of this world and in the Hereafter.'" [^1] We have other narrations with the same theme.

It is clear that these narrations refer to a part of the glad tidings for people of faith and Taqwa, not all of them. It is also clear that this observation is not an observation of the material body, but rather an observation of the Barzakh (purgatorial) body with Barzakh vision. For we know that in the world of Barzakh—which is the interval between this world and the abode of the Hereafter—the human soul remains with its Barzakh body.

[^1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 2, p. 310 (summarized).

Ayahs

أَلَآ إِنَّ لِلَّهِ مَن فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَن فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۗ وَمَا يَتَّبِعُ ٱلَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ شُرَكَآءَ ۚ إِن يَتَّبِعُونَ إِلَّا ٱلظَّنَّ وَإِنْ هُمْ إِلَّا يَخْرُصُونَ (٦٦)
Look! To Allah indeed belongs whoever is in the heavens and whoever is on the earth. What do they pursue who invoke partners besides Allah? They merely follow conjectures and they just make surmises. (66)
هُوَ ٱلَّذِى جَعَلَ لَكُمُ ٱلَّيْلَ لِتَسْكُنُوا۟ فِيهِ وَٱلنَّهَارَ مُبْصِرًا ۚ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّقَوْمٍ يَسْمَعُونَ (٦٧)
It is He who made the night for you, that you may rest in it, and the day to provide visibility. There are indeed signs in that for people who listen. (67)

Commentary

Portions of the Signs of His Greatness

The above verses return once again to the issue of Monotheism (Tawhid) and polytheism (Shirk), which is one of the most important topics of Islam and the themes of this Surah. It brings the polytheists to trial and proves their utter helplessness.

First, it says: "Behold! Verily to Allah belong all those who are in the heavens and those who are on the earth" (أَلا إِنَّ لِلَّهِ مَنْ فِي السَّماواتِ وَ مَنْ فِي الْأَرْضِ‌).

When persons are His property and belong to Him, the objects in this world belong to Him by greater reason (a fortiori). Therefore, He is the Owner of the entire universe; given this, how is it possible for His subjects to be His partners?! Then it adds: "Those who call upon partners besides Allah do not follow any [logical] proof or reason, and they have no evidence or witness for their claims" (وَ ما يَتَّبِعُ الَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ شُرَكاءَ).

"They follow nothing but baseless and unfounded conjectures and fancies" (إِنْ يَتَّبِعُونَ إِلَّا الظَّنَ‌).

"Rather, they only speak by way of guesswork and estimation, and they lie!" (وَ إِنْ هُمْ إِلَّا يَخْرُصُونَ‌).

The word "Khars" (خرص) in the lexicon means both "lying" and "guesswork or estimation." Originally—as Raghib stated in Al-Mufradat—it meant the harvesting of fruit, and subsequently, it was applied to the calculation and estimation of fruit on trees. Since guesswork and estimation sometimes turn out to be incorrect, this root has also come to mean "lying."

Fundamentally, it is the nature of following baseless fancies and conjectures that eventually drags a person into the valley of lies.

Those who made idols partners to Allah relied on nothing more than illusions—illusions that are difficult for us to even imagine today: how could a human being craft lifeless shapes and statues and then consider his own creation to be his lord and master? To entrust his destiny to it and seek the solution to his problems from it?! Can this be anything other than a lie and fabrication?

In fact, with a bit of reflection, one can derive a general law from this verse: anyone who follows baseless conjectures will eventually be led to lying. Truth and honesty are built upon certainty and conviction, while lying is based on estimations, fancies, and rumors!

Then, to complete this discussion and demonstrate the path to knowing God, and to distance [the people] from polytheism and idol worship, it points to a corner of the Divine blessings placed within the system of creation, which are signs of the greatness, power, and wisdom of "Allah," saying:

"He is the One Who made the night for you so that you may find rest in it" (هُوَ الَّذِي جَعَلَ لَكُمُ اللَّيْلَ لِتَسْكُنُوا فِيهِ‌):

"And the day to be sight-giving [bright]" (وَ النَّهارَ مُبْصِراً).

This system of light and darkness, which has been emphasized many times in the verses of the Quran, is an amazing and fruitful system. On one hand, by the shining of light for a specific duration, it illuminates the scene of human life, creating movement and stimulating effort. On the other hand, with the dark and soothing veils of the night, it prepares the tired soul and body for work and movement once again.

Indeed, "in this calculated system, there are signs and tokens of the Creator's ability, but for those who have a listening ear and hear the truths" (إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآياتٍ لِقَوْمٍ يَسْمَعُونَ‌).

Those who hear and understand, and those who, after understanding the truth, put it into practice.

Key Points

  1. The tranquility and rest (Sukun) which is designated as the purpose of the creation of the night is an established scientific reality that modern science has proven. The veils of darkness are not only a compulsory means to cease daily activities, but they also have a direct effect on the nervous system and muscles of humans and other living beings, plunging them into a state of rest, sleep, and stillness. How ignorant are those people who keep the night alive with hedonism and spend the day—especially the invigorating mornings—in sleep, and for this reason, they always possess unbalanced and troubled nerves.

  2. Considering that the root "Ibsar" (ابصار) means "vision," the meaning of the phrase "وَ النَّهارَ مُبْصِراً" would be that God "made the day seeing," whereas the day is "sight-giving" (enabling vision) rather than "seeing" itself. This is a beautiful metaphor and trope of the type where the cause is described with the attributes of the effect. Similarly, regarding the night, they say "Laylun Na'im" (ليل نائم), meaning "a night that has gone to sleep," while the night does not sleep; rather, the night causes people to sleep.

  3. The above verses once again condemn and reject conjecture (Zann). However, considering that the discourse is about the superstitious and baseless fancies of the idolaters, "Zann" here does not refer to calculated rational conjectures which are authoritative (Hujjah) in certain cases—such as the testimony of witnesses, the apparent meaning of words, confessions, and correspondences. Therefore, the above verses cannot be used as evidence for the lack of authority of [rational] conjecture.

Ayahs

قَالُوا۟ ٱتَّخَذَ ٱللَّهُ وَلَدًا ۗ سُبْحَـٰنَهُۥ ۖ هُوَ ٱلْغَنِىُّ ۖ لَهُۥ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ إِنْ عِندَكُم مِّن سُلْطَـٰنٍۭ بِهَـٰذَآ ۚ أَتَقُولُونَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ مَا لَا تَعْلَمُونَ (٦٨)
They say, ‘Allah has taken a son!’ Immaculate is He! He is the All-sufficient. To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth. You have no authority for this [statement]. Do you attribute to Allah what you do not know? (68)
قُلْ إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يَفْتَرُونَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ ٱلْكَذِبَ لَا يُفْلِحُونَ (٦٩)
Say, ‘Indeed those who fabricate lies against Allah will not be felicitous.’ (69)
مَتَـٰعٌ فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا ثُمَّ إِلَيْنَا مَرْجِعُهُمْ ثُمَّ نُذِيقُهُمُ ٱلْعَذَابَ ٱلشَّدِيدَ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَكْفُرُونَ (٧٠)
An enjoyment in this world; then to Us shall be their return, then We shall make them taste the severe punishment because of what they used to defy. (70)

Commentary

These verses continue the discussion with the polytheists, recounting one of their lies and slanders against the sacred Essence of Allah. First, it says:

"They said: 'Allah has taken a son!'" (قالُوا اتَّخَذَ اللَّهُ وَلَداً).

This claim was made primarily by the Christians regarding Prophet Jesus (peace be upon him), then by the idolaters of the Age of Ignorance regarding the angels—whom they imagined to be the daughters of God—and by the Jews regarding "Ezra" ('Uzayr).

The Quran responds to them in two ways:

First: "He is Glory-bound (Free from any defect or deficiency); He is the Self-Sufficient (سُبْحانَهُ هُوَ الْغَنِيُ‌)."

This is an allusion to the fact that the need for a child arises either from a physical need for strength and assistance, or from a psychological and emotional need. Since Allah is free from every defect, deficiency, and situational lack, and His Pure Essence is absolute Richness and Self-Sufficiency, it is impossible for Him to choose a child for Himself.

"To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth" (لَهُ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَ ما فِي الْأَرْضِ‌).

With this being the case, what meaning does it have for Him to choose a child to provide Him with comfort or to assist Him?! It is interesting that the term used here is "attakhadha" (He chose/adopted), which indicates they believed that a child was not born of Allah, but rather that Allah had selected certain beings as His children—much like those who cannot have children and choose a child from an orphanage or the like. In any case, these narrow-minded ignorant ones were trapped in the error of comparing the Creator to the created, measuring the Self-Sufficient Essence of Allah against their own limited and needy existence.

The second response the Quran gives them is: Anyone who makes a claim must provide proof for their assertion. "Do you have any authority (proof) for this?

No, you have no proof with you for this claim" (إِنْ عِنْدَكُمْ مِنْ سُلْطانٍ بِهذا).

Despite this, "Do you say against Allah that which you do not know?" (أَ تَقُولُونَ عَلَى اللَّهِ ما لا تَعْلَمُونَ‌).

That is to say, even if you do not accept the first clear logical proof, you ultimately cannot deny the fact that your statement is a slander and a saying without knowledge.

In the next verse, it recounts the wretched end of forging lies and slanders against Allah. Addressing His Prophet, He says: "Say: 'Indeed, those who invent lies against Allah will never succeed'" (قُلْ إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَفْتَرُونَ عَلَى اللَّهِ الْكَذِبَ لا يُفْلِحُونَ‌).

Supposing they are able to attain some worldly wealth and status for a few days through their lies and slanders, "This is but a brief enjoyment in this world; then to Us is their return, then We will make them taste the severe punishment because they used to disbelieve" (مَتاعٌ فِي الدُّنْيا ثُمَّ إِلَيْنا مَرْجِعُهُمْ ثُمَّ نُذِيقُهُمُ الْعَذابَ الشَّدِيدَ بِما كانُوا يَكْفُرُونَ‌).

In reality, this verse and the preceding one describe two types of punishment for these liars who falsely attribute the taking of a son to Allah. One is that this lie and slander will never be the cause of their prosperity and salvation, and will never lead them to their goal; rather, they will wander lost in the wilderness, and misery and failure will overtake them.

The other is that even if they deceive people for a few days with these words and gain some benefit from the system of idolatry, this enjoyment and profit has no permanence or stability, and the eternal divine punishment awaits them.

In the above verses, careful attention must be paid to the meaning of several words:

  1. The word "Sultan" here means "proof" or "evidence." This word is more meaningful and expressive than the word dalil (proof), because dalil means a guide, but sultan means something that gives a person mastery over the opposing party. It is appropriate for contexts of debate, argument, and discussion, referring to a "crushing proof."

  2. "Mata'" means something from which a person derives benefit, and its concept is very broad, encompassing all means of living and material blessings. Raghib says in the book Al-Mufradat: "Everything that is benefited from in some way is called mata' or mut'ah."

  3. The expression "Nudhiquhum" (We make them taste), which is used regarding divine punishment, indicates that this punishment reaches them in such a way that it is as if they taste it with their tongue and mouth. This expression is far more expressive than merely seeing or even touching the punishment.

Ayahs

۞ وَٱتْلُ عَلَيْهِمْ نَبَأَ نُوحٍ إِذْ قَالَ لِقَوْمِهِۦ يَـٰقَوْمِ إِن كَانَ كَبُرَ عَلَيْكُم مَّقَامِى وَتَذْكِيرِى بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ فَعَلَى ٱللَّهِ تَوَكَّلْتُ فَأَجْمِعُوٓا۟ أَمْرَكُمْ وَشُرَكَآءَكُمْ ثُمَّ لَا يَكُنْ أَمْرُكُمْ عَلَيْكُمْ غُمَّةً ثُمَّ ٱقْضُوٓا۟ إِلَىَّ وَلَا تُنظِرُونِ (٧١)
Relate to them the account of Noah when he said to his people, ‘O my people! If my stay [among you] be hard on you and [also] my reminding you of Allah’s signs, [for my part] I have put my trust in Allah. So conspire together, along with your partners, leaving nothing vague in your plan, then carry it out against me without giving me any respite. (71)
فَإِن تَوَلَّيْتُمْ فَمَا سَأَلْتُكُم مِّنْ أَجْرٍ ۖ إِنْ أَجْرِىَ إِلَّا عَلَى ٱللَّهِ ۖ وَأُمِرْتُ أَنْ أَكُونَ مِنَ ٱلْمُسْلِمِينَ (٧٢)
If you turn your back [on me], I do not ask any reward from you; my reward lies only with Allah and I have been commanded to be of those who submit [to Allah].’ (72)
فَكَذَّبُوهُ فَنَجَّيْنَـٰهُ وَمَن مَّعَهُۥ فِى ٱلْفُلْكِ وَجَعَلْنَـٰهُمْ خَلَـٰٓئِفَ وَأَغْرَقْنَا ٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا ۖ فَٱنظُرْ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلْمُنذَرِينَ (٧٣)
But they impugned him. So We delivered him and those who were with him in the ark and We made them the successors, and We drowned those who denied Our signs. So observe how was the fate of those who were warned! (73)

Commentary

A Glimpse into the Struggles of Noah

The above verses mark the beginning of an exposition of a portion of the history of the Prophets and the fate of past nations, intended to awaken the polytheists and opposing groups. Allah commands His Prophet to complement his discourse with the polytheists by detailing the cautionary history of those who came before.

First, He addresses the story of "Noah," saying:

"Recite to them the news of Noah, when he said to his people: 'O my people, if my stay among you and my reminding you of the signs of Allah is hard and unbearable for you, then do whatever is in your power and do not hesitate'" (وَ اتْلُ عَلَيْهِمْ نَبَأَ نُوحٍ إِذْ قالَ لِقَوْمِهِ يا قَوْمِ إِنْ كانَ كَبُرَ عَلَيْكُمْ مَقامِي وَ تَذْكِيرِي بِآياتِ اللَّهِ‌).

"For I have relied upon Allah," and for this reason, I do not fear or dread anyone besides Him! (فَعَلَى اللَّهِ تَوَكَّلْتُ‌).

Then he emphasizes: "Now that it is so, gather your resolve and call upon your idols to assist you in your decision" (فَأَجْمِعُوا أَمْرَكُمْ وَ شُرَكاءَكُمْ‌).

"So that nothing remains hidden from you, and no grief or anxiety remains in your hearts regarding this matter"; rather, make your decision about me with ultimate clarity (ثُمَّ لا يَكُنْ أَمْرُكُمْ عَلَيْكُمْ غُمَّةً).

"Ghummah" (غمة) is derived from the root ghamm, meaning to cover something. The reason sorrow is called ghamm is that it covers the human heart.

Then he says: If you can, "Arise and end my life, and do not give me a moment's respite" (ثُمَّ اقْضُوا إِلَيَّ وَ لا تُنْظِرُونِ‌) [1]. Noah, the great messenger of the Lord, with the decisiveness characteristic of the Ulul-'Azm (Arch-Prophets), stands with ultimate courage and bravery against powerful and stubborn enemies despite his few and limited followers. He mocks their power, shows his indifference to their plans, thoughts, and idols, and thereby delivers a heavy psychological blow to their mindset.

Considering that these verses were revealed in Mecca at a time when the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was living in conditions similar to those of Noah, and the believers were in the minority, the Quran intends to give the Prophet the same instruction: that he must not give importance to the enemy's power. Instead, he should move forward with decisiveness and bravery, for his support is Allah, and no force has the power to resist His might.

Although some commentators have considered this expression by Noah—or similar ones in the history of other Prophets—as a type of miracle, because they threatened the enemy with defeat despite lacking outward means and foretold their own ultimate victory, and this is not possible except through miracle—nevertheless, this is a lesson for all Islamic leaders never to fear the multitude of enemies. Rather, by relying and putting their trust in the Lord and with the utmost decisiveness, they should challenge them to the field and belittle their power. This will be a significant factor in strengthening the morale of the followers and breaking the spirit of the enemies.

[1]: There is a discussion among commentators regarding what the "result" (jaza) of the "condition" (shart) is in the phrase "إِنْ كانَ كَبُرَ عَلَيْكُمْ‌". Among all the possibilities mentioned, two seem more likely: First, that "فَأَجْمِعُوا أَمْرَكُمْ" is the result of the condition, and the phrase "فَعَلَى اللَّهِ تَوَكَّلْتُ‌" is a parenthetical statement separating the condition and its result. Second, that the result is omitted and the subsequent sentences indicate it, the estimation being: "Do what you wish, for I rely upon Allah." In fact, the phrase "فَعَلَى اللَّهِ تَوَكَّلْتُ‌" is of the type of a cause that has replaced the effect. And "شركائكم" in the following phrase refers to the idols, and the waw before it means "with" (take note).

In the next verse, another statement from Noah is quoted to prove his truthfulness, where he says: "If you turn away from my call, I suffer no loss, for I have not asked you for any reward" (فَإِنْ تَوَلَّيْتُمْ فَما سَأَلْتُكُمْ مِنْ أَجْرٍ) [1]. "For my reward is only with Allah" (إِنْ أَجْرِيَ إِلَّا عَلَى اللَّهِ‌). I work for Him and seek reward only from Him.

"And I have been commanded to be only of those who submit to the command of Allah" (وَ أُمِرْتُ أَنْ أَكُونَ مِنَ الْمُسْلِمِينَ‌).

Noah's statement, "I ask no reward from you," is another lesson for divine leaders: that in their calling and preaching, they should have no expectation of material or spiritual reward from the people. Such expectations create a kind of dependency that blocks their explicit preaching and free activities, and naturally, their preaching and call will become less effective. For this reason, the correct way to call to Islam and preach it is for Islamic preachers to rely only on the public treasury (Bayt al-Mal) for their livelihood, not to be in need of the people!

In the final verse under discussion, the end of the affair of Noah's enemies and the truth of his prediction is stated as follows:

"They denied him, but We saved him and all those who were with him in the ship" (فَكَذَّبُوهُ فَنَجَّيْناهُ وَ مَنْ مَعَهُ فِي الْفُلْكِ‌) [1]. Not only did We save them, but "We made them successors of the oppressive people" (وَ جَعَلْناهُمْ خَلائِفَ‌).

"And We drowned those who denied Our signs" (وَ أَغْرَقْنَا الَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا بِآياتِنا).

And at the end, addressing the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, it says: "Now behold what was the end of those who were warned but did not take the divine threats seriously" (فَانْظُرْ كَيْفَ كانَ عاقِبَةُ الْمُنْذَرِينَ‌).

[1]: The answer to this condition is also omitted, and the estimation is: "If you turn away, you do not harm me"—or "If you turn away, then you are on your own."

Ayah

ثُمَّ بَعَثْنَا مِنۢ بَعْدِهِۦ رُسُلًا إِلَىٰ قَوْمِهِمْ فَجَآءُوهُم بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ فَمَا كَانُوا۟ لِيُؤْمِنُوا۟ بِمَا كَذَّبُوا۟ بِهِۦ مِن قَبْلُ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ نَطْبَعُ عَلَىٰ قُلُوبِ ٱلْمُعْتَدِينَ (٧٤)
Then after him We sent [other] apostles to their people. They brought them manifest proofs, but they would not believe something they had denied before. Thus do We seal the hearts of the transgressors. (74)

Commentary

The Messengers After Noah

After concluding the brief discussion regarding the story of Noah, the text refers to other Prophets who came after Noah and before Moses (such as Abraham, Hud, Salih, Lot, and Joseph) to guide the people, stating:

"Then, after him, We sent messengers to their people" (ثُمَّ بَعَثْنا مِنْ بَعْدِهِ رُسُلًا إِلى‌ قَوْمِهِمْ‌).

"They came to their people with clear and manifest proofs," and like Noah, they were equipped with the weapons of logic, miracles, and constructive programs (فَجاؤُهُمْ بِالْبَيِّناتِ‌).

"But those who followed the path of obstinacy and stubbornness, and had previously risen to deny the former Prophets, also denied these Prophets and did not believe in them" (فَما كانُوا لِيُؤْمِنُوا بِما كَذَّبُوا بِهِ مِنْ قَبْلُ‌).

This was because, due to their rebellion, sin, and enmity toward the truth, a veil had fallen over their hearts; indeed, "Thus do We seal the hearts of the transgressors" (كَذلِكَ نَطْبَعُ عَلى‌ قُلُوبِ الْمُعْتَدِينَ‌).

Two points must be noted here

1- The sentence "فَما كانُوا لِيُؤْمِنُوا بِما كَذَّبُوا بِهِ مِنْ قَبْلُ‌" (But they were not to believe in what they had denied before) indicates that there were groups among the nations who would not bow in submission to the call of any Prophet or reformer. They remained steadfast in their initial stance, and the repeated calls of the Prophets had not the slightest effect on them. Therefore, the aforementioned sentence refers to a group that faced the calls of different Prophets at two different times (as the literal meaning of the sentence suggests that the referent of all the pronouns is the same).

Another possibility has also been suggested for the meaning of the verse: that it refers to two different groups. One group existed during the time of Noah and denied his call, and the group that came after them followed their path by rejecting and denying the Prophets.

Accordingly, the meaning of the sentence would be: "The transgressors of other nations refused to believe in that which the previous nations had refused." Of course, considering that the opponents of Noah's call perished during the Flood, this possibility in the interpretation of the verse gains strength. However, in any case, it necessitates making a distinction between the referents of the pronouns in the sentence (the plural "waw" in kanu, li-yu'minu, and kadhdhabu).

2- It is clear that the sentence "كَذلِكَ نَطْبَعُ عَلى‌ قُلُوبِ الْمُعْتَدِينَ‌" (Thus do We seal the hearts of the transgressors) is not evidence of predestination (Jabr), and its explanation is hidden within itself. This is because it says We seal the hearts of the "transgressors" so that they do not perceive anything. Therefore, repeated transgressions against the boundaries of Divine laws and truth are first committed by them, which gradually affects their hearts and strips them of the power to discern the truth. Their condition reaches a point where rebellion, disobedience, and sin become their second nature, such that they do not submit to any truth [1].

[^1]: "Fulk" means ship, and its difference from "Safinah" is that "Safinah" is singular and its plural is "Safa'in," whereas "Fulk" is used for both singular and plural.

[^1]: We have discussed this matter in detail in Volume 1, under verse 7 of Surah Al-Baqarah, page 50.

Ayahs

ثُمَّ بَعَثْنَا مِنۢ بَعْدِهِم مُّوسَىٰ وَهَـٰرُونَ إِلَىٰ فِرْعَوْنَ وَمَلَإِي۟هِۦ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا فَٱسْتَكْبَرُوا۟ وَكَانُوا۟ قَوْمًا مُّجْرِمِينَ (٧٥)
Then, after them, We sent Moses and Aaron to Pharaoh and his elite with Our signs, but they acted arrogantly and they were a guilty lot. (75)
فَلَمَّا جَآءَهُمُ ٱلْحَقُّ مِنْ عِندِنَا قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّ هَـٰذَا لَسِحْرٌ مُّبِينٌ (٧٦)
When the truth from Us came to them, they said, ‘This is indeed plain magic!’ (76)
قَالَ مُوسَىٰٓ أَتَقُولُونَ لِلْحَقِّ لَمَّا جَآءَكُمْ ۖ أَسِحْرٌ هَـٰذَا وَلَا يُفْلِحُ ٱلسَّـٰحِرُونَ (٧٧)
Moses said, ‘Do you say of the truth when it comes to you [that it is magic]? Is this magic? Magicians do not find salvation.’ (77)
قَالُوٓا۟ أَجِئْتَنَا لِتَلْفِتَنَا عَمَّا وَجَدْنَا عَلَيْهِ ءَابَآءَنَا وَتَكُونَ لَكُمَا ٱلْكِبْرِيَآءُ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا نَحْنُ لَكُمَا بِمُؤْمِنِينَ (٧٨)
They said, ‘Have you come to us to turn us away from what we found our fathers following, so that supremacy may be yours in the land? We will not believe in the two of you.’ (78)

Commentary

A Portion of the Struggles of Moses and Aaron

In the narration of the stories of the Prophets and past nations as living models and examples, the discourse first addressed Noah, and then the Prophets after Noah. Now, in these verses, it is the turn of Moses and Aaron and their persistent struggles against Pharaoh and his people.

In the first verse, it says: "Then, after those Messengers, We sent Moses and Aaron to Pharaoh and his chiefs with Our signs and miracles" (ثُمَّ بَعَثْنا مِنْ بَعْدِهِمْ مُوسى‌ وَ هارُونَ إِلى‌ فِرْعَوْنَ وَ مَلَائِهِ بِآياتِنا) [^1]. As we have previously mentioned, "Mala" (chiefs) refers to the flamboyant aristocrats whose appearance fills the eye and whose presence in society is seen everywhere; in verses such as the one under discussion, it usually means the inner circle, entourage, and advisors.

If we see that the mission of Moses is mentioned only toward Pharaoh and his chiefs—while Moses was sent to all the Egyptians and the Children of Israel—it is because the pulse of societies is in the hands of the ruling classes and their cliques. Therefore, any reformist or revolutionary program must first target them, just as verse 12 of Surah At-Tawbah says: فَقاتِلُوا أَئِمَّةَ الْكُفْرِ ("Fight the leaders of disbelief").

However, Pharaoh and his people refused to accept the call of Moses and Aaron, and they acted arrogantly instead of submitting to the Truth (فَاسْتَكْبَرُوا).

Due to their pride, self-superiority, and lack of a spirit of humility, they ignored the clear realities in the call of Moses. For this reason, this criminal and sinful people continued in their crimes and sins (وَ كانُوا قَوْماً مُجْرِمِينَ‌).

The subsequent verses speak of the multi-stage struggle of the Pharaohs against Moses and his brother Aaron. The first stage consisted of denial, rejection, slander, and accusing them of ill intentions, overturning the traditions of the ancestors, and disrupting the social order. As the Quran says:

"When the Truth came to them from Us (even though they recognized it by its face), they said: 'This is indeed evident magic!'" (فَلَمَّا جاءَهُمُ الْحَقُّ مِنْ عِنْدِنا قالُوا إِنَّ هذا لَسِحْرٌ مُبِينٌ‌).

The extraordinary attraction and pull of Moses' call on one hand, his striking miracles on the other, and his increasing and dazzling influence on a third, caused the Pharaohs to think of a remedy. They found no better label than to call him a magician and his work magic. This accusation is something seen throughout the history of the Prophets, especially regarding the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله).

However, Moses (عليه السلام) rose to defend himself, pulling back the curtains with two arguments to expose their lies and slander. First, "He said to them: 'Do you say [this] of the Truth when it has come to you? Is this magic? While it bears no resemblance to magic?'" (قالَ مُوسى‌ أَ تَقُولُونَ لِلْحَقِّ لَمَّا جاءَكُمْ أَ سِحْرٌ هذا) [^1]. That is to say, it is true that both "magic" and "miracles" have influence, and both "truth" and "falsehood" may affect people, but the face of magic—which is a false matter—is completely distinct from the face of a miracle—which is the Truth. The influence of Prophets is not comparable to the influence of magicians. The works of magicians are aimless, limited, and of little value, whereas the miracles of Prophets have clear reformist, revolutionary, and educational goals, and are presented in an extensive and unlimited form.

[^1]: The "signs" refer to the numerous and famous miracles of Moses that he possessed at the beginning of his mission.

[^1]: The above sentence actually has an implied omitted part understood from the context, originally being: "Do you say of the Truth when it has come to you [that it is] magic? Is this magic?"

Furthermore, "Magicians shall never be successful" (وَ لا يُفْلِحُ السَّاحِرُونَ‌).

This expression is, in fact, another proof of the distinction between the work of Prophets and magic. In the previous argument, the separation was proven based on the difference between magic and miracles and their respective appearances and goals. Here, however, the difference in the states and attributes of the practitioners of magic versus the bringers of miracles is used to prove the point.

Magicians, by virtue of their work and craft which is inherently deviant and deceptive, are profit-seeking, deviant, misleading, and fraudulent individuals who can be recognized through their actions. But Prophets are truth-seeking, compassionate, pure-hearted, purposeful, and pious men who are indifferent to material matters.

Magicians never see the face of true success and work for nothing but money, wealth, status, and personal interests, whereas the goal of the Prophets is guidance, the benefit of God's creation, and the reform of human society in all spiritual and material aspects.

Then, they continued their torrent of accusations against Moses, explicitly saying to him: "Have you come to us to turn us away from the way of life we found our fathers following?" (قالُوا أَ جِئْتَنا لِتَلْفِتَنا عَمَّا وَجَدْنا عَلَيْهِ آباءَنا). In fact, they brought up the idol of "ancestral traditions" and their imaginary, legendary greatness to prejudice public opinion against Moses and Aaron, suggesting that they wanted to toy with the sanctities of your society and country.

They then continued, claiming that your call to the religion and law of God is nothing but a lie; these are all traps and treacherous schemes "so that the two of you may have greatness (power) in the land" (وَ تَكُونَ لَكُمَا الْكِبْرِياءُ فِي الْأَرْضِ‌).

In reality, because they themselves exerted every effort for the sake of tyrannical rule over the people, they perceived others in the same way and interpreted the efforts of reformers and Prophets through that same lens.

But know that "We will never believe in you two," for we have read your hand and are aware of your destructive plans (وَ ما نَحْنُ لَكُما بِمُؤْمِنِينَ‌).

And this was the first stage of their struggle against Moses.

Ayahs

وَقَالَ فِرْعَوْنُ ٱئْتُونِى بِكُلِّ سَـٰحِرٍ عَلِيمٍ (٧٩)
Pharaoh said, ‘Bring me every expert magician.’ (79)
فَلَمَّا جَآءَ ٱلسَّحَرَةُ قَالَ لَهُم مُّوسَىٰٓ أَلْقُوا۟ مَآ أَنتُم مُّلْقُونَ (٨٠)
So when the magicians came, Moses said to them, ‘Throw down what you have to throw.’ (80)
فَلَمَّآ أَلْقَوْا۟ قَالَ مُوسَىٰ مَا جِئْتُم بِهِ ٱلسِّحْرُ ۖ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ سَيُبْطِلُهُۥٓ ۖ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُصْلِحُ عَمَلَ ٱلْمُفْسِدِينَ (٨١)
So when they threw down [their sticks and ropes], Moses said, ‘What you have produced is magic. Indeed Allah will bring it to naught presently. Indeed Allah does not foster the efforts of those who cause corruption. (81)
وَيُحِقُّ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْحَقَّ بِكَلِمَـٰتِهِۦ وَلَوْ كَرِهَ ٱلْمُجْرِمُونَ (٨٢)
Allah will confirm the truth with His words, though the guilty should be averse.’ (82)

Commentary

The Second Stage of the Struggle Against Moses

These verses describe another stage of the struggle and speak of Pharaoh's practical measures against Moses and his brother Aaron.

When Pharaoh observed some of Moses' miracles, such as the "White Hand" and the attack of the great serpent, and saw that Moses' claim was not without proof—and that this proof would more or less affect his circle of associates and others—he thought of a practical response. As the Quran says: "And Pharaoh said: 'Bring every learned sorcerer to me,'" so that through them, I may repel the trouble of Moses from myself (وَ قالَ فِرْعَوْنُ ائْتُونِي بِكُلِّ ساحِرٍ عَلِيمٍ).

He knew that every task must be approached through its proper channel and that help must be sought from experts in that field.

Did Pharaoh truly doubt the truthfulness of Moses' call and want to test him through this method? Or did he know that he was from Allah but thought that through the clamor of the sorcerers, he could calm the people and temporarily prevent the danger of Moses' influence on public opinion? Perhaps he wanted to say to the people: "If he performs an extraordinary feat, we are not incapable of doing the same! And if our royal will so desires, such a thing is easy and simple for us!"

The second possibility seems closer to the truth, and other verses related to the story of Moses in Surah Ta-Ha and similar chapters support this view—that he consciously rose to fight against Moses.
