In any case, "When the sorcerers gathered on the day appointed for this historic struggle, and a public invitation had been extended to the people, Moses turned to them and said: 'First, bring forth whatever you can into the field'" (فَلَمَّا جاءَ السَّحَرَةُ قالَ لَهُمْ مُوسى‌ أَلْقُوا ما أَنْتُمْ مُلْقُونَ).

The phrase "أَلْقُوا ما أَنْتُمْ مُلْقُونَ" literally means "Cast down whatever you are going to cast," and this refers to the ropes and special staffs that were hollow inside and filled with specific chemicals that would cause them to move and stir when exposed to the heat of the sun.

Evidence for this is found in verses from Surah Al-A'raf and Ash-Shu'ara. In verses 43 and 44 of Surah Ash-Shu'ara, we read: قالَ لَهُمْ مُوسى‌ أَلْقُوا ما أَنْتُمْ مُلْقُونَ فَأَلْقَوْا حِبالَهُمْ وَ عِصِيَّهُمْ وَ قالُوا بِعِزَّةِ فِرْعَوْنَ إِنَّا لَنَحْنُ الْغالِبُونَ: "Moses said to them: 'Cast down what you are going to cast.' So they cast down their ropes and their staffs and said: 'By the might of Pharaoh, it is we who will be the victors.'"

However, this phrase also carries the internal meaning of: "Bring forth into the field whatever power you possess."

In any case, they mobilized all they could and threw all the tools they had brought into the middle of the field. "At this moment, Moses said to them: 'What you have brought here is sorcery, and Allah will soon nullify it'" (فَلَمَّا أَلْقَوْا قالَ مُوسى‌ ما جِئْتُمْ بِهِ السِّحْرُ إِنَّ اللَّهَ سَيُبْطِلُهُ). You are corrupt and mischief-making individuals because you are in the service of a tyrannical, oppressive, and rebellious system, and you have sold your knowledge and science to strengthen the foundations of this autocratic government, and this itself is the best proof of your being mischief-makers, and "Allah does not amend the work of the mischief-makers" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ لا يُصْلِحُ عَمَلَ الْمُفْسِدِينَ).

In reality, anyone there who possessed intellect, intelligence, and knowledge could have realized this truth even before Moses' victory over the sorcerers—that the sorcerers' work was baseless because it was placed in the service of strengthening the foundations of oppression and tyranny. Who did not know that Pharaoh was a usurper, a plunderer, an oppressor, and a mischief-maker? Were the servants of such a system not partners in his oppression and corruption? And was it possible for their work to be a correct and Divine act? Never. Therefore, it was clear that Allah would nullify this corrupt effort.

Does the expression "سيبطله" (He will soon nullify it) prove that sorcery has a reality but Allah can nullify it? Or does this sentence mean that Allah will manifest its falsehood?

Verse 116 of Surah Al-A'raf says that the sorcery of the sorcerers affected the eyes of the people and struck them with terror (فَلَمَّا أَلْقَوْا سَحَرُوا أَعْيُنَ النَّاسِ وَ اسْتَرْهَبُوهُمْ). However, this expression does not contradict the idea that they—through a series of mysterious means as implied in the linguistic meaning of "sorcery," especially by utilizing the physical and chemical properties of various objects—created a series of real movements in those ropes and staffs. But certainly, these ropes and staffs were not living creatures as they appeared to the eyes of the superficial observers. As the Quran says in Surah Ta-Ha, verse 66: فَإِذا حِبالُهُمْ وَ عِصِيَّهُمْ يُخَيَّلُ إِلَيْهِ مِنْ سِحْرِهِمْ أَنَّها تَسْعى‌: "Then lo! their ropes and their staffs, by their magic, appeared to him as though they ran!" Therefore, part of the effect of sorcery is real, and part of it is illusion and imagination.

And in the final verse, it says: Moses told them that in this conflict and struggle, victory is certainly with us, because "Allah has promised to manifest the truth and, through the crushing logic and overwhelming miracles of His prophets, to disgrace the mischief-makers and the followers of falsehood, even if criminals like Pharaoh and his chiefs are averse to it" (وَ يُحِقُّ اللَّهُ الْحَقَّ بِكَلِماتِهِ وَ لَوْ كَرِهَ الْمُجْرِمُونَ).

The meaning of "بكلماته" (by His words) is either Allah's promise regarding the victory of the messengers of truth, or His overwhelming and powerful miracles. [^1]

Ayahs

فَمَآ ءَامَنَ لِمُوسَىٰٓ إِلَّا ذُرِّيَّةٌ مِّن قَوْمِهِۦ عَلَىٰ خَوْفٍ مِّن فِرْعَوْنَ وَمَلَإِي۟هِمْ أَن يَفْتِنَهُمْ ۚ وَإِنَّ فِرْعَوْنَ لَعَالٍ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَإِنَّهُۥ لَمِنَ ٱلْمُسْرِفِينَ (٨٣)
But none believed in Moses except some youths from among his people, for the fear of Pharaoh and his elite that he would persecute them. For Pharaoh was indeed a tyrant in the land, and indeed he was an unrestrained [despot]. (83)
وَقَالَ مُوسَىٰ يَـٰقَوْمِ إِن كُنتُمْ ءَامَنتُم بِٱللَّهِ فَعَلَيْهِ تَوَكَّلُوٓا۟ إِن كُنتُم مُّسْلِمِينَ (٨٤)
And Moses said, ‘O my people! If you have faith in Allah, put your trust in Him, if you have submitted [to Him].’ (84)
فَقَالُوا۟ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ تَوَكَّلْنَا رَبَّنَا لَا تَجْعَلْنَا فِتْنَةً لِّلْقَوْمِ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٨٥)
Whereat they said, ‘In Allah we have put our trust.’ ‘Our Lord! Do not make us a [means of] test for the wrongdoing lot, (85)
وَنَجِّنَا بِرَحْمَتِكَ مِنَ ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (٨٦)
and deliver us by Your mercy from the faithless lot.’ (86)

Commentary

The Third Stage of Moses' Struggle Against the Egyptian Tyrant

In these verses, another stage of the story of the revolutionary struggles of Moses and Pharaoh is reflected.

At the beginning, it describes the situation of the first group of believers in Moses and says: "But none believed in Moses except some youths of his people" (فَما آمَنَ لِمُوسى‌ إِلَّا ذُرِّيَّةٌ مِنْ قَوْمِهِ‌).

This small and few group, which according to the literal meaning of the word dhurriyyah (offspring/youth), mostly consisted of young people and adolescents, were under severe pressure from Pharaoh and his associates. At every moment, they "feared that the Pharaonic apparatus, with the intense pressure it exerted on the believers, would force them to abandon the religion and faith of Moses" (عَلى‌ خَوْفٍ مِنْ فِرْعَوْنَ وَ مَلَائِهِمْ أَنْ يَفْتِنَهُمْ‌).

This was because "Pharaoh was a tyrant in the land" (وَ إِنَّ فِرْعَوْنَ لَعالٍ فِي الْأَرْضِ‌).

"And he was indeed of the transgressors" who recognized no limits or boundaries (وَ إِنَّهُ لَمِنَ الْمُسْرِفِينَ‌).

There is discussion among commentators regarding who this dhurriyyah (offspring/youth) were that believed in Moses, and to whom the pronoun "of his people" (min qawmihi) refers (to Moses or to Pharaoh).

Some have suggested that this group consisted of a few individuals from the people of Pharaoh and the Copts, such as the Believer of the Family of Pharaoh, Pharaoh's wife, his daughter's hairdresser, and her maid. Apparently, the reason for choosing this view is that the majority of the Children of Israel believed, and this does not align with the expression "a youth/offspring of his people," as it implies a small group.

However, others believe that this group was from the Children of Israel, and the pronoun refers back to Moses, because the name of Moses was mentioned just before it, and according to grammatical rules, the pronoun should return to him.

Undoubtedly, the second meaning is more consistent with the literal appearance of the verse. Another piece of evidence supporting this is the subsequent verse which says: "And Moses said: O my people..." (وَ قالَ مُوسى‌ يا قَوْمِ‌), meaning he addresses the believers as "my people."

The only objection that might remain against this interpretation is that all of the Children of Israel believed in Moses, not just a small group.

However, this objection can be dismissed by noting one point: in every revolution, the first group attracted to it are the youth. Besides having purer hearts and untouched thoughts, the revolutionary fervor and passion are greater in them. Furthermore, the material attachments that invite adults to conservatism and various considerations are not present in them; they have neither wealth nor property to fear losing, nor positions or ranks to dread being endangered! Therefore, it is natural that this group would be attracted to and absorbed by Moses very early on, and the term dhurriyyah is very fitting for this meaning.

Additionally, adults also joined this group later, but because they had no role in the society of that day and were weak and powerless, this expression—as narrated from Ibn Abbas—is not so far-fetched regarding them. It is just like when we invite ordinary and common friends and say "the kids were invited," even if they are adults! And if we consider this expression unlikely, the first possibility remains strong.

Furthermore, although dhurriyyah is usually applied to children, from a linguistic root perspective—as Raghib stated in Al-Mufradat—it includes both the small and the great.

Another point to be noted here is that the meaning of "fitnah" (persecution/trial) used in the phrase "lest he should persecute them" (أَنْ يَفْتِنَهُمْ‌) refers to diverting them from the religion and faith of Moses through threats, intimidation, and torture, or it could mean any kind of trouble and headache, whether religious or non-religious.

In any case, to calm their minds and souls, Moses said to them in an affectionate tone: "O my people! If you have believed in Allah, and are sincere in your speech and your faith and Islam, then put your trust and reliance in Him," and do not fear the waves and storms of calamity, for faith is inseparable from trust (وَ قالَ مُوسى‌ يا قَوْمِ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ آمَنْتُمْ بِاللَّهِ فَعَلَيْهِ تَوَكَّلُوا إِنْ كُنْتُمْ مُسْلِمِينَ‌).

The reality of Tawakkul (trust in Allah) is entrusting one's affairs to another and choosing Him as one's representative (Wakil).

The concept of Tawakkul does not mean that a person should stop striving and effort, retreat into a corner of isolation, and say "my reliance is on God." Rather, its meaning is that whenever one exerts their utmost effort and cannot solve the problem or remove the obstacles from their path, they should not let fear in. Instead, relying on the grace of the Lord and seeking help from His Pure Essence and infinite power, they should show steadfastness and continue their persistent struggle. Even in matters where one has the ability, one should not consider themselves independent of Allah's grace, for whatever power exists ultimately comes from Him.

This is the concept of Tawakkul which cannot be separated from faith and Islam, because a believer who is submitted to the command of the Lord considers Him capable and powerful over all things, sees every difficulty as easy before His will, and believes in His promises of victory.

These true believers responded to Moses' call to trust: "And they said: In Allah we put our trust" (فَقالُوا عَلَى اللَّهِ تَوَكَّلْنا). Then they requested from the Holy Presence of God to be safe from the evil of the enemies and their whispers and pressures, saying: "Our Lord! Make us not a trial for the wrongdoing people" (رَبَّنا لا تَجْعَلْنا فِتْنَةً لِلْقَوْمِ الظَّالِمِينَ‌).

"And deliver us by Your mercy from the disbelieving people" (وَ نَجِّنا بِرَحْمَتِكَ مِنَ الْقَوْمِ الْكافِرِينَ‌).

It is interesting that Pharaoh is described in the first verse as being among the "transgressors" (musrifin), in the third verse he and his associates are described as "wrongdoers" (zalimin), and in the last verse as "disbelievers" (kafirin).

This variation in expressions might be because a person, in the path of sin and error, first starts with "extravagance" (israf), meaning exceeding limits and boundaries, then begins "wrongdoing" (zulm), and finally, their work ends in "disbelief and denial" (kufr)!

Ayahs

وَأَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَىٰ مُوسَىٰ وَأَخِيهِ أَن تَبَوَّءَا لِقَوْمِكُمَا بِمِصْرَ بُيُوتًا وَٱجْعَلُوا۟ بُيُوتَكُمْ قِبْلَةً وَأَقِيمُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ ۗ وَبَشِّرِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (٨٧)
We revealed to Moses and his brother [saying], ‘Settle your people in the city, and let your houses face each other, and maintain the prayer, and give good news to the faithful.’ (87)
وَقَالَ مُوسَىٰ رَبَّنَآ إِنَّكَ ءَاتَيْتَ فِرْعَوْنَ وَمَلَأَهُۥ زِينَةً وَأَمْوَٰلًا فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا رَبَّنَا لِيُضِلُّوا۟ عَن سَبِيلِكَ ۖ رَبَّنَا ٱطْمِسْ عَلَىٰٓ أَمْوَٰلِهِمْ وَٱشْدُدْ عَلَىٰ قُلُوبِهِمْ فَلَا يُؤْمِنُوا۟ حَتَّىٰ يَرَوُا۟ ٱلْعَذَابَ ٱلْأَلِيمَ (٨٨)
Moses said, ‘Our Lord! You have given Pharaoh and his elite glamour and wealth in the life of this world, our Lord, that they may lead [people] astray from Your way! Our Lord! Blot out their wealth and harden their hearts so that they do not believe until they sight the painful punishment.’ (88)
قَالَ قَدْ أُجِيبَت دَّعْوَتُكُمَا فَٱسْتَقِيمَا وَلَا تَتَّبِعَآنِّ سَبِيلَ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (٨٩)
Said He, ‘Your supplication has already been granted. So be steadfast, and do not follow the way of those who do not know.’ (89)

Commentary

The Fourth Stage of the Constructive Era for the Revolution

In these verses, another stage of the uprising and revolution of the Children of Israel against the Pharaohs is described.

First, Allah says: We sent revelation to Moses and his brother that "Choose houses for your people in the land of Egypt" (وَ أَوْحَيْنا إِلى‌ مُوسى‌ وَ أَخِيهِ أَنْ تَبَوَّءا لِقَوْمِكُما بِمِصْرَ بُيُوتاً).

And specifically, build these houses close to one another and facing each other (وَ اجْعَلُوا بُيُوتَكُمْ قِبْلَةً).

Then, engage in spiritual and internal self-building and "establish prayer," and through this, purify and strengthen your souls (وَ أَقِيمُوا الصَّلاةَ).

And so that the traces of fear and terror may be removed from their hearts, and they may regain their spiritual and revolutionary power, "give glad tidings to the believers"—glad tidings of victory and the grace and mercy of Allah (وَ بَشِّرِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ‌).

From the entirety of this verse, it is understood that the Children of Israel at that time were a scattered, defeated, dependent, and parasitic group, contaminated and fearful. They had no homes of their own, no social cohesion or focus, no constructive spiritual program, and lacked the necessary courage and bravery for a crushing revolution! Therefore, Moses and his brother Aaron عليه السلام were commissioned to implement a program consisting of several points to rebuild the society of the Children of Israel, especially from a spiritual perspective:

1- First, they should strive to build houses and separate their dwellings from the Pharaohs. This action had several benefits: One was that by owning dwellings in the land of Egypt, they would develop a greater interest in defending themselves and that land and soil. Another was that they would transition from a parasitic life in the houses of the Copts to an independent life. Furthermore, the secrets of their affairs and plans would not fall into the hands of their enemies.

2- They should build their houses facing one another and close together, because Qiblah (قِبْلَةً) originally means "a state of facing," and applying the word Qiblah to what is known today is actually a secondary meaning for this word. [^1] This action was an effective aid to the concentration and gathering of the Children of Israel, allowing them to examine social issues collectively, gather together under the guise of performing religious ceremonies, and devise the necessary plans for their freedom.

3- Attention to worship, and especially prayer, which detaches man from the servitude of servants and links him to the Creator of all powers. It washes his heart and soul from the pollution of sin and revives the sense of self-reliance within them. By relying on the power of the Lord, it breathes a new spirit into the human frame.

4- It commands Moses, as a leader, to reach into the depths of the souls of the Children of Israel and pull out the refuse of fear and terror, which was the legacy of long years of slavery and humiliation. Through "giving glad tidings to the believers" regarding the final conquest, victory, and the grace and mercy of the Lord, he was to strengthen their will and nurture courage and bravery within them.

It is interesting that the Children of Israel are the descendants of Jacob, and a group of them are naturally the descendants of Joseph, who, along with his brothers, ruled Egypt for many years and strove for the prosperity and development of this land. However, due to disobedience to Allah, ignorance, and internal disputes, their lives had reached such a pitiable state. This worn-out, calamity-stricken society must be renovated, its negative points must be purged, and constructive spiritual traits must be instilled in their place so that it may regain its past greatness.

[^1]: Of course, some commentators have not taken Qiblah in the above verse to mean "facing," but rather the same meaning as the Qiblah of prayer, considering the phrase "and establish prayer" as evidence for it. However, the first meaning is more consistent with the original linguistic concept of the word. Additionally, there is no problem with intending both meanings from this word, as we have seen similar instances many times.

Then, it points to one of the causes of the rebellion of Pharaoh and his people, and says through the tongue of Moses: "Our Lord, You have given Pharaoh and his establishment splendor and wealth in the life of this world" (وَ قالَ مُوسى‌ رَبَّنا إِنَّكَ آتَيْتَ فِرْعَوْنَ وَ مَلَأَهُ زِينَةً وَ أَمْوالًا فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا). "But the end of this wealth and luxury and the consequence of his actions has become that they lead Your servants astray from Your path" (رَبَّنا لِيُضِلُّوا عَنْ سَبِيلِكَ‌).

The "Lām" (ل) in "Liyudillū" (لِيُضِلُّوا) is what is called the "Lām of Consequence" (Lām al-Āqibah). That is, an aristocratic, luxury-worshipping, wealthy population will inevitably strive to mislead people from the path of Allah, and their end will be nothing but this. This is because the call of the prophets and divine programs make people awake, vigilant, united, and organized; in such a state, the space for looters and plunderers will become narrow and their days will turn dark. They, in turn, show a reaction and rise in opposition to the prophets.

Then Moses عليه السلام petitions the presence of Allah and says: "Our Lord! Wipe out and render ineffective their wealth" so that they cannot benefit from it (رَبَّنَا اطْمِسْ عَلى‌ أَمْوالِهِمْ‌).

"Tams" (طمس) in the language means to erase and render something useless. It is interesting that it has been mentioned in some narrations that after this curse, the wealth of the Pharaohs turned into clay and stone. Perhaps this is an allusion to the fact that such an economic crisis gripped them that the value of their wealth completely collapsed and became as worthless as clay! Then he added:

"Our Lord, in addition to this, take away from them the power of thought and reflection" (وَ اشْدُدْ عَلى‌ قُلُوبِهِمْ‌).

For by losing these two capitals (wealth and intellect), they will become ready for decline and annihilation, and our path toward revolution and delivering the final blow to them will open.

O Allah! If I ask this of You regarding the Pharaohs, it is not out of a spirit of revenge and malice, but because they no longer have any readiness for faith "and they will not believe until they see the painful punishment" (فَلا يُؤْمِنُوا حَتَّى يَرَوُا الْعَذابَ الْأَلِيمَ‌).

Of course, it is clear that faith after witnessing the punishment—as will soon be mentioned—will be of no benefit to them.

Allah addressed Moses and his brother that now that you have become ready for the training and building of the population of the Children of Israel, "Your prayer regarding your enemies has been answered" (قالَ قَدْ أُجِيبَتْ دَعْوَتُكُما). "So stand firm on your path and show perseverance" and do not fear the multitude of problems, and be decisive in your work (فَاسْتَقِيما).

And never surrender to the suggestions of ignorant and uninformed individuals "and do not follow the path of those who do not know," but rather continue your revolutionary programs with full awareness (وَ لا تَتَّبِعانِّ سَبِيلَ الَّذِينَ لا يَعْلَمُونَ‌).

Ayahs

۞ وَجَـٰوَزْنَا بِبَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ ٱلْبَحْرَ فَأَتْبَعَهُمْ فِرْعَوْنُ وَجُنُودُهُۥ بَغْيًا وَعَدْوًا ۖ حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَآ أَدْرَكَهُ ٱلْغَرَقُ قَالَ ءَامَنتُ أَنَّهُۥ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا ٱلَّذِىٓ ءَامَنَتْ بِهِۦ بَنُوٓا۟ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ وَأَنَا۠ مِنَ ٱلْمُسْلِمِينَ (٩٠)
We carried the Children of Israel across the sea, whereat Pharaoh and his troops pursued them, out of defiance and aggression. When overtaken by drowning, he called out, ‘I believe that there is no god except Him in whom the Children of Israel believe, and I am one of those who submit [to Him]!’ (90)
ءَآلْـَٔـٰنَ وَقَدْ عَصَيْتَ قَبْلُ وَكُنتَ مِنَ ٱلْمُفْسِدِينَ (٩١)
[He was told,] ‘What! Now? When you have been disobedient heretofore and were among the agents of corruption?! (91)
فَٱلْيَوْمَ نُنَجِّيكَ بِبَدَنِكَ لِتَكُونَ لِمَنْ خَلْفَكَ ءَايَةً ۚ وَإِنَّ كَثِيرًا مِّنَ ٱلنَّاسِ عَنْ ءَايَـٰتِنَا لَغَـٰفِلُونَ (٩٢)
So today We shall deliver your body so that you may be a sign for those who come after you.’ Indeed many of the people are oblivious to Our signs. (92)
وَلَقَدْ بَوَّأْنَا بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ مُبَوَّأَ صِدْقٍ وَرَزَقْنَـٰهُم مِّنَ ٱلطَّيِّبَـٰتِ فَمَا ٱخْتَلَفُوا۟ حَتَّىٰ جَآءَهُمُ ٱلْعِلْمُ ۚ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ يَقْضِى بَيْنَهُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ فِيمَا كَانُوا۟ فِيهِ يَخْتَلِفُونَ (٩٣)
Certainly We settled the Children of Israel in a worthy settlement and We provided them with all the good things, and they did not differ until [after] the knowledge had come to them. Your Lord will indeed judge between them on the Day of Resurrection concerning that about which they used to differ. (93)

Commentary

The Final Chapter of the Struggle Against the Oppressors

In these verses, the final stage of the Israelites' struggle against the Pharaohs and their ultimate fate is depicted in brief yet precise and clear terms. As is the method of the Quran, it omits additional details that can be understood from the preceding and succeeding sentences.

First, it states: "We brought the Children of Israel across the sea (the great Nile River, which is referred to as bahr [sea] due to its vastness) while they were facing the Pharaohs and were under their pressure and pursuit" (وَ جاوَزْنا بِبَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ الْبَحْرَ).

"Pharaoh and his hosts pursued them out of insolence and hostility to crush the Children of Israel and to oppress and transgress against them, but soon they were all drowned amidst the waves of the Nile" (فَأَتْبَعَهُمْ فِرْعَوْنُ وَ جُنُودُهُ بَغْياً وَ عَدْواً).

Baghy means oppression and ’adw means transgression; that is, they pursued them for the sake of oppressing and transgressing against the Children of Israel.

The phrase fa-atba‘ahum (so he followed them) indicates that Pharaoh and his army pursued the Children of Israel by their own choice. Some narrations support this meaning, while others are not entirely consistent with it; however, what is understood from the apparent meaning of the verse is our primary evidence.

As for how the Children of Israel crossed the sea and what miracle occurred at that moment, the explanation will come, God willing, under verse 63 of Surah Ash-Shu'ara.

In any case, this event continued "until the drowning overtook Pharaoh, and he was rolling like a piece of straw upon the massive waves of the Nile. At this moment, the veils of pride and ignorance were removed from before his eyes, and the light of innate monotheism (fitra) began to shine. He cried out: 'I believe that there is no deity except Him in whom the Children of Israel believe'" (حَتَّى إِذا أَدْرَكَهُ الْغَرَقُ قالَ آمَنْتُ أَنَّهُ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا الَّذِي آمَنَتْ بِهِ بَنُوا إِسْرائِيلَ).

Not only did I believe with my heart, but I am also practically "submitting to such a powerful Lord" (وَ أَنَا مِنَ الْمُسْلِمِينَ).

In reality, when Moses' prophecies were fulfilled one after another, and Pharaoh became increasingly aware of the truthfulness of this great Prophet's words and witnessed his display of power, he was forced to declare faith. He hoped that just as the "God of the Children of Israel" had rescued them from these mountain-like waves, He would rescue him as well; hence he says: "I believe in the same God as the Children of Israel!"

However, it is obvious that such faith, expressed at the time of the descent of calamity and being caught in the clutches of death, is actually a type of "forced faith" (iman-e idtirari) that every criminal, culprit, and sinner professes, without it having any value or being evidence of his perfection, good intention, or truthfulness.

For this reason, Allah addressed him and said: "Now [you believe], while you had disobeyed before this and were among the spreaders of corruption on earth and the evildoers?" (آلْآنَ وَ قَدْ عَصَيْتَ قَبْلُ وَ كُنْتَ مِنَ الْمُفْسِدِينَ).

We previously read in verse 18 of Surah An-Nisa: (وَ لَيْسَتِ التَّوْبَةُ لِلَّذِينَ يَعْمَلُونَ السَّيِّئاتِ حَتَّى إِذا حَضَرَ أَحَدَهُمُ الْمَوْتُ قالَ إِنِّي تُبْتُ الْآنَ): "And repentance is not [accepted] for those who [continue to] do evil deeds until, when death comes to one of them, he says, 'Indeed, I have repented now.'"

For this reason, it has often been seen that if the waves of calamity subside and they are rescued from the clutches of death, they return to their former ways.

A similar expression to what we read above has appeared in the poems and words of Arab and Persian men of letters, such as: She came while the pools of death stood between me and her, And she offered union at a time when union was of no benefit!

However, "Today We will save your body from the waves so that you may be a lesson for those after you"—for arrogant rulers, for all oppressors and corrupters, and also for the oppressed groups (فَالْيَوْمَ نُنَجِّيكَ بِبَدَنِكَ لِتَكُونَ لِمَنْ خَلْفَكَ آيَةً). There is discussion among commentators as to what is meant by "body" (badan) here. Most believe it refers to the lifeless corpse of Pharaoh, because Pharaoh's greatness in the minds of the people of that environment was such that if his body had not emerged from the water, many would not have believed that Pharaoh could possibly have drowned. It was possible that following this event, false legends would be made regarding Pharaoh's survival and life; therefore, Allah cast his lifeless body out of the water.

Interestingly, badan in the language—as Raghib has stated in Al-Mufradat—means a "large body," and this suggests that Pharaoh, like many affluent people who have legendary, glamorous lives, had a large and stout physique!

However, others have said that one of the meanings of badan is "armor," and this is an allusion to the fact that Allah sent Pharaoh out of the water with the same golden armor he was wearing so that he would be recognized by it and no doubt would remain for anyone.

It is also noteworthy that some have inferred from the word nunnajjika (We save you) that Allah commanded the waves to cast his body onto a high point on the shore, because the root najwah means a high place or elevated ground.

Another point visible in the verse is that the sentence fal-yawma nunnajjika begins with the particle fa (so/consequently), which may indicate that Pharaoh's soulless faith at that moment of despair and being caught in the clutches of death—this faith which was like a lifeless body—had this much effect: that Allah saved Pharaoh's lifeless body from the water so it would not become food for the fish of the sea and would be a lesson for those to come! Currently, in the museums of Egypt and Britain, one or two bodies of the Pharaohs remain in a mummified state. Whether the body of the Pharaoh contemporary with Moses is among them, having been mummified later, we have no definitive proof. However, the expression li-man khalfaka (for those after you) might strengthen the possibility that the body of that Pharaoh is among them, to be a lesson for all future generations, as the verse's expression is absolute and includes all who come after (reflect on this).

And at the end of the verse, He says: But despite all these signs and tokens of Allah's power, and all these lessons that fill human history, "Indeed, many of the people are heedless of Our signs" (وَ إِنَّ كَثِيراً مِنَ النَّاسِ عَنْ آياتِنا لَغافِلُونَ).

In the final verse under discussion, the ultimate victory of the Children of Israel and their return to the holy lands after being rescued from the clutches of the Pharaohs is stated as follows: "We settled the Children of Israel in a place of truth and sincerity" (وَ لَقَدْ بَوَّأْنا بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ مُبَوَّأَ صِدْقٍ).

The expression mubawwa’a sidqin (a truthful settlement) may refer to the fact that Allah fulfilled His promise to the Children of Israel and returned them to the Promised Land, or that the "land of truth" refers to the purity and goodness of this land, and for this reason, it corresponds to the land of Sham (Levant) and Palestine, which was the dwelling place of the Prophets of Allah.

A group has also suggested that it refers to the land of Egypt, because as the Quran says in Surah Ad-Dukhan: "After the destruction of the Pharaohs, gardens, springs, lands, palaces, and blessings were left by them, and We gave them to another group, namely the Children of Israel" (كَمْ تَرَكُوا مِنْ جَنَّاتٍ وَ عُيُونٍ وَ زُرُوعٍ وَ مَقامٍ كَرِيمٍ وَ نَعْمَةٍ كانُوا فِيها فاكِهِينَ كَذلِكَ وَ أَوْرَثْناها قَوْماً آخَرِينَ - Surah Ad-Dukhan, verses 25-28). The same theme appears in Surah Ash-Shu'ara, verses 57-59, and at the end, we read: "And We caused the Children of Israel to inherit them" (وَ أَوْرَثْناها بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ).

From these verses, it appears that the Children of Israel stayed in Egypt for a while before migrating to the land of Sham and benefited from the blessings of that productive land.

Then the Quran adds: "We provided them with good things" (وَ رَزَقْناهُمْ مِنَ الطَّيِّباتِ).

Of course, there is no obstacle to both the land of Egypt and the lands of Sham and Palestine being intended.

But they did not appreciate this blessing, "and they fell into disagreement and dispute with one another, and that was not out of ignorance, but out of knowledge and after witnessing all those miracles of Moses and the proofs of the truthfulness of his call" (فَمَا اخْتَلَفُوا حَتَّى جاءَهُمُ الْعِلْمُ).

But "your Lord will surely judge between them on the Day of Resurrection concerning that over which they used to differ," and if they do not taste the punishment of disagreement today, they will taste it tomorrow! (إِنَّ رَبَّكَ يَقْضِي بَيْنَهُمْ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ فِيما كانُوا فِيهِ يَخْتَلِفُونَ).

This possibility has also been given in the interpretation of the above verse: that the disagreement refers to the disagreement of the Children of Israel and the Jews contemporary with the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله regarding the acceptance of his call. That is, despite knowing the truthfulness of his call according to the signs in their own heavenly books, they disagreed; a group believed, and a larger group refused to accept his call, and Allah will judge between them on the Day of Resurrection.

However, the first possibility is more consistent with the apparent meaning of the verse.

This was a part of the instructive story of the Children of Israel mentioned in the verses of this Surah. How similar their state is to the Muslims of today! Allah, in the light of His grace, grants victories to the Muslims and crushes their powerful enemies in a miraculous manner, making this oppressed group victorious over those powerful ones by His grace and mercy. But unfortunately, instead of making this victory a means for the globalization of the religion of Islam, they make it an excuse for division and the creation of hypocrisy and disagreement, such that all their victories are endangered! May Allah save us from this ingratitude for His blessings.

Ayahs

فَإِن كُنتَ فِى شَكٍّ مِّمَّآ أَنزَلْنَآ إِلَيْكَ فَسْـَٔلِ ٱلَّذِينَ يَقْرَءُونَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ مِن قَبْلِكَ ۚ لَقَدْ جَآءَكَ ٱلْحَقُّ مِن رَّبِّكَ فَلَا تَكُونَنَّ مِنَ ٱلْمُمْتَرِينَ (٩٤)
So if you are in doubt about what We have sent down to you, ask those who read the Book [revealed] before you. The truth has certainly come to you from your Lord; so do not be among the skeptics. (94)
وَلَا تَكُونَنَّ مِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ فَتَكُونَ مِنَ ٱلْخَـٰسِرِينَ (٩٥)
And do not be of those who deny the signs of Allah, [for] then you shall be among the losers. (95)
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ حَقَّتْ عَلَيْهِمْ كَلِمَتُ رَبِّكَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ (٩٦)
Indeed those against whom your Lord’s judgement has become due will not have faith, (96)
وَلَوْ جَآءَتْهُمْ كُلُّ ءَايَةٍ حَتَّىٰ يَرَوُا۟ ٱلْعَذَابَ ٱلْأَلِيمَ (٩٧)
even though every sign were to come to them, until they sight the painful punishment. (97)

Commentary

Do Not Harbor Any Doubt!

Since the preceding verses mentioned portions of the stories of the Prophets and past nations, and it was possible that some of the polytheists and deniers of the Prophet’s (صلى الله عليه وآله) call might doubt their authenticity, the Quran asks them to refer to the People of the Book to understand the truth of these statements and to seek the details from them, as many of these matters are present in their books.

However, instead of addressing the opponents directly, it addresses the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and says:

"So if you are in doubt about what We have revealed to you, then ask those who have been reading the Scripture before you" (فَإِنْ كُنْتَ فِي شَكٍّ مِمَّا أَنْزَلْنا إِلَيْكَ فَسْئَلِ الَّذِينَ يَقْرَؤُنَ الْكِتابَ مِنْ قَبْلِكَ‌).

This is so that it may be proven through this path that "The truth has certainly come to you from your Lord" (لَقَدْ جاءَكَ الْحَقُّ مِنْ رَبِّكَ‌).

"So never be among the doubters" (فَلا تَكُونَنَّ مِنَ المُمْتَرِينَ‌).

There is also the possibility that the above verse introduces a new and independent discussion regarding the truthfulness of the Prophet’s (صلى الله عليه وآله) call, telling the opponents that if they doubt his legitimacy, they should ask the People of the Book about his signs which were revealed in previous scriptures like the Torah and the Gospel.

The occasion of revelation (Shan al-Nuzul) narrated in some commentaries [1] also supports this meaning: a group of the Quraysh pagans used to say, "This Quran has not been revealed by Allah; rather, Satan inspires it to Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله)!" This statement caused some people to fall into doubt, and Allah responded to them with the above verse.

Did the Prophet Have Doubts?

At first glance, it might seem that the verse suggests the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) had doubts about the truth of the verses revealed to him, and that Allah removed his doubt through the aforementioned method.

[^1]: Tafsir Abu al-Futuh Razi, Vol. 6, p. 227, under the verse.

However, considering that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) perceived the matter of revelation through direct witnessing and vision—as the verses of the Quran indicate—doubt in this regard would be meaningless. Furthermore, it is a common rhetorical style to address those close to the speaker in order to warn those further away; this is what the Arabs refer to with the famous proverb: "I am speaking to you, but listen, O neighbor!" (اياك اعنى و اسمعى يا جاره), and we have similar expressions in Persian. This type of speech is often more effective than a direct address.

Moreover, the mention of a conditional sentence (if...) does not always imply the possibility of the condition's existence; rather, it is sometimes used to emphasize a point or to state a general law. For example, in Surah Al-Isra, verse 23, we read: "And your Lord has decreed that you worship none but Him, and that you be kind to parents. Whether one or both of them attain old age with you [note that the addressee here is apparently only the Prophet], say not to them [so much as] 'uff'..."

Even though we know the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) lost his father before birth and his mother during his childhood, the ruling on respecting parents is stated as a general law, even though the apparent addressee is the Prophet.

Similarly, in Surah At-Talaq, we read: "O Prophet, when you [plural] divorce women..." (يا أَيُّهَا النَّبِيُّ إِذا طَلَّقْتُمُ النِّساءَ). This expression does not mean the Prophet ever divorced a woman in his life; rather, it is the statement of a general law. Interestingly, the addressee at the beginning of this sentence is the Prophet, while at the end, it refers to all people.

Among the clues confirming that the primary targets of the verse are the polytheists and disbelievers are the verses following it, which speak of their disbelief and lack of faith.

A similar case is seen in the verses regarding Jesus (عليه السلام), where Allah asks him on the Day of Resurrection whether he invited people to worship him and his mother. He explicitly denies this and adds: "If I had said it, You would have known it" (إِنْ كُنْتُ قُلْتُهُ فَقَدْ عَلِمْتَهُ) (Surah Al-Ma'idah, verse 116).

In the next verse, it adds that now that the signs of your Lord and the truth of this call have become clear to you, "And do not be among those who denied the signs of Allah, for then you will be among the losers" (وَ لا تَكُونَنَّ مِنَ الَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا بِآياتِ اللَّهِ فَتَكُونَ مِنَ الْخاسِرِينَ‌).

In fact, the previous verse says: if you have doubt, ask those who have knowledge; and this verse says: now that the factors of doubt have been removed, you must submit to these verses, otherwise, opposing the truth will result in nothing but loss and ruin.

This verse is a clear indication that the primary target in the preceding verse was the masses of people, even though the speech was directed at the person of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), for it is obvious that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) would never deny the divine signs; rather, he was the staunch defender of his religion.

Then He informs the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) that among your opponents is a prejudiced and stubborn group whose expectation of faith is futile. They have become so intellectually distorted and have walked so far in the path of falsehood that they have completely lost their awakened human conscience and have become impenetrable beings. However, the Quran expresses this matter with this phrasing: "Indeed, those upon whom the word of your Lord has come true will not believe" (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ حَقَّتْ عَلَيْهِمْ كَلِمَتُ رَبِّكَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ‌).

"Even if every sign should come to them, until they see the painful punishment" (وَ لَوْ جاءَتْهُمْ كُلُّ آيَةٍ حَتَّى يَرَوُا الْعَذابَ الْأَلِيمَ‌), at which time faith will have no effect on them.

In truth, the first of the verses under discussion invited the general public to study, research, and ask those informed, and subsequently asked them to rise in support and defense of the truth once it became clear.

But in these latter verses, it says you should not expect all of them to believe, for a group has become so corrupt that they are no longer reformable. Therefore, do not be discouraged by their lack of faith, nor waste your energy on their guidance; instead, focus on the group that constitutes the majority and is capable of being guided.

As we have repeated many times, expressions like the above verses are by no means evidence of "determinism" (Jabr); rather, they are of the nature of mentioning the consequences of human actions. However, since the effect of everything is by Allah's command, these matters are sometimes attributed to Allah.

It also seems necessary to mention that a few verses prior, we read about Pharaoh that he declared faith after the punishment descended and he was caught in the clutches of the storm. But because such faith was out of desperation, it was of no benefit to him. In the verses under discussion, it says: "This was not only Pharaoh's way; rather, all stubborn, selfish, arrogant, and black-hearted individuals who have reached the peak of rebellion have the same state. They too will not believe until they see the painful punishment—the very faith that is ineffective for them."

Ayah

فَلَوْلَا كَانَتْ قَرْيَةٌ ءَامَنَتْ فَنَفَعَهَآ إِيمَـٰنُهَآ إِلَّا قَوْمَ يُونُسَ لَمَّآ ءَامَنُوا۟ كَشَفْنَا عَنْهُمْ عَذَابَ ٱلْخِزْىِ فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا وَمَتَّعْنَـٰهُمْ إِلَىٰ حِينٍ (٩٨)
Why has there not been any town that might believe, so that its belief might benefit it, except the people of Jonah? When they believed, We removed from them the punishment of disgrace in the life of this world, and We provided for them for a while. (98)

Commentary

Only One Group Believed in Time!

In the preceding verses, regarding Pharaoh and his people specifically, and other ancient nations generally, it was mentioned that they refused to believe in Allah while they possessed choice and health. However, when they found themselves on the brink of death and divine punishment, they declared their faith, which was of no benefit to them.

In the verse under discussion, this matter is stated as a general law, saying: "Why was there not a town that believed [in time] so that its faith might have profited it?" (فَلَوْ لا كانَتْ قَرْيَةٌ آمَنَتْ فَنَفَعَها إِيمانُها).

Then, it excludes the people of Yunus عليه السلام, saying: "Except the people of Yunus; when they believed, We removed from them the punishment of disgrace in the life of this world" (إِلَّا قَوْمَ يُونُسَ لَمَّا آمَنُوا كَشَفْنا عَنْهُمْ عَذابَ الْخِزْيِ فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا).

"And We gave them enjoyment for a while (until the end of their lives)" (وَ مَتَّعْناهُمْ إِلى‌ حِينٍ‌).

According to some commentators, the word "lawla" (لولا) here carries the meaning of negation, and for this reason, an exception has been made from it using "illa" (إلا).

Therefore, the meaning of the sentence becomes: No nation or people who lived in cities and settlements in the past believed collectively before their divine prophets, except for the people of Yunus.

However, others believe that "lawla" does not carry the meaning of negation, but rather always signifies "tahdid" (an expression of urging or prodding, often involving a question coupled with reproach and stimulation); yet, its conceptual implication in such cases is negation. For this reason, something can be excluded from it using "illa".

In any case, there is no doubt that among other nations, many groups did believe. What distinguishes the people of Yunus from other nations is that they all believed collectively, and they did so before the arrival of the definitive punishment of the Lord. In contrast, among other nations, a large group remained stubbornly in their opposition until the command for the definitive punishment of the Lord was issued; they often declared their faith only after witnessing this divine torment, but their faith was of no use for the reasons we previously mentioned.

The Story of the Faith of the People of Yunus

Their story, as recorded in histories, was as follows: When Yunus became despaired of his people—who lived in the land of "Nineveh" (in Iraq)—believing, he invoked a curse upon them at the suggestion of a worshiper (abid) who lived among them. This was while there was also a scholar (alim) among that group who suggested to Yunus that he should continue to pray for them and engage in further guidance and not lose hope.

However, after this incident, Yunus left his people. His people, who had tested the truth of his words many times, gathered around the scholar. The definitive command of punishment had not yet arrived, but its signs were more or less visible. They seized the opportunity and, under the leadership of the scholar, poured out of the city while raising their hands in prayer and supplication, declaring their faith and repentance. To create a greater revolution and focus within their souls and spirits, they separated mothers from their children and wore coarse, rough, and humble clothing. They set out in search of their Prophet but found no trace of him.

But this repentance, faith, and return toward the Lord, which was performed in time and was accompanied by awareness and sincerity, did its work. The signs of punishment were removed, and tranquility returned to them. When Yunus returned to his people after his long ordeal, they received him with all their heart and soul.

God willing, we will explain the details of the life story of Yunus himself under verses 134 to 148 of Surah As-Saffat.

It is also necessary to remind that the people of Yunus were never placed before the definitive, final punishment; otherwise, even their repentance would not have been accepted. Rather, the warnings and alarms that usually come before the final punishment were awakening enough for them. In contrast, the people of Pharaoh, for example, had seen these warnings many times (such as the incident of the flood, the locusts, the severe transformation of the Nile water, and the like), but they never took these warnings seriously. They only asked Musa عليه السلام to have Allah remove the distress from them so that they might believe, but they never truly believed.

Furthermore, the above story shows how effective and life-giving the role of an aware and compassionate leader is among a people and nation. In contrast, a worshiper who lacks sufficient awareness relies more on harshness. The logic of Islam in comparing unaware worship with knowledge coupled with a sense of responsibility is also understood from this narration.

Ayahs

وَلَوْ شَآءَ رَبُّكَ لَـَٔامَنَ مَن فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ كُلُّهُمْ جَمِيعًا ۚ أَفَأَنتَ تُكْرِهُ ٱلنَّاسَ حَتَّىٰ يَكُونُوا۟ مُؤْمِنِينَ (٩٩)
Had your Lord wished, all those who are on earth would have believed. Would you then force people until they become faithful? (99)
وَمَا كَانَ لِنَفْسٍ أَن تُؤْمِنَ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ وَيَجْعَلُ ٱلرِّجْسَ عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يَعْقِلُونَ (١٠٠)
No soul may have faith except by Allah’s leave, and He lays defilement on those who do not exercise their reason. (100)

Commentary

Forced Faith is Futile

In the preceding verses, we read that compulsory faith is of no avail. For this reason, the first verse under discussion states: "If compulsory and forced faith were of any use, and your Lord had willed, everyone on earth would have believed" (وَ لَوْ شاءَ رَبُّكَ لَآمَنَ مَنْ فِي الْأَرْضِ كُلُّهُمْ جَمِيعاً).

Therefore, do not be grieved or distressed by the lack of faith of a group of them; this is the necessary consequence of the principle of free will and choice—that one group will be believers and another will be disbelievers. "Would you then compel people until they become believers?" (أَ فَأَنْتَ تُكْرِهُ النَّاسَ حَتَّى يَكُونُوا مُؤْمِنِينَ‌).

This verse once again explicitly refutes the unjust accusation that the enemies of Islam have repeatedly made and continue to make, claiming that Islam is a religion of the sword and has been imposed upon the people of the world through force and compulsion. This verse, like many other verses of the Quran, states that forced faith is worthless, and fundamentally, religion and faith are things that must arise from within the soul, not from without or by means of the sword. It specifically warns the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله against coercing or forcing people into faith and Islam.

At the same time, in the following verse, it reminds us of this reality: it is true that humans are autonomous and free, "but still, no soul can believe except by Allah's leave" and His command (وَ ما كانَ لِنَفْسٍ أَنْ تُؤْمِنَ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِ اللَّهِ‌). Consequently, those who walk the path of ignorance and lack of reasoning, and are unwilling to utilize their capital of thought and intellect, "Allah places abomination and filth upon them" such that they will not succeed in attaining faith (وَ يَجْعَلُ الرِّجْسَ عَلَى الَّذِينَ لا يَعْقِلُونَ‌).

Two points must be noted here

  1. At first glance, it might be imagined that the first and second verses contradict each other, because the first verse says Allah does not compel anyone to believe, while the second verse says no one believes unless it is by the command and will of the Lord.

However, by considering one point, this apparent and initial contradiction is resolved: We believe that neither "determinism" (jabr) is correct, nor "absolute delegation" (tafwid). That is, it is not the case that people are compelled and without choice in their actions, nor is it the case that they are left entirely to themselves in every sense. Rather, despite having free will, they still need Divine assistance. This is because Allah gives them this free will; intellect, reason, and a pure conscience are His gifts; and the guidance of the Prophets and the direction of the Heavenly Books also come from Him. Therefore, while the will is free, the source of this gift and its outcome is still from Allah (reflect carefully).

[This verse is not evidence for determinism]

  1. The final sentence of the latter verse, namely "وَ يَجْعَلُ الرِّجْسَ عَلَى الَّذِينَ لا يَعْقِلُونَ‌" (And He places abomination upon those who do not reason), must never be interpreted as determinism. This is because the phrase "they do not reason" (la ya'qilun) is evidence of their own choice. That is, first, individuals refuse to reason, contemplate, and think; eventually, they are afflicted with this punishment where the "abomination" and filth of doubt, uncertainty, darkness of heart, and incorrect insight overcome them, to the point that the ability to believe is taken away from them. However, it must be noted that they themselves provided the preliminaries for this. In reality, in such cases, the Divine permission and command for faith do not exist.

In other words, this sentence points to the fact that Allah's permission and command are not arbitrary; those who are worthy and deserving are included in it, and those who are unworthy will be deprived.

Ayahs

قُلِ ٱنظُرُوا۟ مَاذَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ وَمَا تُغْنِى ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتُ وَٱلنُّذُرُ عَن قَوْمٍ لَّا يُؤْمِنُونَ (١٠١)
Say, ‘Observe what is in the heavens and the earth.’ But neither signs nor warnings avail a people who have no faith. (101)
فَهَلْ يَنتَظِرُونَ إِلَّا مِثْلَ أَيَّامِ ٱلَّذِينَ خَلَوْا۟ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ ۚ قُلْ فَٱنتَظِرُوٓا۟ إِنِّى مَعَكُم مِّنَ ٱلْمُنتَظِرِينَ (١٠٢)
Do they await anything except the like of the days of those who passed away before them? Say, ‘Then wait! I too am waiting along with you.’ (102)
ثُمَّ نُنَجِّى رُسُلَنَا وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ حَقًّا عَلَيْنَا نُنجِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (١٠٣)
Then We shall deliver Our apostles and those who have faith. Thus, it is a must for Us to deliver the faithful. (103)

Commentary

Education and Admonition

In the preceding verses, the discussion centered on the fact that faith must be a matter of choice, not compulsion or necessity. Accordingly, in the first verse under discussion, the way to achieve voluntary faith is shown, and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله is told: "Tell them to look carefully and see what an astonishing and marvelous system exists in the heavens and the earth, where every corner of it is a proof of the greatness, power, knowledge, and wisdom of the Creator (قُلِ انْظُرُوا ما ذا فِي السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ)."

All these shining stars and various celestial spheres, each revolving in its own orbit; these great solar systems and giant galaxies, and the precise order that governs them all; likewise, this planet Earth with all its wonders and secrets, and all these diverse and varied living beings—look closely at the structure of all these, and through studying them, become more acquainted and closer to the Originator of the universe.

This sentence clearly refutes the issue of determinism and the negation of free will, stating: faith is the result of studying the world of creation, meaning this task is in your own hands.

Then it adds that despite all these signs and symbols of Truth, it is still not surprising that a group does not believe, because signs, symbols, warnings, and admonitions are only useful for those who have the readiness to accept the Truth. However, "those who have decided never to believe, these matters have no effect on them" (وَ ما تُغْنِي الْآياتُ وَ النُّذُرُ عَنْ قَوْمٍ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ) [1]. This sentence refers to a reality we have read many times in the Quran: that proofs, words of Truth, signs, and advice are not sufficient on their own; rather, receptive and ready grounds are also a condition for achieving a result.

Then, in a threatening tone but clothed in the form of a question, it says: "Do these stubborn and faithless people expect anything other than a fate similar to the rebellious and neck-stretching nations of the past who were caught in painful divine punishment?" A fate like that of the Pharaohs, the Nimrods, the Shaddads, and their aides and supporters! (فَهَلْ يَنْتَظِرُونَ إِلَّا مِثْلَ أَيَّامِ الَّذِينَ خَلَوْا مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ).

And at the end of the verse, it warns them, saying: "O Prophet! Tell them: now that you are on such a path and are not willing to reconsider, then wait, and we too shall be waiting with you." You wait for the crushing of the call to Truth, and we wait for a grim and painful fate for you, like the fate of the arrogant nations of the past (قُلْ فَانْتَظِرُوا إِنِّي مَعَكُمْ مِنَ الْمُنْتَظِرِينَ).

It should be noted that the "question" in the phrase "Fa-hal yantadhirun" is a rhetorical question of denial, meaning that with this manner of behavior, they cannot expect anything except the arrival of a grim fate.

"Ayyam" (days), although literally the plural of "Yawm" (day), here refers to the painful events that occurred during the lifetimes of past nations.

Then, so that the misconception does not arise that Allah burns the wet and the dry together when punishing, or that He overlooks even a single believer among a vast group of rebels and insurgents, it adds: After the preliminaries for the punishment of past nations were prepared, "We would save Our messengers and those who believed" (ثُمَّ نُنَجِّي رُسُلَنا وَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا).

And in the end, it says: This was not exclusive to past nations and previous messengers and believers; rather, "In the same way, We shall save you and those who believe in you, and this is a right upon Us—a certain and irrevocable right" (كَذلِكَ حَقًّا عَلَيْنا نُنْجِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ) [1].

Ayahs

قُلْ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ إِن كُنتُمْ فِى شَكٍّ مِّن دِينِى فَلَآ أَعْبُدُ ٱلَّذِينَ تَعْبُدُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ وَلَـٰكِنْ أَعْبُدُ ٱللَّهَ ٱلَّذِى يَتَوَفَّىٰكُمْ ۖ وَأُمِرْتُ أَنْ أَكُونَ مِنَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (١٠٤)
Say, ‘O people! if you are in doubt about my religion, then [know that] I do not worship those whom you worship besides Allah. Rather, I worship only Allah, who causes you to die, and I have been commanded to be among the faithful, (104)
وَأَنْ أَقِمْ وَجْهَكَ لِلدِّينِ حَنِيفًا وَلَا تَكُونَنَّ مِنَ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ (١٠٥)
and that: ‘‘Dedicate yourself to the religion, as a Hanif, and never be one of the polytheists. (105)
وَلَا تَدْعُ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ مَا لَا يَنفَعُكَ وَلَا يَضُرُّكَ ۖ فَإِن فَعَلْتَ فَإِنَّكَ إِذًا مِّنَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (١٠٦)
Nor invoke besides Allah that which neither benefits you nor can do you any harm. For if you do so, then you will indeed be among the wrongdoers.’’ ’ (106)
وَإِن يَمْسَسْكَ ٱللَّهُ بِضُرٍّ فَلَا كَاشِفَ لَهُۥٓ إِلَّا هُوَ ۖ وَإِن يُرِدْكَ بِخَيْرٍ فَلَا رَآدَّ لِفَضْلِهِۦ ۚ يُصِيبُ بِهِۦ مَن يَشَآءُ مِنْ عِبَادِهِۦ ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلْغَفُورُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ (١٠٧)
Should Allah visit you with some distress, there is no one to remove it except Him; and should He desire any good for you, none can stand in the way of His grace: He grants it to whomever He wishes of His servants, and He is the All-forgiving, the All-merciful. (107)

Commentary

Decisiveness Against the Polytheists

These verses and the few verses that follow, all of which speak regarding the issue of Monotheism (Tawhid), the struggle against polytheism (Shirk), and the invitation toward the Truth, are the final verses of this Surah. In reality, they serve as an index or a summary of the Monotheistic discussions of this Surah and an emphasis on the struggle against idol-worship, which has been repeatedly mentioned throughout this Surah.

The tone of the verses indicates that the polytheists were sometimes under the illusion that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله might show some flexibility or leniency in his belief regarding the idols, or grant them some form of acceptance, acknowledging them in some way alongside the belief in God.

The Quran, with the utmost decisiveness, puts an end to this baseless illusion and sets their minds at ease forever: that no compromise or leniency regarding idols has any meaning, and there is no deity except "Allah"—only "Allah," not a word more and not a word less.

First, He commands the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله to address all people: "Say: O people! If you are in doubt regarding my religion, then know that I do not worship those whom you worship besides Allah" (قُلْ يا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ فِي شَكٍّ مِنْ دِينِي فَلا أَعْبُدُ الَّذِينَ تَعْبُدُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ‌).

He does not suffice only with negating their deities; rather, for further emphasis, He affirms all worship for God, saying: "But I worship the God who causes you to die" (وَ لكِنْ أَعْبُدُ اللَّهَ الَّذِي يَتَوَفَّاكُمْ‌).

And again, for even greater emphasis, He says: This is not merely my personal desire, but rather "this is a command given to me, that I be among the believers in Allah" (وَ أُمِرْتُ أَنْ أَكُونَ مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ‌).

The reason why, among all the attributes of God, the focus here is placed specifically on the issue of taking the soul and causing death, is either because whatever a human may doubt, he cannot doubt death; or it is to draw their attention to the issue of punishment and the annihilating torments mentioned in the previous verses, implicitly threatening them with Divine wrath and anger.

After decisively stating His belief regarding the negation of polytheism and idol-worship, He proceeds to explain the reasoning for it—a reason derived from primordial nature (Fitrah) and a reason derived from intellect and wisdom. "Say: I have been commanded to set my face toward the upright religion, which is pure and sincere in every respect" (وَ أَنْ أَقِمْ وَجْهَكَ لِلدِّينِ حَنِيفاً).

Here too, He does not suffice with the affirmative aspect alone; rather, for emphasis, He negates the opposite side, saying: "And never, and most certainly, be not of the polytheists!" (وَ لا تَكُونَنَّ مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ‌).

"Hanif," as we have mentioned previously, refers to one who turns away from "deviation" toward "truth and uprightness," or in other words, one who turns a blind eye to deviated creeds and methods and turns toward the straight religion of God—the very religion that accords with the Fitrah, and is straight because of this harmony with the pure and upright Fitrah.

Therefore, a kind of allusion to the innate nature of Monotheism is hidden within it, for deviation is something that goes against the Fitrah (reflect deeply).

After pointing to the falsehood of polytheism through the path of Fitrah, He points to a clear intellectual reason, stating that the command has been given: "Do not worship, besides Allah, things that neither benefit you nor harm you; for if you do so, you will then be among the wrongdoers"—you will have wronged both yourself and the society to which you belong (وَ لا تَدْعُ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ ما لا يَنْفَعُكَ وَ لا يَضُرُّكَ فَإِنْ فَعَلْتَ فَإِنَّكَ إِذاً مِنَ الظَّالِمِينَ‌).

What intellect or wisdom allows a human to engage in the worship of objects and beings that possess no benefit or harm, and cannot have the slightest impact on human destiny?

Here as well, He does not suffice with the negative aspect alone; in addition to the negation, He emphasizes the affirmative aspect, saying: "If a hardship or harm reaches you from God (whether for punishment or for trial), none can remove it except Him" (وَ إِنْ يَمْسَسْكَ اللَّهُ بِضُرٍّ فَلا كاشِفَ لَهُ إِلَّا هُوَ). Likewise, "If God intends for good and blessing to reach you, no one has the power to repel His grace and mercy" (وَ إِنْ يُرِدْكَ بِخَيْرٍ فَلا رَادَّ لِفَضْلِهِ‌).

"He causes it to reach whomever He wills (and deems worthy) among His servants" (يُصِيبُ بِهِ مَنْ يَشاءُ مِنْ عِبادِهِ‌).

For His forgiveness and mercy encompass all: "And He is the Forgiving, the Merciful" (وهُوَ الْغَفُورُ الرَّحِيمُ‌).

Ayahs

قُلْ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ قَدْ جَآءَكُمُ ٱلْحَقُّ مِن رَّبِّكُمْ ۖ فَمَنِ ٱهْتَدَىٰ فَإِنَّمَا يَهْتَدِى لِنَفْسِهِۦ ۖ وَمَن ضَلَّ فَإِنَّمَا يَضِلُّ عَلَيْهَا ۖ وَمَآ أَنَا۠ عَلَيْكُم بِوَكِيلٍ (١٠٨)
Say, ‘O mankind! The truth has already come to you from your Lord. Whoever is guided, is guided only for [the good of] his own soul, and whoever goes astray, goes astray only to its detriment, and it is not my business to watch over you.’ (108)
وَٱتَّبِعْ مَا يُوحَىٰٓ إِلَيْكَ وَٱصْبِرْ حَتَّىٰ يَحْكُمَ ٱللَّهُ ۚ وَهُوَ خَيْرُ ٱلْحَـٰكِمِينَ (١٠٩)
And follow that which is revealed to you, and be patient until Allah issues [His] judgement, and He is the best of judges. (109)

Commentary

The Final Word

These two verses—one being an admonition to the general public and the other specifically to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله)—complete the instructions that Allah has expressed in these two areas throughout this Surah, and with them, Surah Yunus concludes.

First, as a general command, He says: "Tell all people: Truth has come to you from your Lord." These teachings, this Heavenly Book, this program, and this Prophet are all Truth, and the signs of their truthfulness are manifest (قُلْ يا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ قَدْ جاءَكُمُ الْحَقُّ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ‌).

In light of this reality, "whoever is guided in the light of this Truth is guided for his own benefit, and whoever chooses the path of misguidance by refusing to submit to it, has stepped toward his own loss" (فَمَنِ اهْتَدى‌ فَإِنَّما يَهْتَدِي لِنَفْسِهِ وَ مَنْ ضَلَّ فَإِنَّما يَضِلُّ عَلَيْها).

"And I am not a trustee, advocate, or guardian over you" (وَ ما أَنَا عَلَيْكُمْ بِوَكِيلٍ‌).

This means: I am neither duty-bound to force you to accept the Truth—since compulsion in accepting faith is meaningless—nor can I protect you from Divine punishment if you do not accept it. Rather, my duty is invitation, propagation, exhortation, guidance, and leadership; the rest is up to you, to choose your path by your own free will.

In addition to emphasizing the issue of free will and freedom of choice once again, this verse is proof that accepting the Truth is primarily to the benefit of the human being himself, just as opposing it is to his own detriment. In fact, the teachings of Divine leaders and Heavenly Books are classrooms for the education and perfection of human beings; neither does agreement with them add anything to the greatness of Allah, nor does opposition to them diminish His majesty!

Then, He defines the duty of the Prophet in two sentences. First: "You must follow only that which is revealed to you" (وَ اتَّبِعْ ما يُوحى‌ إِلَيْكَ‌). Allah has determined your path through revelation, and the slightest deviation from it is not permissible for you.

Second, on this path, there are exhausting difficulties and many hardships ahead of you; you must not allow fear or dread of the multitude of problems to enter your heart. "You must practice patience, perseverance, and steadfastness until Allah issues His decree and command for your victory over the enemies" (وَ اصْبِرْ حَتَّى يَحْكُمَ اللَّهُ‌), "for He is the best of judges; His command is Truth, His decree is justice, and His promise is unfailing" (وَ هُوَ خَيْرُ الْحاكِمِينَ‌).

O Lord! You have promised victory to Your servants—to those who strive in Your way with a struggle coupled with sincerity and faith, and to those who exhibit patience, perseverance, and steadfastness in Your path.

O Allah! In these moments and on the threshold of establishing an Islamic government, a multitude of problems surrounds us, and by Your success and grace, we shall not cease from striving and steadfastness.

O God! By Your grace, dispel the dark clouds of difficulties and favor us with the life-giving rays of Truth and justice. Amen, O Lord of the Worlds.

18 Rajab 1399 - June 13, 1979.

(End of Volume Eight)
